# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) in a thermocouple instrument. Why is it necessary, and what reference temperature is conventionally used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** CJC is the technique of measuring the temperature $T_{\text{t}}$ of the thermocouple terminal block (the *cold junction*) and electronically adding the equivalent Seebeck voltage $V_{\text{CJC}} = S(T_{\text{ref}}) \cdot (T_{\text{t}} - T_{\text{ref}})$ so that the instrument reading corresponds to the hot-junction temperature alone.
2. **Necessity (1 mark):** The thermocouple output is proportional to the *difference* between hot-junction and cold-junction temperatures. Without CJC, every variation in ambient temperature at the terminal block would appear as a measurement error of the hot junction.
3. **Conventional reference (1 mark):** $T_{\text{ref}} = 0^\circ\text{C}$ (the ice-point), per the ITS-90 international temperature scale.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three hardware techniques used to reduce errors due to Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) in a remotely mounted sensor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Shielded twisted-pair cabling** — the shield drains capacitive-coupled currents to ground; twisting cancels magnetic loop pickup. **(1 mark)**
2. **Galvanic isolation (optocoupler or isolation amplifier)** — breaks the conductive common-mode path between field and receiver. **(1 mark)**
3. **Common-mode chokes and ferrite beads** — present a high impedance to common-mode RF currents without attenuating the differential signal. **(1 mark)**

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** A precision pressure transducer with a full-scale output of 10 V has the following error contributions at the operating point:
- Sensor intrinsic non-linearity: $\pm 0.15\%$ FS
- Temperature drift: $\pm 0.08\%$ FS per $10^\circ\text{C}$ deviation from $25^\circ\text{C}$
- Channel-induced EMI: $\pm 0.05\%$ FS

The unit operates at $45^\circ\text{C}$ ambient.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compute the total expected error using the Root-Sum-Square (RSS) method and the arithmetic-sum (worst-case) method. Compare and justify which is more realistic.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Propose a Tier-1 and a Tier-2 compensation scheme to reduce the temperature-induced error by at least 5×. Show the quantitative impact on the total RSS error.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Compute temperature contribution:

$$
e_{\text{temp}} = \frac{0.08\%}{10^\circ\text{C}} \times (45 - 25)^\circ\text{C} = 0.16\% \text{ FS}
$$

Step 2 — Other contributions:
- $e_{\text{sensor}} = 0.15\%$ FS
- $e_{\text{channel}} = 0.05\%$ FS

Step 3 — Arithmetic (worst-case) sum:

$$
e_{\text{arith}} = 0.15 + 0.16 + 0.05 = 0.36\% \text{ FS}
$$

Step 4 — RSS calculation:

$$
e_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{(0.15)^2 + (0.16)^2 + (0.05)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0256 + 0.0025}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{0.0506} \approx 0.225\% \text{ FS}
$$

Step 5 — Justification (1 mark):
The RSS value of **0.225%** is more physically realistic because the three error sources are statistically independent. The arithmetic sum of **0.36%** is a pessimistic bound used only for *safety-critical* worst-case design.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Tier-1 scheme (3 marks):
Insert a **Wheatstone bridge nulling** arrangement using a dummy transducer exposed to the *same* temperature but vented to a sealed reference pressure. The dummy arm generates an equal-and-opposite temperature drift, which cancels in the differential measurement.

Step 2 — Tier-2 scheme (3 marks):
Add an **on-board NTC thermistor** feeding a microcontroller lookup table that subtracts a pre-calibrated polynomial:

$$
V_{\text{corrected}}(T) = V_{\text{raw}} \cdot \left[1 + \alpha (T - 25^\circ\text{C})\right]
$$

with $\alpha = -0.008\%/^\circ\text{C}$.

Step 3 — Quantitative impact (1 mark):
A 5× reduction takes the temperature error from **0.16% to 0.032%** FS. New RSS:

$$
e_{\text{RSS,new}} = \sqrt{(0.15)^2 + (0.032)^2 + (0.05)^2} \approx \sqrt{0.0257} \approx 0.160\% \text{ FS}
$$

A **30% overall improvement** in total system error.

---

### Question B — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** A wireless sensor node transmits at **433 MHz** with $P_{\text{tx}} = +10$ dBm, using isotropic antennas ($G_{\text{tx}} = G_{\text{rx}} = 0$ dBi). The receiver sensitivity is **−95 dBm**.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compute the maximum line-of-sight range $d_{\max}$ assuming free-space propagation only.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*If the same link must operate over a non-line-of-sight industrial floor with an additional 25 dB of obstruction loss, propose two architectural changes to restore the original range. Quantify the improvement of each.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Maximum allowable path loss:

$$
L_{\text{fs,max}} = P_{\text{tx}} - P_{\text{rx,sens}} = 10 - (-95) = 105 \text{ dB}
$$

Step 2 — Wavelength at 433 MHz:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{433 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \approx 0.693 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3 — Invert the free-space path-loss formula:

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot 10^{\,L_{\text{fs,max}}/20}
$$

Step 4 — Substitute:

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{0.693}{4\pi} \cdot 10^{105/20} = \frac{0.693}{12.566} \cdot 10^{5.25}
$$

$$
= 0.05515 \times 177{,}828 \approx 9807 \text{ m} \approx 9.8 \text{ km}
$$

**Answer: $d_{\max} \approx 9.8$ km.**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Recompute with 25 dB obstruction loss:
Effective maximum path loss drops to $105 - 25 = 80$ dB.

Step 2 — New range (without architectural change):

$$
d_{\max}' = \frac{0.693}{4\pi} \cdot 10^{80/20} = 0.05515 \times 10^{4} \approx 551.5 \text{ m}
$$

Range reduced by **17.8×**.

Step 3 — **Change 1: Antenna gain upgrade (3 marks).**
Replace isotropic antennas with patch/Yagi antennas of $G_{\text{tx}} = G_{\text{rx}} = +8$ dBi.

Net gain $= +8 + 8 = +16$ dB.
New range:

$$
d_{\max}'' = 0.05515 \times 10^{(80 + 16)/20} = 0.05515 \times 10^{4.8} \approx 4366 \text{ m}
$$

Step 4 — **Change 2: Forward Error Correction coding (3 marks).**
Adopt Hamming (7,4) FEC, providing ~3 dB of coding gain at the operating BER.

$$
d_{\max}''' = 0.05515 \times 10^{(80 + 16 + 3)/20} \approx 5513 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5 — Comparison (1 mark):
Combined changes restore link range from **551 m** to **5.5 km**, comfortably exceeding the original 9.8 km-free-space case when allowance is made for additional obstructions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion.** Many students plug $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ in km/s — always declare your units explicitly in the first line.
> 2. **Mixing RSS and arithmetic sums.** Do *not* add errors arithmetically and then claim the result is RSS; KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Confusing $S(T)$ with a constant.** In CJC derivations, the Seebeck coefficient is temperature-dependent — using a single average value when the question states "non-linear thermocouple" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the antenna gain terms.** A wireless link-budget answer without Friis gains is incomplete and loses 3 marks.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without labels.** Mermaid or hand-drawn block diagrams must have *every* block labelled; an unlabelled box is treated as "no contribution to the answer".

## Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Environmental errors** are *additive* to sensor output; the Big Five stressors are **temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration, EMI/RFI**.
- **Three compensation tiers:** Passive (bridge nulling, shielding), Active (CJC, chopper amps), Algorithmic (polynomial correction, Kalman filtering).
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is *mandatory* for every thermocouple instrument; reference temperature is **$T_{\text{ref}} = 0^\circ\text{C}$**.
- **RSS error combination** is correct for *independent random* errors; arithmetic sum is reserved for safety-critical worst-case design.
- **Remote communication errors** arise in the *channel* and require *channel-level* mitigation: shielding, twisting, differential signalling, isolation, FEC, ARQ.
- **Current loops (4–20 mA)** are immune to cable resistance drops because the *current*, not voltage, carries the information.
- **Friis equation** governs wireless link budgets: **+6 dB per doubling of distance or frequency**.
- **FEC coding** provides square-law improvement in BER — a powerful tool in industrial wireless telemetry.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** of a good instrumentation amplifier exceeds **100 dB** at DC.
- **Standard Reference Conditions (SRC):** $25^\circ\text{C}$, $45\%$ RH, $101.325$ kPa — always quote datasheet accuracy against SRC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Techniques for Direct Interfacing of Resistive Sensors with Embedded Controller: Embedded Controller Based Excitation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Advanced Techniques for Direct Interfacing of Resistive Sensors with Embedded Controller: Embedded Controller Based Excitation System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Embedded Controller Based Excitation System (ECES)** is defined as a digitally controlled, closed-loop signal conditioning architecture in which an embedded microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, MSP430, PIC, AVR, ESP32) generates, regulates, and modulates the **excitation signal** (voltage or current) supplied to a **resistive sensor element**, while simultaneously performing **ratiometric analog-to-digital conversion**, **linearization**, and **lead-resistance compensation** in firmware. The resistive sensor family primarily includes: - **Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)** — e.g., PT100 / PT1000 (platinum), conforming to **IEC 60751**. - **Thermistors** — NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) and PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) types. - **Strain Gauges** — foil or semiconductor type with **Gauge Factor (GF)** typically **2.0 for metallic** and **100–200 for semiconductor** gauges. - **Light Dependent Resistors (LDR)**, **Magneto-resistors**, and **Humidity-sensitive resistors**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Scale"** > Imagine weighing flour with a kitchen scale. If the spring inside is stiff, your reading drifts and you need constant re-calibration. A *smart scale* uses a tiny computer (microcontroller) that gently pulses a known *test weight* (excitation signal), checks the spring's response, recalibrates itself, and only then measures your flour. It also compensates for the *weight of your hands* on the tray (lead resistance) automatically. **In the same way, the ECES:** - Generates a **precise, stable excitation** (test weight) via DAC or PWM. - Uses **ratiometric ADC** so any drift in excitation cancels out. - Runs **software linearization** (Callendar-Van Dusen, Steinhart-Hart) to convert non-linear resistance back to the physical quantity. - Switches excitation polarity (AC excitation) to eliminate **thermocouple/EMF errors** and detect ground loops. ### 1.3 Why "Advanced Techniques" Are Required | Limitation of Basic Voltage Divider | Why ECES Solves It | |---|---| | Self-heating error from continuous DC | Pulsed / chopped excitation reduces average power dissipation | | Lead wire resistance corrupts RTD reading | 3-wire / 4-wire Kelvin switching via analog MUX | | Sensor non-linearity | Firmware linearization using polynomial or LUT | | Source voltage drift affects accuracy | Ratiometric ADC: same reference used for excitation | | Single sensor per ADC channel | Multiplexed excitation for sensor arrays | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The embedded-controller-based excitation system is treated as the **bridge between passive analog signal conditioning** and **digital sensor fusion**, and is a high-weightage topic for both Module 2 descriptive questions and lab/design viva. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Layered Architecture The ECES operates in **four tightly coupled layers**, each managed by the embedded controller: 1. **Excitation Generation Layer** - Produces a stable **DC voltage**, **DC current**, **PWM-derived current**, or **AC-bipolar excitation**. - Typical building blocks: 16-bit DAC, PWM + op-amp current pump, or matched precision reference IC (e.g., REF5025, ADR02). 2. **Sensor Interface Layer** - Connects to resistive sensor via 2-wire, 3-wire, or 4-wire (Kelvin) topology. - Includes analog MUX (e.g., CD4051, ADG708) for multi-sensor scanning. 3. **Signal Conditioning Layer** - Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD8421) for low-level signals. - Anti-aliasing filter (active, Sallen-Key topology). 4. **Digital Acquisition & Compensation Layer** - 16/24-bit **Sigma-Delta ADC** (e.g., ADS1115, ADS1256) for high resolution. - Firmware routines for linearization, lead compensation, and self-calibration. ### 2.2 The Core "Why" — Ratiometric Measurement In **ratiometric measurement**, the *same reference voltage* is used to: - Excite the sensor, AND - Serve as the **ADC's full-scale reference ($V_{REF}$)**. Therefore, any drift, noise, or temperature variation in the excitation source cancels out mathematically. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** — All symbols, units, and boundary conditions required for Module 2 numerical/ESE questions are tabulated below. The vertical bar `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to protect markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Values | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_{T} = R_{0}\bigl[1 + AT + BT^{2} - 100\,CT^{3} + CT^{4}\bigr]$ (for $T<0^{\circ}C$); $R_T = R_0[1+AT+BT^2]$ (for $T\geq0^{\circ}C$) | Callendar-Van Dusen (PT100) | $A = 3.9083\times10^{-3}$, $B=-5.775\times10^{-7}$, $C=-4.183\times10^{-12}$ | | 2 | $R_{T} = R_{0}\,\exp\!\Bigl[B\bigl(\tfrac{1}{T}-\tfrac{1}{T_{0}}\bigr)\Bigr]$ | NTC Thermistor (B-parameter) | $B \approx 3000\text{–}5000\,\mathrm{K}$ | | 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain Gauge Equation | $GF_{metal} \approx 2.0$, $GF_{semi} \approx 100\text{–}200$ | | 4 | $V_{out} = V_{EXC}\cdot\dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor}+R_{ref}}$ | Voltage Divider Output | $V_{EXC}$ typically $2.5\,\mathrm{V}$ or $5\,\mathrm{V}$ | | 5 | $V_{out} = I_{EXC}\cdot R_{sensor}$ | Current-Excitation Output | $I_{EXC}$ typically $1\,\mathrm{mA}$ for PT100 | | 6 | $Code_{ADC} = \dfrac{V_{sensor}}{V_{REF}}\cdot(2^{n}-1)$ | ADC Output Code (ratiometric) | $n =$ ADC bits (16/24) | | 7 | $V_{sensor}/V_{REF} = R_{sensor}/(R_{sensor}+R_{ref})$ | Ratiometric cancellation | Independent of $V_{EXC}$ drift | | 8 | $I_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{REF}\cdot D}{R_{sense}}$ | PWM-derived Current Source | $D =$ PWM duty cycle $(0\le D\le 1)$ | | 9 | $\Delta T_{self} = \dfrac{I_{EXC}^{2}\cdot R_{sensor}}{\delta}$ | Self-Heating Error | $\delta =$ thermal dissipation coeff. $(\mathrm{mW/^{\circ}C})$ | | 10 | $R_{T,meas} = R_{lead1}+R_{RTD}+R_{lead2}$ | 2-wire Error Model | Kelvin correction needed | | 11 | $V_{A}-V_{B} = I_{EXC}\cdot (R_{lead1}-R_{lead2})$ | 3-wire Lead Cancellation | Matched lead pairs | | 12 | $R_{RTD} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{force}}$ | 4-wire Kelvin (true resistance) | Lead resistance eliminated | ### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility ECES architectures are deployed in: - **Industrial process control** (PT100 monitoring of chemical reactors). - **Automotive ECUs** for exhaust-gas temperature sensing. - **Aerospace flight control** (strain-gauge-based load sensing). - **Medical instrumentation** (thermistor-based patient thermometry, ECG/EEG front ends). - **Smart agriculture & IoT** (soil moisture, LDR light, RTD ambient temperature). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ratiometric ADC Code for Voltage Divider **Problem Statement:** A PT100 sensor $R_S$ is placed in series with precision reference resistor $R_{ref}=1\,\mathrm{k\Omega}$. The voltage across $R_S$ is digitized by a 12-bit ADC using $V_{REF}=V_{EXC}$. Derive an expression for the ADC code that **eliminates** $V_{EXC}$ drift. **Step 1 — Write the voltage divider equation.** $$ V_{sensor} \;=\; V_{EXC}\cdot\dfrac{R_{S}}{R_{S}+R_{ref}} $$ **Step 2 — Write the ratiometric ADC code equation.** $$ N_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{sensor}}{V_{REF}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) $$ **Step 3 — Apply ratiometric condition $V_{REF}=V_{EXC}$.** $$ N_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{EXC}\cdot \dfrac{R_{S}}{R_{S}+R_{ref}}}{V_{EXC}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $V_{EXC}$ from numerator and denominator.** $$ N_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{S}}{R_{S}+R_{ref}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) $$ **Conclusion (engineering insight):** The digital code now depends **only on the resistor ratio**, which is determined by the **physical fabrication tolerances** of $R_S$ and $R_{ref}$, NOT on the magnitude or stability of $V_{EXC}$. This is the mathematical foundation of every ratiometric sensor interface. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Self-Heating Error Bound for PT100 **Problem Statement:** For PT100 with $\delta = 5\,\mathrm{mW/^{\circ}C}$ (still air) and excitation current $I_{EXC}=1\,\mathrm{mA}$ at $0^{\circ}C$, find the self-heating temperature error. **Step 1 — Resistance of PT100 at 0 °C.** $$ R_{0} \;=\; 100\,\Omega $$ **Step 2 — Power dissipated in the sensor element.** $$ P \;=\; I_{EXC}^{2}\cdot R_{0} $$ $$ P \;=\; (1\times 10^{-3})^{2}\cdot 100 \;=\; 1\times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{W} \;=\; 100\,\mu\mathrm{W} $$ **Step 3 — Temperature rise from self-heating.** $$ \Delta T_{self} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\delta} \;=\; \dfrac{100\,\mu\mathrm{W}}{5\,\mathrm{mW/^{\circ}C}} \;=\; 0.02\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ **Conclusion:** For PT100, a 1 mA excitation keeps self-heating well within industrial accuracy ($\pm 0.05\,^{\circ}C$). However, increasing to $I_{EXC}=5\,\mathrm{mA}$ produces $P=2.5\,\mathrm{mW}$ and $\Delta T_{self}=0.5\,^{\circ}C$ — **unacceptable** for precision applications. ECES mitigates this via **pulsed excitation** where the duty cycle is reduced to e.g., 25%, giving an effective $\langle I_{EXC}\rangle = 0.5\,\mathrm{mA}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — 3-Wire RTD Lead-Compensation **Problem Statement:** For a 3-wire RTD with equal lead resistances $R_{lead1}=R_{lead2}=R_L$, show that the lead-resistance error cancels when measured in **ratiometric fashion**. **Step 1 — Circuit equation at Node A (top of RTD):** $$ V_{A} \;=\; I_{EXC}\cdot(R_{lead1}+R_{RTD}) $$ **Step 2 — Circuit equation at Node B (bottom, low-side of lead 2):** $$ V_{B} \;=\; I_{EXC}\cdot R_{lead2} $$ **Step 3 — Differential measurement:** $$ V_{A}-V_{B} \;=\; I_{EXC}\cdot(R_{lead1}+R_{RTD}-R_{lead2}) $$ **Step 4 — Apply matched-lead condition $R_{lead1}=R_{lead2}=R_L$:** $$ V_{A}-V_{B} \;=\; I_{EXC}\cdot R_{RTD} $$ **Conclusion:** The matched 3-wire topology removes the **common-mode lead drop** and yields the true RTD voltage. ECES uses a **dual-input ADC** (or an instrumentation amplifier) to perform this differential measurement automatically. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — PWM-Derived Current Source with Op-Amp **Step 1 — A low-pass filter converts PWM to DC.** $$ V_{DC,out} \;=\; V_{REF}\cdot D $$ **Step 2 — Op-amp + pass-transistor + sense resistor form a Howland / buffered current pump:** $$ I_{load} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DC,out}}{R_{sense}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{REF}\cdot D}{R_{sense}} $$ **Step 3 — The current $I_{load}$ is forced through the sensor $R_{sensor}$, producing:** $$ V_{sensor} \;=\; I_{load}\cdot R_{sensor} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute.** $$ V_{sensor} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{REF}\cdot D\cdot R_{sensor}}{R_{sense}} $$ **Engineering insight:** By digitally tuning the **PWM duty cycle $D$** in firmware, the controller can dynamically adjust excitation current (e.g., reduce during self-heating-sensitive phases, increase for fast transient response). This is a hallmark of the ECES approach. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | DC Voltage Excitation | DC Current Excitation | PWM Current Excitation | AC-Bipolar Excitation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Linearity | Moderate | High | High | High | | Self-heating | High | Medium | Low (via duty) | Very low | | Lead compensation | Difficult | Easy (3/4-wire) | Easy | Easy | | EMF/thermoelectric errors | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Eliminated** | | Firmware complexity | Low | Low | Medium | High | | Best suited for | LDR, thermistor | PT100, strain gauge | PT1000, bridge | Precision lab, ppm accuracy | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ECES ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU1["MCU
Controller Core"] DAC1["16-bit DAC
or PWM Module"] REF1["Precision Voltage
Reference REF5025"] IEXC["Programmable
Current Source"] MUX1["Analog MUX
CD4051B"] SENS1["PT100 RTD
3-wire or 4-wire"] IAMP1["Instrumentation
Amplifier INA128"] AAF1["Active Low-Pass
Anti-Alias Filter"] ADC1["24-bit Sigma-Delta
ADC ADS1256"] CALC["Firmware
Linearization and
Calibration"] DISP1["LCD / UART /
IoT Telemetry"] REF1 --> DAC1 DAC1 --> IEXC MCU1 --> DAC1 MCU1 --> MUX1 IEXC --> MUX1 MUX1 --> SENS1 SENS1 --> IAMP1 IAMP1 --> AAF1 AAF1 --> ADC1 ADC1 --> CALC CALC --> DISP1 MCU1 --> CALC REF1 --> ADC1 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The MCU programs a precision current source (driven by DAC + reference IC) which is routed through an analog multiplexer to one of several resistive sensors. The differential signal is amplified, anti-aliased, and digitized by a high-resolution sigma-delta ADC. Firmware then performs **linearization (Callendar-Van Dusen / Steinhart-Hart)** and publishes the result over UART/LCD/IoT. --- ### 4.2 Excitation Switching & Ratiometric Sampling Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as Microcontroller participant IEXC as Excitation Source participant SENS as Resistive Sensor participant ADC as Ratiometric ADC MCU->>IEXC: Set I_EXC = 1 mA (PWM D=0.4) IEXC->>SENS: Apply excitation pulse (10 ms) SENS-->>ADC: Sensor voltage V_sensor Note over ADC: V_REF = V_EXC (ratiometric) ADC->>MCU: Convert and return Code N MCU->>MCU: Apply Callendar-Van Dusen
or Steinhart-Hart MCU->>IEXC: Reverse polarity / switch off
(reduce self-heating) MCU->>ADC: Re-sample zero offset
(auto-zeroing) MCU->>MCU: Subtract offset, publish result ``` > **Engineering insight:** The sequence diagram highlights the ECES's *cyclic self-calibration* — excitation is pulsed, not continuous, which simultaneously **reduces self-heating** and allows **chopper-stabilized offset cancellation** between samples. --- ### 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting Excitation Mode ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Select Resistive Sensor"] --> Q1{"Sensor type?"} Q1 -- "PT100 RTD" --> PT["Choose DC Current
1 mA, 3/4-wire"] Q1 -- "Thermistor NTC" --> TC["Choose DC Voltage Divider
10 kohm reference"] Q1 -- "Strain Gauge" --> SG["Choose Wheatstone Bridge
DC Voltage Excitation"] Q1 -- "ppm-precision lab" --> AC["Choose AC Bipolar
Chopper Stabilized"] PT --> CHK{"Self-heating
critical?"} CHK -- "Yes" --> PULSE["Use PWM Pulsed
Duty less than 25%"] CHK -- "No" --> CONT["Use Continuous DC"] TC --> END["Final ECES Configuration"] SG --> END AC --> END PULSE --> END CONT --> END ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1.** Define an **Embedded Controller Based Excitation System (ECES)** for resistive sensors. List **two key advantages** it offers over a purely analog excitation scheme. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > An Embedded Controller Based Excitation System is a digital architecture in which a microcontroller generates, regulates, and modulates the excitation (voltage or current) supplied to a resistive sensor, while simultaneously performing ratiometric ADC sampling, linearization, and lead-resistance compensation in firmware. > > **Advantages over analog excitation:** > (i) **Software-tunable excitation** — current or voltage level can be reprogrammed for different sensors or self-heating budgets. > (ii) **Self-calibration & ratiometric operation** — drift in the excitation source cancels out because the same reference drives both the sensor and the ADC. > (iii) Pulsed/chopped excitation reduces **self-heating errors** and eliminates **thermoelectric EMFs**. *(Any two of the above earn full 3 marks.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2.** What is **ratiometric measurement**? Show mathematically how the ratiometric ADC code for a voltage-divider-connected resistive sensor becomes independent of the excitation voltage. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > In ratiometric measurement, the excitation voltage $V_{EXC}$ and the ADC reference voltage $V_{REF}$ are derived from the **same physical reference**, so any drift affects both signals identically and cancels in the digital domain. > > **Derivation:** > > $$ > V_{sensor} \;=\; V_{EXC}\cdot\dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor}+R_{ref}} > $$ > > $$ > N_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{sensor}}{V_{REF}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{EXC}\cdot R_{sensor}}{(R_{sensor}+R_{ref})\cdot V_{REF}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) > $$ > > With $V_{REF}=V_{EXC}$: > > $$ > N_{ADC} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor}+R_{ref}}\cdot(2^{n}-1) > $$ > > The code is independent of $V_{EXC}$ magnitude. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 2]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** for a PT100 RTD. A PT100 sensor has $R_0=100\,\Omega$ at $0^{\circ}C$. Using the IEC 60751 coefficients, compute the resistance at $T=100^{\circ}C$ and at $T=-50^{\circ}C$. Comment on **linearity**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Embedded Controller Based Excitation System (ECES)** architecture for a multi-channel PT100 data-acquisition module. Explain the role of the **ratiometric ADC**, **analog MUX**, and **firmware linearization** in this system. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution — Question A(a):** The **Callendar-Van Dusen (CVD) equation** is the empirical polynomial that relates a platinum RTD's resistance to temperature: For $T \geq 0^{\circ}C$: $$ R_T \;=\; R_0\bigl(1 + A T + B T^{2}\bigr) $$ For $T < 0^{\circ}C$: $$ R_T \;=\; R_0\bigl[1 + A T + B T^{2} + C(T-100)T^{3}\bigr] $$ With **IEC 60751 coefficients**: $$ A = 3.9083\times 10^{-3}\,^{\circ}C^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775\times 10^{-7}\,^{\circ}C^{-2}, \quad C = -4.183\times 10^{-12}\,^{\circ}C^{-4} $$ **Computation at $T = 100^{\circ}C$:** $$ R_{100} \;=\; 100\cdot\bigl(1 + 3.9083\times10^{-3}\cdot 100 + (-5.775\times10^{-7})\cdot 100^{2}\bigr) $$ $$ R_{100} \;=\; 100\cdot(1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775) \;=\; 100\cdot(1.385055) \;=\; 138.51\,\Omega $$ **Computation at $T = -50^{\circ}C$:** $$ R_{-50} \;=\; 100\cdot\bigl[1 + 3.9083\times10^{-3}\cdot(-50) + (-5.775\times10^{-7})\cdot(-50)^{2} + (-4.183\times10^{-12})\cdot(-150)\cdot(-50)^{3}\bigr] $$ $$ R_{-50} \;=\; 100\cdot\bigl[1 - 0.195415 + 0.0014438 - 7.844\times 10^{-5}\bigr] \;=\; 100\cdot(0.805951) \;=\; 80.60\,\Omega $$ **Comment on linearity:** Between $0^{\circ}C$ and $100^{\circ}C$, the quadratic term $BT^2$ introduces a small downward curvature of about $-0.5\%$ full-scale. ECES compensates by storing a **look-up table** or by directly evaluating the CVD polynomial in firmware, restoring linearity to better than $\pm 0.05^{\circ}C$. --- **Model Solution — Question A(b):** (Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.1** above.) **Architecture description:** - **MCU:** Hosts firmware, generates excitation control words, performs linearization. - **DAC / PWM-driven current source:** Produces a stable **1 mA** current excitation. A precision **2.5 V reference IC (REF5025)** ensures long-term stability. - **Analog MUX (CD4051B):** Routes excitation to one of up to **8 PT100 sensors** sequentially. - **3-wire PT100 + INA128 instrumentation amplifier:** Provides lead-resistance-cancelled differential signal with CMRR > 100 dB. - **Anti-aliasing filter:** 2nd-order Sallen-Key, $f_c=10\,\mathrm{Hz}$ suitable for thermal time constants. - **24-bit Σ-Δ ADC (ADS1256):** Uses the **same 2.5 V reference** as the current source → **ratiometric conversion**. - **Firmware linearization:** Applies the CVD polynomial (or a 64-entry LUT) to convert raw counts directly into temperature in °C. **Roles explained:** - **Ratiometric ADC:** Cancels reference drift, improving accuracy by an order of magnitude. - **Analog MUX:** Enables multi-channel scanning with a single excitation chain, reducing cost. - **Firmware linearization:** Removes sensor non-linearity without precision analog multipliers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Derive the expression for **self-heating temperature error** in a PT100 RTD. For a sensor with dissipation constant $\delta = 6.5\,\mathrm{mW/^{\circ}C}$ and excitation current $I_{EXC}=2\,\mathrm{mA}$, calculate the temperature rise. Suggest **two firmware-level mitigation techniques** in an ECES. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the operation of a **3-wire RTD excitation system**. Show mathematically how the matched-lead technique cancels the lead-resistance error. Mention the **role of the analog switch / MUX** in switching between 2-wire and 3-wire modes. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution — Question B(a):** **Derivation:** Power dissipated in the sensor element: $$ P \;=\; I_{EXC}^{2}\cdot R_{sensor} $$ For PT100 at $0^{\circ}C$, $R = 100\,\Omega$: $$ P \;=\; (2\times 10^{-3})^{2}\cdot 100 \;=\; 4\times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{W} \;=\; 400\,\mu\mathrm{W} $$ Temperature rise: $$ \Delta T_{self} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\delta} \;=\; \dfrac{400\,\mu\mathrm{W}}{6.5\,\mathrm{mW/^{\circ}C}} \;=\; 0.0615\,^{\circ}C \;\approx\; 0.062\,^{\circ}C $$ **Firmware-level mitigation in ECES:** 1. **Pulsed excitation:** Drive the current source with a PWM duty cycle of e.g., 25%, so the **time-averaged power** drops to 1/16, reducing $\Delta T_{self}$ to $0.004^{\circ}C$. 2. **Adaptive excitation control:** Use the MCU's DAC/PWM to lower $I_{EXC}$ during high-resolution slow measurements and raise it only during fast transients — balancing **self-heating vs. SNR**. 3. **Auto-zero offsetting:** Take a "zero-current" baseline between samples to subtract any residual self-heating drift. --- **Model Solution — Question B(b):** **3-Wire RTD Operation:** A 3-wire RTD uses **two identical lead wires** on one side of the element and **one lead** on the other. The ECES typically forces **one matched excitation current** through each of the two top leads and measures differentially. **Mathematical cancellation:** Voltage at top of RTD (Node A): $$ V_{A} \;=\; I_{1}\cdot R_{lead1} + I_{EXC}\cdot R_{RTD} $$ Voltage at the junction of lead 2 (Node B): $$ V_{B} \;=\; I_{2}\cdot R_{lead2} $$ If $I_1=I_2=I_{EXC}$ and $R_{lead1}=R_{lead2}=R_L$: $$ V_{A}-V_{B} \;=\; I_{EXC}(R_{L}+R_{RTD}) - I_{EXC}\cdot R_{L} \;=\; I_{EXC}\cdot R_{RTD} $$ **Lead resistance fully cancels.** **Role of analog MUX/switch:** The MUX (e.g., ADG708) is configured by firmware to: - Route excitation current through **lead 1 only** for 2-wire mode. - Route through **both leads 1 and 3** simultaneously for 3-wire differential mode. - Reconfigure dynamically for **4-wire Kelvin** mode by isolating the sense leads. This programmability is what makes the ECES a **universal PT100 interface**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert** > - Students commonly **omit units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "138.51" instead of "$138.51\,\Omega$"). KTU deducts **½ mark per missing unit**. > - In ratiometric derivations, **failing to state the condition $V_{REF}=V_{EXC}$ explicitly** before cancellation costs the "why" marks. > - For CVD equation, **mixing the $T<0^{\circ}C$ and $T\geq0^{\circ}C$ forms** is a frequent mistake — write both forms and clearly tag which temperature range applies. > - In 3-wire derivation, **failing to invoke the matched-lead assumption $R_{lead1}=R_{lead2}$** is treated as an incomplete answer. > - Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for ECES architecture questions — missing arrows / unlabelled blocks can cost 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — ECES for Resistive Sensors** - ✅ **ECES** = MCU-driven, software-controlled excitation + ratiometric ADC + firmware linearization. - ✅ **Resistive sensor family:** RTD (PT100/PT1000), NTC/PTC thermistor, strain gauge, LDR. - ✅ **Callendar-Van Dusen equation:** quadratic (above 0 °C), quartic (below 0 °C). - ✅ **Steinhart-Hart equation:** $1/T = a + b\ln R + c(\ln R)^3$ for thermistors. - ✅ **Ratiometric principle:** $V_{REF} = V_{EXC}$ → excitation drift cancels in the digital code. - ✅ **Current excitation advantages:** linear output, easy 3/4-wire lead compensation. - ✅ **Voltage divider:** simple but introduces $V_{EXC}$ sensitivity (mitigated by ratiometric ADC). - ✅ **Self-heating:** $\Delta T_{self} = I^2 R/\delta$; mitigated by pulsed/PWM excitation. - ✅ **3-wire RTD:** matched lead cancellation using differential ADC / INA. - ✅ **4-wire Kelvin:** ultimate accuracy; uses separate force and sense paths. - ✅ **PWM current source:** $I_{OUT} = V_{REF}\cdot D/R_{sense}$ — firmware-tunable. - ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter:** Sallen-Key, fc well below half the ADC sampling rate. - ✅ **24-bit Σ-Δ ADC** preferred for sub-°C PT100 measurements (e.g., ADS1256). - ✅ **AC-bipolar (chopper) excitation** eliminates thermoelectric EMFs and 1/f noise. - ✅ **ECES advantages:** programmable, self-calibrating, multiplexed, IoT-ready. - ✅ **Common ICs:** REF5025 (reference), INA128 (INA), ADS1256 (ADC), ADG708 (MUX). ---

topics.title: Direct Interfacing Schemes of Various Resistive Sensor Topologies (Single, Differential and Bridge Type) to Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Interfacing Schemes of Various Resistive Sensor Topologies (Single, Differential and Bridge Type) to Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Resistive Sensor** is a transducer whose electrical resistance changes as a deterministic, monotonic function of a measurand (temperature, strain, light intensity, humidity, displacement, force, etc.). Examples include **RTDs, thermistors, strain gauges, photoresistors (LDRs), and conductive-polymer humidity sensors**.

A **Direct Interfacing Scheme** is a signal-conditioning topology that converts the variable resistance of a sensor into a **voltage signal compatible with the analog input range of a microcontroller's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, without intermediate frequency conversion or digital modulation. The three canonical topologies recognized in the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

| Topology | Sensors Used | Typical Application |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Single resistive sensor** | 1 sensor + 1 reference resistor | RTD temperature sensing, thermistor alarm |
| **Differential (half-bridge)** | 2 active sensors + 2 references | Dual-element temperature compensation, push-pull strain |
| **Bridge (Wheatstone / full-bridge)** | 4 active sensors or 2 sensors + 2 references | Strain gauge load cells, pressure transducers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The phrase "Direct Interfacing" specifically excludes AC-excited bridges, chopper-stabilized amplifiers, or sigma-delta modulation. Only **DC-excited voltage-divider/Wheatstone configurations feeding the MCU ADC (or a single in-amp stage before the ADC)** are in scope.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bathroom Scale"**
> Imagine a bathroom scale. When you stand on it, the **internal metal spring stretches** (its "resistance to current" changes, just like a strain gauge). The scale has only **two wires coming out** — these are your sensor terminals. But a microcontroller cannot read a stretching spring directly. It only understands **voltage between 0 V and 3.3 V**. So we connect the sensor inside a circuit that converts the "stretchiness" into a **voltage number** (e.g., 1.234 V = 47.3 kg). That conversion circuit is the **interfacing scheme**. A single resistor scheme is like a **one-spring scale**, a differential scheme uses **two springs pushing against each other** (cancels common errors like temperature drift), and a full-bridge uses **four springs** (maximum sensitivity and rejection of common-mode noise — this is what precision load cells use).

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters

- **Universal applicability**: More than 60% of all physical sensors in industrial automation are resistive.
- **Direct ADC interfacing** eliminates expensive dedicated ASICs (e.g., 24-bit sigma-delta) for many applications.
- **Bridge topology** is the de-facto standard in weigh scales, automotive strain sensing, and biomedical pressure monitoring.
- **Microcontroller compatibility**: Modern MCUs (STM32, ESP32, PIC, ATmega) have 12-bit/16-bit ADCs that demand the sensor output be conditioned to fit within the **ADC reference window (typically $V_{REF+}$ to $V_{REF-}$)**.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **ADC Resolution (LSB)**: $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$, where $n$ is the number of bits (**$n=12$** for typical KTU-spec MCUs).
- **Sensor Sensitivity**: $S = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta x}$ (strain gauges: gauge factor $\approx 2$).
- **Bridge Sensitivity**: $V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ (small-signal linearization).
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**: for differential stages, **> 80 dB** at DC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Resistive Sensor as a Two-Terminal Variable Resistor

A resistive sensor can be modelled as a resistor whose value $R_s(x)$ depends on the measurand $x$. To make a microcontroller sense $x$, we need to **convert $R_s$ into $V_{ADC}$**. The simplest and most KTU-relevant technique is the **voltage divider**.

### 2.2 Topology 1 — Single Resistive Sensor with Voltage Divider

**Circuit Description**: A reference resistor $R_{ref}$ is placed in series with the sensor $R_s$, and the divider is excited by a stable DC voltage $V_{EX}$ (often $V_{CC}$ of the MCU, e.g., **3.3 V**). The midpoint is the analog output $V_o$.

**Why it works**: As $R_s$ changes, the voltage at the divider tap changes predictably. The MCU samples this voltage via its ADC.

**Key Drawback**: The output is **non-linear in $R_s$** (hyperbolic). Sensitivity is highest when $R_s \approx R_{ref}$.

**Step-by-Step Logic**:
1. Apply stable excitation $V_{EX}$ across the series combination of $R_s$ and $R_{ref}$.
2. By the voltage-divider rule, the output voltage at the tap is $V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$.
3. Feed $V_o$ directly to an MCU ADC channel (or via a unity-gain buffer to prevent loading).
4. MCU firmware computes the digital code $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_o}{V_{REF}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor$ and then inverts the divider equation to recover $R_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip (KTU Lab Standard)**: Always add a small **R-C low-pass filter** (e.g., $R_f = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_f = 100\,\text{nF}$) between the divider tap and the ADC pin. This suppresses broadband noise and prevents the MCU's sample-and-hold capacitor ($C_{S/H} \approx 5$–$20$ pF) from causing $V_o$ to droop during acquisition.

### 2.3 Topology 2 — Differential (Half-Bridge) Configuration

**Circuit Description**: Two resistive sensors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form one half of a Wheatstone bridge, with two precision reference resistors $R_3$ and $R_4$ completing the other half. The output is taken **between the two midpoints** (differential).

**Why it works**: When $R_1$ and $R_2$ experience the **same common-mode change** (e.g., ambient temperature drift), the differential output is **zero**, achieving first-order rejection of common-mode errors.

**Key Drawback**: Only half the sensors are active; sensitivity is half of a full-bridge.

**Logic Steps**:
1. Excite the bridge with $V_{EX}$.
2. Compute the two tap voltages: $V_A = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_3}$ and $V_B = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_2 + R_4}$.
3. The differential output is $V_{OD} = V_A - V_B$.
4. Feed $V_{OD}$ to a **differential amplifier** (e.g., instrumentation amplifier) or to two MCU ADC channels and subtract in firmware.

### 2.4 Topology 3 — Bridge (Full-Bridge / Wheatstone) Configuration

**Circuit Description**: Four arms — typically **all four active sensors** in a load cell — form a complete Wheatstone bridge. Two opposite nodes receive $V_{EX}$ and GND; the other two are the differential output nodes.

**Why it works**:
- **Maximum sensitivity** (4× the single-divider case).
- **Inherent common-mode rejection** when all four sensors are co-located.
- **Self-compensating** for temperature and lead-resistance effects.

**Logic Steps**:
1. Excite the bridge diagonally with $V_{EX}$.
2. The differential output, in general, is:
$$V_{OD} = V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$$
3. For a **balanced bridge at rest** ($R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$), $V_{OD} = 0$ V.
4. For a **symmetric full-bridge** where two opposite arms increase by $\Delta R$ and the other two decrease by $\Delta R$:
$$V_{OD} = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$$
5. Feed $V_{OD}$ to an **instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD620)** to bring it to a level compatible with the MCU ADC range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern**: The most commonly asked derivation is the small-signal linearization of a Wheatstone bridge. Memorize both the **exact non-linear equation** and the **linearized approximation** for small $\Delta R$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet — Print & Memorize**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Single-divider output | $V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$ | Valid for $0 < R_s < \infty$ |
| 2 | Linearized single-divider (around $R_s = R_0$) | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{EX}}{2} + \dfrac{V_{EX}}{4R_0}\Delta R_s$ | Best for $R_{ref} = R_0$ |
| 3 | Bridge exact output | $V_{OD} = V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | Non-linear in $R$ |
| 4 | Symmetric full-bridge (two $\uparrow$, two $\downarrow$) | $V_{OD} = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Maximum sensitivity |
| 5 | Half-bridge (one sensor active) | $V_{OD} = \dfrac{V_{EX}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Half of full-bridge |
| 6 | Sensitivity constant | $V_{OD} \approx V_{EX} \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | $n = 1$ (quarter), $2$ (half), $4$ (full) |
| 7 | ADC LSB voltage | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | $V_{REF} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $n=12$ → $806\,\mu\text{V}$ |
| 8 | Sensor self-heating error | $\Delta T = \dfrac{I^2 R_s}{\delta}$ | $\delta$ = thermal dissipation coefficient |
| 9 | Lead-resistance compensation | $R_{\text{true}} = R_{\text{meas}} - 2 R_{\text{lead}}$ | 3-wire / 4-wire RTD only |
| 10 | Instrumentation amp gain | $V_{\text{OUT}} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot V_{OD}$ | $R_G$ sets gain |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weighing scales & load cells**: Full-bridge strain gauges, 2 mV/V sensitivity, INA125P amplifier, 24-bit ADC.
- **Automotive MAP/MAF sensors**: Differential piezoresistive bridge for pressure sensing.
- **Medical infusion pumps**: Half-bridge RTD with lead-resistance compensation for high-accuracy temperature control.
- **HVAC & smart thermostats**: Single thermistor divider with NTC compensation lookup table in firmware.
- **Consumer wearables**: Optical PPG and SpO₂ sensors use differential photodiode-resistor networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exact Output of a Single Voltage Divider

**Starting point**: A series combination of $R_s$ and $R_{ref}$ is connected to a source $V_{EX}$ with the tap node having voltage $V_o$ measured with respect to ground.

**Step 1** — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$V_{EX} = V_{R_s} + V_{R_{ref}} = I \cdot R_s + I \cdot R_{ref}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the loop current $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{V_{EX}}{R_s + R_{ref}}$$

**Step 3** — Express the output voltage across $R_s$ (the tap-to-ground voltage) using Ohm's law:

$$V_o = I \cdot R_s = \dfrac{V_{EX} \cdot R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}}$$

**Step 4** — Discuss the limiting behaviour:
- If $R_s \to 0$ (short), then $V_o \to 0$ (sensor at minimum).
- If $R_s \to \infty$ (open), then $V_o \to V_{EX}$ (sensor at maximum).

**Step 5** — **Linearization around the operating point** $R_s = R_0$ (with $R_{ref} = R_0$ for maximum sensitivity):

Let $R_s = R_0 + \Delta R$. Then:

$$V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0 + \Delta R}$$

To linearize, use the identity $\dfrac{1}{2 + \epsilon} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{\epsilon}{2} + \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{4} - \ldots\right)$ with $\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \epsilon}{2 + \epsilon} \\
    &= \dfrac{V_{EX}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \epsilon}{1 + \epsilon/2} \\
    &= \dfrac{V_{EX}}{2} \cdot (1 + \epsilon) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\epsilon}{2} + \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{4} - \ldots\right) \\
    &= \dfrac{V_{EX}}{2} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\epsilon}{2} - \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{4} + \ldots\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\epsilon \ll 1$ (i.e., $\Delta R \ll R_0$), we retain only the first two terms:

$$V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{EX}}{2} + \dfrac{V_{EX}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0}$$

**Step 6** — The sensitivity (slope) is therefore:

$$\dfrac{dV_o}{dR_s}\bigg|_{R_0} = \dfrac{V_{EX}}{4R_0}$$

This is the **maximum possible sensitivity** for a single-divider circuit — it occurs when $R_{ref} = R_0$, the nominal sensor resistance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheatstone Bridge Exact and Linearized Output

**Starting point**: A classic Wheatstone bridge with arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ excited by $V_{EX}$.

**Step 1** — Identify the two voltage dividers:
- Left leg: $R_1$ (top) and $R_3$ (bottom); tap node $V_A$.
- Right leg: $R_2$ (top) and $R_4$ (bottom); tap node $V_B$.

**Step 2** — Apply the voltage-divider rule to each leg independently:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} \\
V_B &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Subtract to obtain the differential output:

$$
V_{OD} = V_A - V_B = V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_1+R_3} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_2+R_4}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use $V_{OD} = V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ — both are equivalent provided the arm labelling is consistent. The key idea is that $V_{OD}$ is the **difference between the two tap voltages**.

**Step 4** — Specialize to a **symmetric bridge** where all four arms are nominally $R$, with two opposite arms changing by $+\Delta R$ and the other two by $-\Delta R$ (a typical strain-gauge arrangement):

Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_3 = R - \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \Delta R$, $R_4 = R + \Delta R$ (or any consistent pairing).

Substitute into the general equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{OD} &= V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R - \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \Delta R)} - \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{(R - \Delta R) + (R + \Delta R)}\right) \\
       &= V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R - \Delta R}{2R} - \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R}\right) \\
       &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{(R - \Delta R) - (R + \Delta R)}{2R} \\
       &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{-2\Delta R}{2R} \\
       &= - V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sign depends on the chosen arm pairing; the **magnitude is**:

$$\boxed{\vert V_{OD} \vert = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

**Step 5** — For a **single-active-arm bridge** (one $R_1$ changes to $R + \Delta R$, all others remain $R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{OD} &= V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R}{R + (R + \Delta R)} - \dfrac{R}{R + R}\right) \\
       &= V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) \\
       &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R - (2R + \Delta R)/2}{2R + \Delta R} \\
       &= V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{-\Delta R / 2}{2R + \Delta R}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$\boxed{\vert V_{OD} \vert \approx \dfrac{V_{EX}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

This confirms that a **single-active-arm bridge gives ¼ the sensitivity** of a symmetric full-bridge.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — ADC Quantization and Resolution

**Starting point**: An MCU with $n$-bit ADC and reference voltage $V_{REF}$ samples the bridge output $V_{OD}$.

**Step 1** — The LSB (least significant bit) voltage is:

$$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2** — The digital output code is:

$$D = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{OD}}{V_{REF}} \cdot 2^n\right)$$

**Step 3** — The smallest resolvable $\Delta R$ is then obtained by inverting the linearized bridge equation $V_{OD} = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$, where $k = V_{EX} \cdot n/4$ (with $n$ = active-arm count factor: 1, 2, or 4):

$$\Delta R_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{R \cdot \text{LSB}}{k} = \dfrac{R \cdot V_{REF}}{k \cdot 2^n}$$

**Step 4** — Example calculation: $V_{EX} = 5$ V, $V_{REF} = 5$ V, $n = 12$ bits, $R = 350\,\Omega$ (typical strain gauge), full-bridge ($k = V_{EX}$):

$$\Delta R_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{350 \cdot 5}{5 \cdot 4096} = \dfrac{350}{4096} \approx 85.4\,\text{m}\Omega$$

**Step 5** — Translate to strain $\epsilon$ using the gauge factor $GF$:

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{GF} = \dfrac{85.4 \times 10^{-3} / 350}{2.0} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-4} = 122\,\mu\varepsilon$$

This corresponds to a stress resolution of $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon \approx 200\,\text{GPa} \cdot 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \approx 24.4\,\text{MPa}$ for a steel specimen. **This is the type of numerical problem KTU loves to set in Part B.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Topology Comparison

| Parameter | Single Divider | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Sensitivity factor ($V_{OD}/V_{EX} \cdot \Delta R/R$) | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ (linearized) | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $1$ |
| Linearity | Poor (hyperbolic) | Moderate | Excellent (intrinsic) |
| Common-mode rejection | **None** | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Moderate | Higher |
| Power consumption | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Lead-resistance error | Susceptible | Partially compensated | Self-compensating |
| Typical sensor count | 1 + 1 ref | 2 + 2 refs | 4 active |
| MCU ADC interface | Direct (single-ended) | Differential (via in-amp or 2 ADCs) | Differential (via in-amp) |
| Example KTU use | Thermistor alarm | RTD temperature | Load cell / pressure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Unified Interfacing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Measurand] --> B[Resistive Sensor Network]
    B --> C{Sensor Topology}
    C -->|Single Divider| D1[Voltage Divider with Rref]
    C -->|Half Bridge| D2[Wheatstone Half-Bridge]
    C -->|Full Bridge| D3[Wheatstone Full-Bridge]
    D1 --> E[Excitation VEX and Buffer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D1 --> F1[Unity Gain Buffer OpAmp]
    D2 --> F2[Differential Amplifier or Instrumentation Amp]
    D3 --> F2
    F1 --> G[RC Low Pass Filter 1kHz Cutoff]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[MCU ADC Input Pin]
    H --> I[12 bit or 16 bit SAR ADC]
    I --> J[Digital Code D]
    J --> K[Firmware Linearization and Calibration]
    K --> L[Engineering Units Display or UART Output]
```

### 4.2 Single Voltage-Divider Detailed Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEX[VEX Stable Reference] --> RREF[Rref Fixed Resistor]
    RREF --> TAP[Analog Tap Node]
    TAP --> RS[Rs Variable Sensor]
    RS --> GND1[GND]
    TAP --> FILTER[RC Low Pass Rf Cf]
    FILTER --> ADCPIN[MCU ADC Channel]
    ADCPIN --> SAR[SAR ADC Engine]
    SAR --> CORE[MCU CPU Firmware]
```

### 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VEX[VEX Excitation] --> N1[Top Node A]
    GND[GND] --> N2[Bottom Node D]
    N1 --> R1[R1 Arm]
    N1 --> R2[R2 Arm]
    R1 --> N3[Node B]
    R2 --> N4[Node C]
    R3[R3 Arm] --> N3
    R4[R4 Arm] --> N4
    R3 --> N2
    R4 --> N2
    N3 --> AMP_P[Positive Input of Instrumentation Amp]
    N4 --> AMP_N[Negative Input of Instrumentation Amp]
    AMP_P --> INAMP[INA128 or AD620 Stage]
    AMP_N --> INAMP
    INAMP --> GAIN[Gain Stage G equals 1 plus 2R1 over Rgain]
    GAIN --> OUT[Single Ended Vout to MCU ADC]
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Chain for a Load Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LC[Strain Gauge Load Cell Full Bridge] --> EXC[Excitation 5V or 10V from MCU DAC or Precision Ref]
    EXC --> LC
    LC --> SIG[Diff Output Few mV]
    SIG --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 500]
    IA --> AMP[Single Ended 0 to 3.3V Output]
    AMP --> LPF[Second Order Sallen Key LPF at 10Hz]
    LPF --> MCU[MCU ADC Pin]
    MCU --> FW[Firmware Tare Scale and Calibration]
    FW --> DISP[LCD or UART Display in kg or N]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Instrumentation Amplifier?** A Wheatstone bridge output is **differential** but the MCU ADC is **single-ended**. Direct connection to two ADC channels and software subtraction is possible, but it loses CMRR. A proper in-amp provides **> 100 dB CMRR at DC**, which is essential in noisy industrial environments.

### 4.5 Three Topology Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPO1[Single Divider]
        A1[Vsensor] --> A2[Rref]
        A2 --> A3[Tap Vout ADC]
    end
    subgraph TOPO2[Half Bridge Differential]
        B1[R1] --> B2[Node A]
        B2 --> B3[R3]
        B3 --> B4[GND]
        B5[Vsensor] --> B6[R2]
        B6 --> B7[Node B]
        B7 --> B8[R4]
        B8 --> B4
    end
    subgraph TOPO3[Full Bridge]
        C1[Vsensor] --> C2[Node Top]
        C2 --> C3[R1]
        C2 --> C4[R2]
        C3 --> C5[Node Left Tap]
        C4 --> C6[Node Right Tap]
        C5 --> C7[R3]
        C6 --> C8[R4]
        C7 --> C9[GND Bottom]
        C8 --> C9
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **single voltage-divider** and a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration for interfacing a resistive sensor to a microcontroller. Which one offers better **common-mode noise rejection** and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a single voltage-divider, only **one sensor resistor** $R_s$ is placed in series with a fixed resistor $R_{ref}$ and the output is the tap voltage $V_o = V_{EX} R_s / (R_s + R_{ref})$. The output is **single-ended** with respect to ground.

In a Wheatstone bridge, **four arms** form two voltage dividers and the output is the **differential voltage** between the two tap nodes: $V_{OD} = V_{EX}\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_1+R_3} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_2+R_4}\right)$.

The **Wheatstone bridge** offers better common-mode noise rejection **(2 marks)** because any external noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum, EMI, ground-loop voltage) appears equally on both tap nodes and is cancelled by the differential measurement **(1 mark)**. The single divider has no such rejection since the noise adds directly to $V_o$.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A thermistor with nominal resistance $R_{25} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 25 °C is connected in a single voltage-divider with $R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{EX} = 3.3\,\text{V}$. Calculate the output voltage at 25 °C. Mention the **chief disadvantage** of this scheme.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At 25 °C, $R_s = R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, so:

$$V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{ref}} = 3.3 \cdot \dfrac{10}{20} = 1.65\,\text{V}$$

**(2 marks) — calculation**

**Chief disadvantage (1 mark):** The output is **highly non-linear** in $R_s$ (hyperbolic). It also offers **no common-mode rejection** and is susceptible to **self-heating errors** and **lead-resistance** drops, especially in long-cable installations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full Marks Solution

> **Q.A.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** A strain-gauge load cell uses a **full-bridge configuration** with all four arms nominally $R = 350\,\Omega$. When a load is applied, two opposite arms increase to $R + \Delta R$ and the other two decrease to $R - \Delta R$, where $\Delta R = 0.7\,\Omega$. The bridge is excited by $V_{EX} = 5\,\text{V}$ and its differential output is amplified by an instrumentation amplifier with gain $G = 100$ before being read by a 12-bit MCU ADC with $V_{REF} = 3.3\,\text{V}$.
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the bridge differential output voltage $V_{OD}$ in terms of $V_{EX}$, $R$, and $\Delta R$. Calculate the unamplified $V_{OD}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the amplified output voltage, the ADC LSB, and the digital code produced. Also find the **gauge factor** required to make this bridge produce a strain reading of $\epsilon = 1000\,\mu\varepsilon$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — Set up the Wheatstone bridge equation. Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_3 = R - \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \Delta R$, $R_4 = R + \Delta R$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Apply the voltage-divider rule to each leg:

$$V_A = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R - \Delta R}{2R}$$

$$V_B = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Subtract to obtain $V_{OD}$:

$$
V_{OD} = V_A - V_B = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{(R - \Delta R) - (R + \Delta R)}{2R} = -V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

The magnitude is $\vert V_{OD} \vert = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Substitute numerical values:

$$\vert V_{OD} \vert = 5 \cdot \dfrac{0.7}{350} = 5 \cdot 0.002 = 0.01\,\text{V} = 10\,\text{mV}$$

---

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Amplified output:

$$V_{\text{OUT}} = G \cdot \vert V_{OD} \vert = 100 \cdot 10\,\text{mV} = 1.0\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — ADC LSB:

$$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096} = 805.7\,\mu\text{V} \approx 806\,\mu\text{V}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Digital code:

$$D = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{OUT}}}{V_{REF}} \cdot 2^{12}\right) = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{1.0}{3.3} \cdot 4096\right) = \text{round}(1241.2) = 1241$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Required gauge factor for $\epsilon = 1000\,\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$:

The bridge output is $\vert V_{OD} \vert = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{EX} \cdot GF \cdot \epsilon$. For the bridge to output $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \epsilon$, and using $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$:

$$GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{0.7/350}{10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.002}{0.001} = 2.0$$

This is the **standard gauge factor for constantan alloy strain gauges**.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** A 3-wire RTD (PT100) is interfaced to an STM32 microcontroller for a precision temperature measurement system.
>
> **(a)** Draw the **single-element voltage-divider circuit** for the PT100 (with $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at 0 °C, $\alpha = 0.00385\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$), explain the role of $R_{ref}$, and calculate the tap voltage at $T = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ when $V_{EX} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ and $R_{ref} = 100\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **why a half-bridge or full-bridge configuration is preferred** for industrial RTD measurements. Compute the differential output of a half-bridge RTD arrangement at 100 °C when one RTD is at 100 °C and the compensating RTD is at 0 °C, with $V_{EX} = 5\,\text{V}$ and the two other arms being fixed 100 $\Omega$ resistors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — At 100 °C, RTD resistance is:

$$R_{100} = R_0(1 + \alpha T) = 100(1 + 0.00385 \cdot 100) = 100 \cdot 1.385 = 138.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Circuit: $V_{EX} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ across the series combination of $R_{ref} = 100\,\Omega$ (top) and the PT100 RTD (bottom); tap node feeds the MCU ADC. The role of $R_{ref}$ is to **convert the resistance change into a measurable voltage** by forming a voltage divider.

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Tap voltage:

$$V_o = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{R_{RTD}}{R_{ref} + R_{RTD}} = 3.3 \cdot \dfrac{138.5}{100 + 138.5} = 3.3 \cdot \dfrac{138.5}{238.5} = 3.3 \cdot 0.5807 = 1.916\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Notes on limitations: a single-divider RTD has **(i) non-linearity**, **(ii) no lead-resistance compensation** (a 10 m lead of 24 AWG copper adds $\approx 0.08\,\Omega$, equivalent to $\approx 0.2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ error), and **(iii) self-heating** from the divider current $I = V_{EX} / (R_{ref} + R_{RTD}) \approx 13.8\,\text{mA}$, causing $\Delta T = I^2 R / \delta$ of self-heating.

---

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — Why bridge is preferred:
- **Lead-resistance cancellation** in 3-wire / 4-wire bridge arrangements.
- **Common-mode rejection** of ambient temperature drift in connecting cables.
- **Higher sensitivity** than single divider.
- **Better linearity** when the active element is on opposite arms of the bridge.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Half-bridge: $R_1 = R_2 = 100\,\Omega$ (references), $R_3 = 138.5\,\Omega$ (RTD at 100 °C), $R_4 = 100\,\Omega$ (compensating RTD at 0 °C).

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Compute tap voltages with $V_{EX} = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$V_A = 5 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{138.5}{238.5} = 2.903\,\text{V}$$

$$V_B = 5 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{100}{200} = 2.500\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Differential output:

$$V_{OD} = V_A - V_B = 2.903 - 2.500 = 0.403\,\text{V} = 403\,\text{mV}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Never write the bridge output as $V_{OD} = V_{EX} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ without first stating the assumption** $\Delta R \ll R$ and the topology (quarter / half / full). Marks are deducted for unjustified approximations.
> 2. **Always include units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict.
> 3. **For the ADC code calculation, show the formula $D = V_{\text{OUT}} / V_{REF} \cdot 2^n$ explicitly.** Students who write only the final number lose 1 mark.
> 4. **For the gauge-factor part**, do not forget that the bridge **output is amplified** — the strain $\epsilon$ corresponds to the *bridge-side* $\Delta R / R$, not the amplified $V_{\text{OUT}}$.
> 5. **Confusing the "single divider" and "Wheatstone bridge" outputs** in derivation questions is the **#1 reason** students lose marks in Module 2. Always re-derive from KVL rather than memorizing the formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam Day**

- ✅ A **resistive sensor** is a two-terminal device whose resistance varies with a physical quantity.
- ✅ **Single voltage divider** = **one** sensor + **one** reference resistor; **non-linear**, **no CMRR**, **lowest cost**.
- ✅ **Half-bridge** = **two** active sensors + **two** reference resistors; **½ sensitivity** of full-bridge; some CMRR.
- ✅ **Full-bridge (Wheatstone)** = **four** active sensors (load cells); **maximum sensitivity**; **excellent linearity & CMRR**; **self-compensating for lead resistance and temperature**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity factors**: Single (linearized) → $\dfrac{V_{EX}}{4}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$, Quarter bridge → $\dfrac{V_{EX}}{4}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$, Half-bridge → $\dfrac{V_{EX}}{2}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$, Full-bridge → $V_{EX}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$.
- ✅ **ADC LSB** = $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$. With $V_{REF} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $n = 12$, LSB = **806 μV**.
- ✅ Always use a **buffer op-amp or instrumentation amplifier** between the bridge and the ADC to avoid loading and to provide CMRR.
- ✅ Insert an **R-C low-pass filter** ($R_f \approx 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_f \approx 100\,\text{nF}$, $f_c \approx 1.6\,\text{kHz}$) before the ADC pin to suppress noise and switching transients.
- ✅ The **gauge factor** $GF$ relates strain $\epsilon$ to fractional resistance change: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \epsilon$. For constantan: $GF \approx 2.0$.
- ✅ **Self-heating error**: $\Delta T = I^2 R / \delta$ — keep the divider current **low** (use large $R_{ref}$ or pulsed excitation).
- ✅ **Lead-resistance compensation** requires a **3-wire or 4-wire connection** — never use a simple 2-wire for precision RTD.
- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge balance condition**: $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ → $V_{OD} = 0$.
- ✅ **Differential output** must go to a **differential amplifier / in-amp**, not directly to a single-ended ADC.
- ✅ **Resolution in strain**: smallest detectable $\epsilon$ = $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n \cdot V_{EX} \cdot GF \cdot n_{\text{active}}}$.
- ✅ **Application mapping**: RTD → single or half-bridge; thermistor → single; strain gauge → full-bridge; LDR → single; load cell → full-bridge.
- ✅ **Direct interfacing** in KTU context means **DC-excited**, no frequency conversion; the **instrumentation amplifier is allowed** (it is a "direct" analog stage, not a modulation scheme).
- ✅ **Symmetric full-bridge** with two arms increasing and two decreasing gives the **highest sensitivity** and is intrinsically linear for small $\Delta R$.
- ✅ **KTU trick**: the question may ask for the **linearization error** if $\Delta R$ is not small. Always quote the **second-order error term** $V_o^{(2)} \approx -\dfrac{V_{EX}}{8}\left(\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}\right)^2$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Scheme for Sensor Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Scheme for Sensor Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Interfacing Scheme for a Sensor Array** is a systematic hardware-and-software architecture that defines the physical, electrical, and logical pathway through which multiple sensors (forming an *array* or *cluster*) are connected to a downstream signal processing unit such as a microcontroller, FPGA, Data Acquisition System (DAQ), or Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). The interfacing scheme governs *how* signals from N individual sensing elements are conditioned, switched, multiplexed, isolated, scaled, and digitized — with explicit attention to **timing, channel integrity, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), bandwidth, settling accuracy, and synchronization**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Outcome-Based Education), the interfacing scheme is treated as a *cyber-physical convergence layer* — bridging the analog physical domain (sensors) to the digital computational domain (processor). The scheme must satisfy three KTU-aligned Course Outcomes:

- **CO2:** Design signal conditioning and interfacing circuits for sensor arrays.
- **CO3:** Evaluate multiplexing, switching, and routing strategies for multi-sensor systems.
- **CO4:** Analyze timing, noise, and bandwidth trade-offs in array-level integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT521, Module 2):**
> *"Interfacing schemes for sensor arrays — analog and digital multiplexing, matrix addressing, sample-and-hold networks, frequency/time/code-division methods, settling time and crosstalk analysis, and bus-based architectures."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **press conference in a large auditorium** with 50 journalists, but only **one microphone** is available.

- **Scheme A (Parallel Interface):** Give every journalist their own dedicated microphone and sound channel. Clean, no interference, but wildly expensive and complex.
- **Scheme B (Multiplexed Interface):** A moderator says *"Mr. A, speak"* → Mr. A talks → *"Mr. B, speak"* → Mr. B talks. Only one person uses the mic at a time, but everyone eventually gets heard. This is *Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)*.
- **Scheme C (Matrix Interface):** The journalists are seated in a 10×5 grid. The moderator presses a button like *(Row 3, Column 4)*, and only that seat's mic turns on. This is *Matrix Addressing*.
- **Scheme D (FDM Interface):** Each journalist is given a different radio frequency. All can speak simultaneously; a tuner filters them out. This is *Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)*.

A **sensor array** is exactly this auditorium. Each *sensor* (e.g., 64 temperature thermistors on a battery pack) must "report" its value to a single ADC, and the **interfacing scheme** is the rulebook that decides whose turn it is, how fast, and how accurately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an Interfacing Scheme is Mandatory:**
> 1. **Cost:** A 64-channel dedicated ADC system is impractical; multiplexing to a single high-performance ADC is standard.
> 2. **Scalability:** Adding a sensor should not require redesigning the entire hardware — a structured scheme allows modular expansion.
> 3. **Synchronization:** Some applications (e.g., phased array radar, beam-forming microphones) require *coherent* sampling across all sensors.
> 4. **Power & Thermal Budget:** Sensor arrays in IoT/edge devices must share limited bias current and reference voltages.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **ADC Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** Typically **1 ns to 100 µs** for successive-approximation ADCs.
- **Multiplexer On-Resistance ($R_{on}$):** **10 Ω to 500 Ω** for CMOS analog switches.
- **Channel-to-Channel Crosstalk:** Expressed in **dB** (typically **–80 dB to –100 dB** at low frequencies).
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** For 0.01% accuracy, **6.9 × RC** (where RC is the dominant time constant).
- **Standard Reference Voltages:** **$V_{ref}$ = 1.024 V, 2.048 V, 3.300 V, 4.096 V, 5.000 V** (chosen to be exact powers of 2 for binary scaling).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for **naming the standard reference voltages** and **recognizing why 4.096 V is preferred for 12-bit ADCs** (1 LSB = 1 mV exactly).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensor Array Interfacing Schemes

The interfacing scheme can be taxonomically divided into **five primary categories**, each with sub-variants.

### 2.1.1 Parallel (Direct) Interfacing
- **Logic:** Each sensor has its own dedicated signal conditioning chain (amplifier, filter, ADC channel).
- **Pros:** Maximum bandwidth per channel, no switching transients, true simultaneous sampling.
- **Cons:** High cost, large PCB footprint, high power consumption, scalability bottleneck.
- **Use Case:** High-end DAQ systems, scientific instrumentation, phased-array beamforming.

### 2.1.2 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) — Analog Multiplexing
- **Logic:** A single high-speed analog multiplexer (e.g., 8:1, 16:1, 32:1, 64:1 MUX) sequentially connects each sensor to a **shared** signal conditioning and ADC chain.
- **Core Components:**
  * **Analog MUX IC** (e.g., CD4051, CD4052, ADG726, MUX36S16)
  * **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) amplifier** to freeze voltage during ADC conversion
  * **Sequencer / address decoder** (binary counter or shift register)
- **Critical Parameter: Settling Time**
  * When a MUX switches from channel *k* to channel *k+1*, the analog output must *settle* to within ±½ LSB of the new channel's true value **before** the ADC samples it.
  * Settling time has two components: the MUX's internal switching transient plus the RC charging of the load capacitance.

### 2.1.3 Matrix Addressing (Crossbar Switching)
- **Logic:** Sensors are organized in a **row × column** grid. A sensor is selected by energizing its row and column select lines. Particularly common in **resistive sensor arrays** (e.g., tactile sensor skins, IR focal plane arrays, memristor crossbars).
- **Sub-types:**
  * **Passive Matrix:** Simple row/column selection, but suffers from *sneak currents* through unselected elements.
  * **Active Matrix:** Each cell has an embedded transistor (e.g., TFT in image sensors) that isolates the cell during readout, eliminating sneak paths.
- **Sneak Path Current:** In an M×N passive matrix of resistive sensors, the leakage through unselected paths can be modeled as a resistive divider network that distorts the measured value.

### 2.1.4 Sample-and-Hold (Track-and-Hold) Networks
- **Logic:** A *single* S/H stage is shared across all channels in a TDM system. The S/H holds the *most recently* muxed voltage constant while the ADC performs its conversion.
- **Why Needed:** Successive-Approximation Register (SAR) ADCs need a *stable* input during the entire conversion period (typically 1–20 µs). Without S/H, voltage drift during conversion would corrupt the reading.
- **Key Parameters:**
  * **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** Time to charge the hold capacitor to within ½ LSB of the input.
  * **Aperture Uncertainty ($t_{ap}$):** Jitter in the exact moment the switch opens.
  * **Droop Rate:** Leakage-induced voltage decay during hold (typically 1 mV/ms for high-quality S/H).

### 2.1.5 Frequency / Code / Wavelength Division Multiplexing
- **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM):**
  * Each sensor modulates a unique carrier frequency (e.g., $f_1$ = 10 kHz, $f_2$ = 20 kHz, ..., $f_N$ = 100 kHz).
  * All sensors transmit simultaneously; a bank of bandpass filters (or FFT) separates them.
  * Common in **optical sensor arrays** where each sensor is paired with a unique wavelength (WDM).
- **Code Division Multiplexing (CDM):**
  * Each sensor is assigned a unique orthogonal (or pseudo-orthogonal) binary spreading code (e.g., Walsh-Hadamard, Gold codes).
  * All sensors share the same physical medium; correlation at the receiver extracts individual sensor signals.
  * Used in **distributed acoustic sensing (DAS)** and **wireless sensor networks (WSN)** with CDMA-like protocols.

### 2.1.6 Digital / Bus-Based Interfacing
- **Logic:** Each sensor (or sub-array) has an integrated ADC and a digital address. Sensors communicate over a shared digital bus.
- **Protocols:** I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, RS-485, Modbus, 1-Wire, LIN.
- **Address Schemes:**
  * **Hardware Addressing:** Each sensor has a unique chip-select pin.
  * **Software Addressing:** Each sensor has a unique 7-bit or 10-bit I²C address (up to 128 sensors on a single bus).
  * **Daisy-Chain Addressing:** Sensors connected in series (e.g., daisy-chain SPI, shift-register addressing).

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Architecture

| Step | Why It Exists |
|---|---|
| **1. Sensor Output** | Physical phenomena → electrical signal (mV, µA, Ω, pF). Often too small and noisy for direct ADC. |
| **2. Per-Channel Conditioning** | Amplification, linearization, cold-junction compensation, bridge excitation. Done *per channel* to preserve independence. |
| **3. Multiplexer** | Shares one expensive ADC across N sensors. Saves cost, power, board area. |
| **4. S/H Amplifier** | Freezes the analog voltage during ADC conversion. Eliminates aperture-time distortion. |
| **5. Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** | Adapts full-scale range to varying sensor amplitudes. Improves effective resolution. |
| **6. Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ to satisfy Nyquist and prevent spectral folding. |
| **7. ADC** | Converts analog voltage to digital code. |
| **8. Microcontroller / FPGA** | Sequences the MUX, controls the S/H, reads the ADC, performs averaging, scaling, calibration. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $t_s = -\ln(\epsilon) \cdot R_{on} C_L$ | Settling time to fractional error $\epsilon$ | $R_{on}$ in Ω, $C_L$ in F, $t_s$ in s |
| $t_{s,0.01\%} \approx 9.2 \, R_{on} C_L$ | Settling to 0.01% accuracy | $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ |
| $t_{s,0.1\%} \approx 6.9 \, R_{on} C_L$ | Settling to 0.1% accuracy | $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$ |
| $t_{s,1\,LSB,\,N\text{-bit}} \approx (N+1) \ln 2 \cdot R_{on} C_L$ | Settling to ±1 LSB of an N-bit ADC | $N$ = resolution in bits |
| $BW = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{on} C_L}$ | –3 dB bandwidth of MUX output | Hz |
| $T_{scan} = N \cdot t_{s}$ | Total scan time for N channels | Seconds |
| $f_{sample,eff} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Effective per-channel sample rate | Hz (samples/s) |
| $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_{s,eff}}{2}$ | Nyquist criterion for anti-aliasing | Hz |
| $SNR = 6.02 N + 1.76$ | Ideal SNR of an N-bit ADC | dB |
| $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | 1 LSB of an N-bit ADC | Volts |
| $Crosstalk_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{leak}}{V_{signal}} \right)$ | Channel-to-channel crosstalk | dB (negative) |
| $V_{drop} = I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} / C_{hold}$ | S/H droop voltage during hold | Volts |
| $R_{total} = R_{sensor} + R_{on} + R_{trace}$ | Total series resistance in MUX path | Ohms |
| $t_{acq} = (N+1) \ln 2 \cdot (R_{source} \vert\vert R_{on}) C_{hold}$ | S/H acquisition time | Seconds |
| $I_{sneak} = \dfrac{V_{exc}}{R_{sensor} + R_{parasitic}}$ | Sneak path current in passive matrix | Amperes |
| $f_{carrier,k} = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f$ | FDM carrier of channel *k* | Hz, with $\Delta f$ guard band |

> [!NOTE]
> **For all tables above:** Vertical bar in formulas is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown rendering.

| **Medical Imaging (CT, MRI)** | 1024–4096 photodiode array + active-matrix TFT readout + column-parallel ADC. |
