## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT] **Benchmark Numbers You Must Memorize for KTU Boards**
> - **Microcontroller GPIO source/sink current:** **±20 mA (typical), ±40 mA (absolute maximum)** per pin.
> - **Standard logic levels (TTL):** **V_IL ≤ 0.8 V**, **V_IH ≥ 2.0 V** (at V_CC = 5 V).
> - **Standard logic levels (CMOS 3.3 V):** **V_IL ≤ 0.8 V**, **V_IH ≥ 2.0 V** (with V_CC = 3.3 V).
> - **ADC input impedance (successive-approximation type):** typically **≥ 10 kΩ** to **≥ 1 MΩ** depending on architecture.
> - **ADC reference voltage** is the **full-scale input** that maps to digital code **2^n − 1**.

## 1.4 The Three Sub-Domains of Direct Interfacing

| # | Sub-Domain | Direction | Example Components |
|---|------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Resistive / Analog Sensor to ADC** | Sensor → µC | Thermistor, LDR, RTD, Strain Gauge |
| 2 | **Digital / Logic-Level Sensor to GPIO** | Sensor → µC | Push-button, Hall sensor, Reed switch, encoder |
| 3 | **Controller Output to Actuator** | µC → Actuator | LED, Buzzer, Relay coil, Small DC motor, Solenoid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Four Pillars of Direct Interfacing

### Pillar 1 — The Voltage Divider (Foundation of Resistive Sensor Interfacing)

Almost every resistive sensor (thermistor, LDR, strain gauge) is read by placing it in a **voltage divider network** with a fixed reference resistor. The sensor's resistance change becomes a proportional voltage change at the ADC pin.

**Why it works directly without any op-amp:**
The ADC pin has a very high input impedance (mega-ohms). It draws almost **zero current**, so it does not "load" the voltage divider. Thevenin voltage appears cleanly at the ADC input.

### Pillar 2 — Current Limiting (Foundation of Actuator Interfacing)

Microcontroller GPIO pins cannot supply unlimited current. An LED, for example, requires typically **10–20 mA** at **1.8–3.2 V** forward drop. Since the pin supplies **3.3 V or 5 V**, a **series resistor** must drop the excess voltage and limit the current. This is the most ubiquitous direct-interface element.

### Pillar 3 — Logic-Level Compatibility

A **5 V microcontroller** reading a **3.3 V sensor** is generally safe (3.3 V > 2.0 V V_IH). But a **3.3 V microcontroller** reading a **5 V sensor** will damage the input pin unless protected. Direct interfacing requires that the *signal voltage never exceed V_CC + 0.3 V* on any pin.

### Pillar 4 — Passive Protection

Direct interfacing always includes basic passive protection:
- **Flyback diode** across inductive loads (relays, solenoids, motors).
- **Pull-up / pull-down resistors** on floating inputs.
- **Decoupling capacitor** (typically **100 nF**) across every active IC's supply pins.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Memorize this table — every equation below is a 14-mark-question trigger.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage divider output | Volts (V) | Valid when ADC input impedance $Z_{in} \gg (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{load} = \dfrac{R_{source}}{R_{source} + R_{load}}$ | Loading error ratio (dimensionless) | unitless | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 3 | $V_{out,\,loaded} = V_{out,\,ideal} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)$ | Actual voltage under loading | V | Reduces accuracy of ADC reading |
| 4 | $R_{series} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{f}}{I_{f}}$ | LED current-limiting resistor | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $I_f \le I_{pin,max}$ (typically 20 mA) |
| 5 | $P_{R} = I_{f}^{2} \cdot R_{series}$ | Power dissipated in current-limit resistor | Watts (W) | Use a resistor with $\ge 2\times$ rating |
| 6 | $V_{f} \approx 1.8\text{–}3.2\text{ V}$ (LED), $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ (Si diode) | Forward voltage drop of diode/LED | V | Depends on semiconductor material |
| 7 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | ADC quantization step (LSB) | V | For an ideal n-bit ADC |
| 8 | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1)\right)$ | Digital output code for $V_{in}$ | integer | $0 \le D \le 2^{n}-1$ |
| 9 | $V_{in} = D \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Reconstructed input voltage | V | Reverse of formula 8 |
| 10 | $R_{pull} \le \dfrac{V_{IH}}{I_{leak}}$ (max) and $R_{pull} \ge \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{OH}}{I_{OH}}$ (min) | Pull-up / pull-down design bounds | $\Omega$ | Both bounds must be satisfied simultaneously |
| 11 | $R_{th}(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | Thermistor (NTC) resistance at temperature T | $\Omega$ | T, T₀ in Kelvin; B in Kelvin |
| 12 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant (for debounce / filter) | seconds (s) | Debounce: $\tau \approx 1\text{–}10\text{ ms}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Where Direct Interfacing Lives in Industry**
> - **Consumer electronics:** Every microwave oven, washing machine, and air conditioner uses direct-interfaced thermistors, push-buttons, and LEDs.
> - **Automotive dashboards:** Tactile buttons, LED indicators, and rotary encoders connect directly to BCM (Body Control Module) GPIOs.
> - **IoT edge nodes:** Low-power sensor nodes (like those on a digital agriculture field) use direct voltage-divider reads to maximize battery life — every extra op-amp burns µA of quiescent current.
> - **Educational kits:** Arduino, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi Pico tutorials are essentially 100% direct-interfacing exercises.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voltage Divider with Resistive Sensor (Full Derivation)

**Problem Setup:** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor is placed in series with a 10 kΩ fixed reference resistor $R_{ref}$. The series combination is excited by a stable **$V_{CC} = 5.0\text{ V}$** rail. The mid-point voltage $V_{out}$ is fed directly to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0\text{ V}$. Find (a) the digital output code at $T = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, and (b) the loading error if the ADC input impedance is **100 kΩ** instead of being ideal.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard voltage divider expression.**
By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Ohm's Law, the current through the series combination is:

$$
I = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{sensor} + R_{ref}}
$$

The output voltage at the divider tap is the voltage across $R_{ref}$:

$$
V_{out} = I \cdot R_{ref} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{R_{sensor} + R_{ref}}
$$

This is the standard voltage divider equation, here applied with $R_{sensor}$ as the variable element.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{sensor}$ at $T = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$.**
At 25 °C = 298.15 K, the thermistor's nominal resistance is its rated value, typically $R_0 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$.

$$
R_{sensor}(25\,^\circ\text{C}) = 10{,}000\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unloaded $V_{out}$.**
Substitute into the divider equation:

$$
V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 10{,}000} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 2.5\text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the corresponding 10-bit ADC code.**
For a 10-bit ADC, $2^{10} = 1024$ quantization levels, and the code ranges from **0** to **1023**. Using the digital-output formula:

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1)\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}(511.5) = 512
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the loading error when $Z_{in} = 100\text{ k}\Omega$.**
When the ADC is non-ideal, its input impedance $Z_{in}$ forms a parallel combination with $R_{ref}$. The effective load resistance becomes:

$$
R_{ref}^{\,\text{eff}} = R_{ref} \parallel Z_{in} = \frac{10{,}000 \cdot 100{,}000}{10{,}000 + 100{,}000} = \frac{10^{9}}{110{,}000} \approx 9090.91\ \Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the loaded $V_{out}$.**
Now substitute $R_{ref}^{\,\text{eff}}$ into the divider equation:

$$
V_{out,\,loaded} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{9090.91}{10{,}000 + 9090.91} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{9090.91}{19{,}090.91} \approx 2.3809\text{ V}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the absolute and percentage loading error.**

$$
\Delta V = V_{out,\,ideal} - V_{out,\,loaded} = 2.5000 - 2.3809 = 0.1191\text{ V}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{\%} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{out,\,ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.1191}{2.5000} \times 100\% \approx 4.76\%
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering conclusion.**
A 100 kΩ ADC input impedance is **inadequate** for this 10 kΩ source impedance sensor network — it would introduce a 4.76% systematic error. Either choose a sensor with higher nominal resistance (e.g., 100 kΩ thermistor), or use a **buffer amplifier (voltage follower)** — which is no longer *direct* interfacing, marking the boundary where direct interfacing fails.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rule of Thumb (Board-Exam Favourite):** Direct interfacing is valid only when $Z_{in,ADC} \ge 100 \times (R_1 \parallel R_2)$. Otherwise, an op-amp buffer is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LED Current-Limiting Resistor Design

**Problem Setup:** A red LED (forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$ at $I_f = 15\text{ mA}$) is to be driven directly from a **3.3 V** GPIO pin of an ESP32-class microcontroller. Design the current-limiting resistor and check its power dissipation.

**Step 1 — State the design constraint.**
The microcontroller pin can sink/source at most **$I_{pin,max} = 20\text{ mA}$** continuously. We target $I_f = 15\text{ mA}$, leaving a **5 mA** safety margin.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the LED loop.**

$$
V_{supply} = V_R + V_f
$$

The voltage that must drop across the series resistor is:

$$
V_R = V_{supply} - V_f = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3\text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resistance using Ohm's Law.**

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_f} = \frac{1.3}{0.015} \approx 86.67\ \Omega
$$

Choose the **next standard E12 value**: $R = 100\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 — Recompute the actual current with $R = 100\ \Omega$.**

$$
I_{f,\,actual} = \frac{1.3}{100} = 0.013\text{ A} = 13\text{ mA}
$$

This is within the 20 mA pin limit and gives a slightly dimmer but safe LED operation.

**Step 5 — Compute the power dissipation in the resistor.**

$$
P_R = I_{f,\,actual}^{2} \cdot R = (0.013)^{2} \cdot 100 = 0.0169\text{ W} = 16.9\text{ mW}
$$

**Step 6 — Select the resistor's power rating.**

A quarter-watt (**0.25 W = 250 mW**) resistor gives a safety factor of $250 / 16.9 \approx 14.8\times$, which is excellent.

> [!NOTE] **Common KTU Board Mistake (and how to lose 2 marks):** Forgetting to subtract $V_f$ from $V_{supply}$, and computing $R = V_{supply}/I_f$ alone. The LED **always** drops its own forward voltage first; the resistor drops the rest.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interfacing Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT] **The KTU Examiner's "Show Your Reasoning" Matrix**
> Use this table to justify *why* you chose a particular direct-interface topology. In 14-mark answers, this matrix earns 2–3 marks for *design rationale* alone.

| Sensor / Actuator Type | Output / Input Domain | Direct-Interface Element | Failure Mode if Ignored |
|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Resistance (Ω) | Voltage divider + 100 nF decoupling cap | Self-heating, ADC noise |
| **LDR** | Resistance (Ω) | Voltage divider to ADC | Slow response, dark-current error |
| **Strain gauge** | Resistance ($\Delta R/R$) | Wheatstone bridge → ADC (direct if bridge is complete) | Common-mode noise, nonlinearity |
| **Push-button / tactile switch** | Open/Short (kΩ / < 1 Ω) | Pull-up (10 kΩ) to V_CC + 100 nF debounce cap | False triggering (bounce) |
| **Hall-effect switch** | Open-drain (0 / 5 V) | Pull-up (10 kΩ) to V_CC | Floating output, indeterminate logic |
| **LED indicator** | Opto (needs 10–20 mA) | Series resistor (100–470 Ω) | Burnt pin / burnt LED |
| **Relay coil (5 V, 70 mA)** | Inductive (12–15 mW needed) | NPN driver transistor (BC547) + 1N4007 flyback diode | GPIO pin damage, EMI, contact arcing |
| **Small DC motor (6 V, 200 mA)** | Inductive, bidirectional desired | DPDT switch or L293D H-bridge (still qualifies as direct, since it is passive/driver only) | Motor stall current burns pin |
| **Piezo buzzer** | Capacitive (high-Z, high-V spike) | Series resistor (100 Ω) + clamping diode | Microcontroller reset or pin damage |
| **Solenoid valve** | Inductive (1–2 A spike) | MOSFET (IRF540) + flyback diode + bulk cap | Voltage spike destroys driver |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Top-Level Direct-Interfacing Architecture (Block Flow)

This diagram maps the **end-to-end information flow** in any direct-interfacing scheme, from the physical measurand on the sensor side to the physical action on the actuator side, passing through a single host controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Physical Environment]
        meas[Physical Measurand: Temperature Light Pressure Position]
    end

    subgraph SEN[Sensor Subsystem Direct Interface]
        sres[Resistive Sensor: R variable]
        div[Passive Voltage Divider: R fixed plus R sensor]
        sig[Analog Signal: 0 to V ref Volts]
    end

    subgraph HOST[Host Controller: Microcontroller or DAQ]
        adc[Analog to Digital Converter: 10 to 12 bit]
        mcu[Processing Core: CPU or DSP]
        dac[Digital to Analog Converter: optional]
        gpi[General Purpose Input Output Pin: 3.3 or 5 V logic]
        pwm[Pulse Width Modulator: for analog drive]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuator Subsystem Direct Interface]
        drv[Passive Driver: Resistor Transistor or MOSFET]
        act[Actuator Load: LED Motor Relay Solenoid Buzzer]
        fb[Flyback Diode: across inductive loads]
    end

    meas --> sres
    sres --> div
    div --> sig
    sig --> adc
    adc --> mcu
    mcu --> gpi
    mcu --> pwm
    pwm --> gpi
    gpi --> drv
    drv --> act
    fb --> act
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Resistive Sensor Direct-Interface Schematic (Mermaid Block Equivalent)

The actual circuit consists of a sensor and a fixed resistor forming a divider between $V_{CC}$ and GND, with the midpoint going to the ADC pin. Since Mermaid cannot draw literal resistor symbols, we use a **functional block equivalent** that preserves every electrical node.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vccNode[Node A: V CC equals 5.0 V DC rail]
    gndNode[Node B: GND equals 0 V reference]
    r1Node[Node C: top of divider, R fixed equals 10 kohm]
    r2Node[Node D: bottom of divider, R sensor equals 10 kohm nominal]
    tapNode[Node E: midpoint tap, output to ADC]
    adcNode[Node F: ADC input pin, Z in equals 100 kohm]
    decC[Node G: 100 nF decoupling capacitor, between Node E and GND]

    vccNode --- r1Node
    r1Node --- tapNode
    r2Node --- gndNode
    tapNode --- r2Node
    tapNode -.->|analog signal path| adcNode
    decC --- tapNode
    decC --- gndNode
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Actuator Direct-Interface Schematic (Mermaid Block Equivalent)

This is the canonical **GPIO → Resistor → LED → GND** configuration, the most-frequently-asked direct-interface exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pinNode[Node A: GPIO output pin, 3.3 V logic high or 0 V logic low]
    rNode[Node B: current limiting resistor R equals 100 ohm to 470 ohm]
    ledNode[Node C: LED anode, V f equals 1.8 to 3.2 V]
    gndNode[Node D: GND, cathode return path]

    pinNode --- rNode
    rNode --- ledNode
    ledNode --- gndNode
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Inductive-Load Direct-Interface (Relay with Flyback Protection)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pinNode[Node A: GPIO pin, drives base of transistor]
    rbase[Node B: base resistor R B equals 1 kohm, limits base current]
    qnode[Node C: NPN transistor Q1, BC547 or 2N2222, collector to relay]
    coil[Node D: relay coil L, 5 V 70 mA inductive]
    fly[Node E: flyback diode 1N4007, cathode to V CC, anode to collector]
    vcc[Node F: V CC equals 5 V rail, supplies coil current]

    pinNode --- rbase
    rbase --- qnode
    qnode --- coil
    coil --- vcc
    fly --- qnode
    fly --- vcc
```

> [!NOTE] **Why the flyback diode?** When the NPN turns OFF, the relay coil's collapsing magnetic field induces a reverse-EMF spike of **tens to hundreds of volts** in microseconds. The 1N4007 clamps this spike to one diode drop above $V_{CC}$, protecting the transistor's collector-emitter junction. **This is the most-tested protection element in KTU boards.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> Each Part A question in KTU's 2024 scheme is a **direct-concept, 3-mark recall** question. Answers must be **≤ 80 words**, crisp, and must include any governing formula.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] (3 Marks)
**Define the term "direct interfacing scheme" in the context of sensor-actuator systems. Mention any two conditions under which direct interfacing becomes infeasible.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 80 words):**
A *direct interfacing scheme* connects a sensor or actuator to a host controller **without intermediate active signal-conditioning stages**, relying only on passive components (resistors, capacitors, diodes) for level shifting and current limiting. **(1 mark)**
Direct interfacing becomes infeasible when:
1. The sensor output impedance is comparable to the ADC input impedance, causing **loading errors > 5%**. **(1 mark)**
2. The actuator current demand exceeds the **GPIO pin's source/sink limit (typically 20 mA)**, requiring a driver. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)
**An LED with forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$ is to be driven from a 5 V GPIO pin at $I_f = 10\text{ mA}$. Calculate the series current-limiting resistor and its power dissipation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{0.010} = 300\ \Omega
$$

**(1.5 marks for the formula, 0.5 marks for the numerical substitution)**

$$
P_R = I_f^{2} \cdot R = (0.010)^{2} \cdot 300 = 0.030\text{ W} = 30\text{ mW}
$$

**(1 mark)** A quarter-watt (250 mW) resistor gives a **safety factor of 8.3×**, which is more than adequate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU's 2024 ESE pattern awards **14 marks per Part B question**, with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You may attempt **either Choice A or Choice B in full** (no mixed attempts allowed). Below are two fully-independent 14-mark model questions.

---

### **Choice A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Sample Model]

#### Q3 (a). [7 Marks]
**Explain, with a neat functional block diagram, the four main elements of a direct-interfacing scheme for a resistive sensor (such as an NTC thermistor) to a 10-bit SAR-ADC of a microcontroller operating at $V_{CC} = 3.3\text{ V}$. Clearly state the role of each element.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The four main elements of a direct-interfacing scheme for a resistive sensor are:

**1. The Resistive Sensor (NTC Thermistor):** Converts temperature into a resistance change. Its nominal resistance at 25 °C is typically 10 kΩ. **(1 mark)**

**2. The Reference (Fixed) Resistor:** Forms a voltage divider with the sensor. Chosen equal to the sensor's mid-range resistance (10 kΩ) to maximize dynamic range. **(1 mark)**

**3. The Decoupling Capacitor (100 nF):** Placed at the divider midpoint to GND. Suppresses high-frequency noise and provides a charge reservoir for the ADC's sample-and-hold capacitor, improving conversion accuracy. **(1 mark)**

**4. The ADC Input Pin:** Samples the midpoint voltage, which is the analog representation of temperature. The digital output is:

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023\right)
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Functional Block Diagram (textual equivalent):**

```
   VCC (3.3V)
     |
     |
   [R_fixed = 10k]
     |
     +------[100nF]------GND
     |
     |
   (Mid-point --> ADC channel pin of microcontroller)
     |
     |
   [R_sensor = NTC]
     |
     |
    GND
```

**(2 marks)** for diagram + role description.

---

#### Q3 (b). [7 Marks]
**For the scheme in Q3(a), if the thermistor resistance is 8 kΩ at the current temperature, calculate:**
- **(i)** The voltage at the ADC pin.
- **(ii)** The corresponding 10-bit digital code.
- **(iii)** The temperature resolution per LSB, assuming the sensor spans 0 °C to 100 °C linearly across the full 0–3.3 V range.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(i) Voltage at ADC pin — (2 marks):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{sensor}}{R_{fixed} + R_{sensor}} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{8000}{10000 + 8000}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{8}{18} = 3.3 \cdot 0.4444 = 1.4667\text{ V}
$$

**(ii) 10-bit digital code — (2 marks):**

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{1.4667}{3.3} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}(454.6) = 455
$$

**(iii) Temperature resolution per LSB — (3 marks):**

One LSB corresponds to:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{3.3}{1024} = 3.2227\text{ mV per LSB}
$$

Since 0–100 °C maps linearly to 0–3.3 V:

$$
T_{LSB} = \frac{100\ ^\circ\text{C}}{1024} \approx 0.0977\ ^\circ\text{C per LSB} \approx 0.098\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

---

### **Choice B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Sample Model]

#### Q4 (a). [7 Marks]
**A 5 V relay coil (coil resistance 70 Ω, inductance ~50 mH) is to be switched ON/OFF by a 3.3 V GPIO pin of an ESP32 microcontroller. Design the complete direct-interface circuit. Justify the choice of every component.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Design Summary:**

A 3.3 V GPIO cannot directly drive a 5 V / 70 Ω relay (it would need **71 mA**, far above the 20 mA pin limit, and the voltage would be insufficient). We use an **NPN low-power transistor (BC547)** as a switch.

**Component Justification (each 1 mark):**

1. **Base Resistor $R_B$ (1 kΩ):** Limits base current. With $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ and GPIO at 3.3 V, base current $I_B = (3.3 - 0.7)/1000 = 2.6\text{ mA}$. With BC547's $\beta \ge 100$, this drives up to **260 mA** of collector current — sufficient. **(1 mark)**

2. **Flyback Diode 1N4007 (across coil):** Cathode to +5 V, anode to collector. Clamps the inductive kickback to one diode drop (~0.7 V) above +5 V, protecting the transistor. **(1 mark)**

3. **BC547 NPN Transistor:** Acts as a low-side switch. Saturated ON when GPIO is HIGH. **(1 mark)**

4. **Pull-down resistor on GPIO (10 kΩ):** Ensures the transistor is OFF during microcontroller reset, when the GPIO is in high-impedance state. Prevents accidental relay activation at boot. **(1 mark)**

5. **Freewheeling capacitor across relay contacts (optional, 100 nF):** Suppresses contact arcing for longer contact life. **(1 mark)**

6. **Separate 5 V supply for the relay coil** (not powered by the GPIO). **(1 mark)**

7. **Schematic justification:** When GPIO=3.3 V → $I_B$ flows → transistor saturates → collector pulled to ~0.2 V → coil energizes with $I_C = (5 - 0.2)/70 \approx 68.6\text{ mA}$. When GPIO=0 V → transistor OFF → coil de-energizes → flyback diode carries the recirculating current. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Q4 (b). [7 Marks]
**Compare direct interfacing with indirect (signal-conditioned) interfacing across six parameters. State the most important limitation of direct interfacing.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| # | Parameter | Direct Interfacing | Indirect (Signal-Conditioned) Interfacing |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Signal conditioning** | None — passive only | Op-amps, isolators, level shifters used |
| 2 | **Cost** | Low (few cents in components) | Higher (IC cost, board area) |
| 3 | **Power consumption** | Minimal (only leakage) | Adds quiescent current of active ICs |
| 4 | **Accuracy** | Limited by loading, ADC impedance | High (buffers isolate loading effects) |
| 5 | **Noise immunity** | Poor (sensor wire picks EMI directly) | Good (filtering, isolation, common-mode rejection) |
| 6 | **Range of sensors supported** | Only low-impedance or logic-level sensors | Supports virtually any sensor (thermocouples, etc.) |
| 7 | **Speed of design** | Fast prototyping | Requires analog design expertise |

**(6 marks — 1 mark per row, capped at 6 rows, the 7th is the limitation row)**

**Most important limitation of direct interfacing — (1 mark):**
Direct interfacing is fundamentally limited by the **loading effect** when the source impedance is high (e.g., a pH sensor with > 10⁸ Ω source impedance, or a thermocouple with ~10 µV/°C output). In these cases, the ADC input impedance is no longer negligible, and the voltage divider formed by the source and the ADC distorts the reading. This is when indirect interfacing (with an op-amp instrumentation front-end) becomes mandatory.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Lose Marks on Direct-Interfacing Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $V_f$ in LED resistor design** (–2 marks). Many students write $R = V_{CC}/I_f$ without subtracting the LED's forward drop.
>
> 2. **Ignoring ADC loading error** (–2 marks). The examiner will specifically test whether you computed the **effective resistance** $R_{ref} \parallel Z_{in}$ and showed the voltage drop.
>
> 3. **Omitting the flyback diode in relay/motor designs** (–2 to –3 marks). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists "protection of inductive loads" as a CO. Drawing a relay without a flyback diode is an automatic mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Confusing pull-up and pull-down resistor roles** (–1 mark). Pull-up: resistor to V_CC (default HIGH, active-LOW input). Pull-down: resistor to GND (default LOW, active-HIGH input).
>
> 5. **Using non-standard E12 resistor values in numerical answers** (–0.5 marks). Always express answers in standard values (10, 12, 15, 22, 33, 47, 68, 100, 120, 150, 220, 330, 470, 680 Ω) or explain your choice.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the safety factor on resistor power rating** (–1 mark). State that a resistor rated at **≥ 2× the computed dissipation** is selected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final Pre-Exam Checklist — Read Twice Before Submitting**

- [ ] **Definition:** Direct interfacing = sensor/actuator to host with **only passive components** in between.
- [ ] **Three sub-domains:** resistive sensors (voltage divider), digital sensors (pull-up + debounce), actuators (current-limit + flyback).
- [ ] **Voltage divider formula:** $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$ — memorize and practice variations.
- [ ] **Loading error rule:** Direct interface is valid only when $Z_{in,ADC} \ge 100 \times (R_1 \parallel R_2)$.
- [ ] **LED resistor formula:** $R = (V_{supply} - V_f)/I_f$. Always state the **next E12 standard value**.
- [ ] **Power rating:** Choose resistor with $\ge 2\times$ computed $P_R$ to be safe.
- [ ] **ADC LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^{n}$. For 10-bit at 3.3 V, this is **3.223 mV** — memorize this number.
- [ ] **Pull-up resistor (digital inputs):** Typical value **10 kΩ**, ensures default HIGH state with negligible current draw.
- [ ] **Debounce capacitor (mechanical switches):** **100 nF** ceramic in parallel with a 10 kΩ pull-up gives $\tau = 1\text{ ms}$, killing the 1–5 ms bounce duration.
- [ ] **Flyback diode (relay, solenoid, motor):** **1N4007** (or any fast-recovery rectifier) across the inductive load, cathode to +V.
- [ ] **GPIO current limit:** **20 mA continuous**, **40 mA absolute maximum** per pin. Never exceed 20 mA in designs.
- [ ] **Logic level compatibility:** 3.3 V sensor → 5 V MCU is **safe**; 5 V sensor → 3.3 V MCU is **unsafe** (need a level shifter — at which point it is no longer *direct*).
- [ ] **NTC thermistor formula:** $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — note T must be in **Kelvin**.
- [ ] **Decoupling capacitor:** **100 nF** across every IC's V_CC and GND pins, placed within 5 mm of the IC.
- [ ] **Worst-case design principle:** Always size for **maximum actuator current** and **minimum sensor resistance** (highest divider current) when checking pin ratings.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Direct Interfacing Schemes**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for PECPT521 — Sensor and Actuator Integration (2024 Scheme).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advancement in Design of Interfacing Circuits for Lossy Capacitive Sensors: Lossy Capacitive Sensor Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Advancement in Design of Interfacing Circuits for Lossy Capacitive Sensors: Lossy Capacitive Sensor Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Lossy Capacitive Sensor** is a capacitive transducer whose equivalent electrical model is no longer a pure (ideal) capacitance, but rather a **parallel combination of a sensor capacitance $C_x$ and a parasitic leakage resistance $R_x$** (also called the dielectric loss resistance or insulation resistance). This non-ideal behavior arises from the finite conductivity of the dielectric medium, surface leakage paths, and imperfections in the insulating materials used to fabricate the sensor. The loss mechanism is quantitatively characterized by the **Loss Tangent** (or **Dissipation Factor**), denoted as $\tan \delta$, which represents the ratio of the energy dissipated per cycle to the energy stored per cycle in the capacitor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, the focus is on **"advancements in the design of interfacing circuits"** that specifically **compensate** for the lossy nature of real-world capacitive sensors, which deviates significantly from the ideal capacitance model taught in basic electrical theory. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **leaky water tank**: - An **ideal capacitor** is like a perfectly sealed water tank — it holds 100% of the charge you pour in, and it releases 100% when needed. - A **lossy capacitor** is like a water tank with a slow leak — some of the water (charge) trickles out through a small drain (the leakage resistance $R_x$) before you can use it. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world example:** When you build a parallel-plate capacitive humidity sensor, the water vapor absorbed by the hygroscopic dielectric changes capacitance $C_x$ — but it also slightly increases the dielectric's conductivity, creating a **leakage path** modeled as $R_x$. The interface circuit must "see through" this leakage to accurately measure the actual capacitance. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following key metrics are central to KTU 2024 examinations on this topic: - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ - **Dielectric constant of the medium:** $\varepsilon_r$ (dimensionless, typically **1–10** for common sensor dielectrics) - **Operating frequency range for high-Q readout:** $f \in \mathbf{[1 \text{ kHz}, 1 \text{ MHz}]}$ - **Acceptable dissipation factor for precision sensors:** $\tan \delta < \mathbf{0.01}$ at the operating frequency ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Model A lossy capacitive sensor is represented by two equivalent topologies: **Parallel RC Model (most common for sensor analysis):** - Sensor Capacitance: $C_x$ (in Farads) - Leakage Resistance: $R_x$ (in Ohms), typically in the range **$10^6$ – $10^{12}\ \Omega$** **Series RC Model** (used when the dielectric is highly conductive): - Series Capacitance: $C_s$ - Series Resistance: $R_s$ (represents the bulk conductivity of the dielectric) The two models are **mathematically equivalent** at a single frequency and can be interconverted using standard transformation equations. ### 2.2 Why Does a Capacitive Sensor Become "Lossy"? The loss mechanism originates from three primary physical phenomena: 1. **Dielectric Absorption & Conduction Loss:** Real dielectric materials have non-zero conductivity, allowing a small DC leakage current to flow through the bulk of the insulator. 2. **Surface Leakage:** Contaminants, moisture, and ionic deposits on the sensor's outer surface create a resistive path between the two electrodes. 3. **Fringe Field Effects:** At high frequencies, the electric field lines leaking out of the capacitor structure encounter lossy surrounding media (e.g., the PCB substrate), contributing additional parasitic conductance. > [!NOTE] > **How this affects the interface design:** A purely capacitive interface (e.g., a simple charge amplifier) will produce a slowly drifting or phase-shifted output if the sensor has significant $R_x$. The interface circuit must either (a) operate at a frequency where $R_x$ is negligible compared to the capacitive reactance, or (b) actively measure and cancel the loss component. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Parallel Admittance | $Y = \dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x$ | Siemens (S) | Direct input to AC bridge analysis | | 2 | Complex Impedance | $Z = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} - j \dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Frequency-response analysis | | 3 | Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_x C_x}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies sensor quality | | 4 | Quality Factor | $Q = \omega R_x C_x = \dfrac{1}{\tan \delta}$ | Dimensionless | Higher $Q$ = better sensor | | 5 | Sensor Time Constant | $\tau_x = R_x C_x$ | Seconds (s) | Determines the lowest usable excitation frequency | | 6 | Series-to-Parallel Transformation | $C_p = C_s(1 + \tan^2\delta)$ | Farads (F) | Model interconversion | | 7 | Series-to-Parallel Transformation | $R_p = R_s \dfrac{1 + \tan^2\delta}{\tan^2\delta}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Model interconversion | | 8 | Dissipated Power in Dielectric | $P_{loss} = V_{rms}^2 \, \omega C_x \tan \delta$ | Watts (W) | Self-heating estimation | | 9 | Cutoff Frequency (Self-discharge) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_x C_x}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Defines the high-pass behavior of the sensor | | 10 | Phase Angle of Impedance | $\phi = \arctan(-\omega R_x C_x) = -90^\circ + \delta$ | Degrees (°) | Used in phase-sensitive demodulation | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for Tables:** All absolute-value or division bars in the above table use $\dfrac{}$ or $\vert$ / $\mid$ to avoid breaking KTU/Markdown rendering. **Never use a literal `|` character inside a table cell.** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Lossy capacitive sensor characterization is essential in: - **Automotive:** Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS) using MEMS capacitive pressure sensors - **Medical:** Non-contact ECG/Electrode-skin interface modeling - **Industrial:** Humidity sensors (polymer-based capacitive RH sensors) - **Aerospace:** Fuel level sensing using capacitive probes in low-conductivity fuels - **Process Control:** Liquid level detection in chemical tanks where the medium is slightly conductive ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Complex Impedance of a Lossy Capacitive Sensor (Parallel RC) **Starting point:** The sensor is modeled as $R_x \parallel C_x$ across its two terminals. **Step 1:** Write the admittance of each branch. For the resistive branch: $\quad Y_R = \dfrac{1}{R_x}$ For the capacitive branch: $\quad Y_C = j\omega C_x$ **Step 2:** Total admittance is the sum (since they are in parallel): $$ Y_{total} = Y_R + Y_C = \dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x $$ **Step 3:** Convert admittance to impedance using $Z = 1/Y$: $$ Z = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x} $$ **Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator, $\left(\dfrac{1}{R_x} - j\omega C_x\right)$: $$ Z = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{R_x} - j\omega C_x}{\left(\dfrac{1}{R_x}\right)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2} $$ **Step 5:** Separate the real (resistive) and imaginary (reactive) parts: $$ Z = \underbrace{\dfrac{1/R_x}{(1/R_x)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}}_{R_{eq}} \; + \; j\underbrace{\left(\dfrac{-\omega C_x}{(1/R_x)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}\right)}_{X_{eq}} $$ **Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator of the real part by $R_x^2$ to obtain a more standard form: $$ R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} $$ **Step 7:** Similarly simplify the imaginary part: $$ X_{eq} = \dfrac{-\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} $$ **Step 8:** Combine the two parts into the final compact expression: $$ \boxed{\,Z(\omega) = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} \; - \; j\dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}\,} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Loss Tangent **Step 1:** In the phasor diagram, the loss angle $\delta$ is the small angle by which the current through a real capacitor **leads** the purely capacitive current. For a parallel RC circuit, the applied voltage is common to both $R_x$ and $C_x$. **Step 2:** The current through the resistor is $I_R = V/R_x$ (in phase with $V$). The current through the capacitor is $I_C = j\omega C_x V$ (leads $V$ by $90^\circ$). **Step 3:** The loss angle satisfies: $$ \tan \delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C} = \dfrac{V/R_x}{\omega C_x V} $$ **Step 4:** Cancel $V$ to obtain the canonical form: $$ \boxed{\,\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_x C_x} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f R_x C_x}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** As $R_x \to \infty$ (perfect insulator), $\tan \delta \to 0$, recovering the ideal capacitor. As frequency $f$ increases, the loss tangent decreases — this is why high-precision capacitive interfaces use high-frequency excitation (typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz). ### 3.3 Derivation of the Series-to-Parallel Model Equivalence **Step 1:** A series RC circuit ($R_s$ and $C_s$ in series) has impedance: $$ Z_s = R_s + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_s} = R_s - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_s} $$ **Step 2:** Equate the real and imaginary parts of the series and parallel impedances at the same frequency: $$ R_s = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}, \qquad \dfrac{1}{\omega C_s} = \dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} $$ **Step 3:** Using the identity $\omega R_x C_x = 1/\tan \delta$, substitute and simplify: $$ \boxed{\,C_p = C_s (1 + \tan^2 \delta), \qquad R_p = R_s \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tan^2 \delta}{\tan^2 \delta}\,} $$ **Step 4:** For small loss ($\tan \delta \ll 1$), the approximations $C_p \approx C_s$ and $R_p \approx R_s / \tan^2 \delta$ hold. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Lossy Capacitive Sensor Readout System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Excitation Signal Generator
Sine Wave Source] --> node2[Reference Phase Shifter
0 to 90 Degree Calibration] node2 --> node3[Lossy Capacitive Sensor
Cx in parallel with Rx] node3 --> node4[Front End Charge Amplator
Convert Charge to Voltage] node4 --> node5[Programmable Gain Stage
Amplification and Filtering] node5 --> node6[Phase Sensitive Demodulator
Extract I and Q Components] node6 --> node7[Microcontroller Unit
Compute Cx and Rx Separately] node7 --> node8[Output Display
or Industrial Bus Interface] style node1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style node3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style node6 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016 style node7 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#661066 ``` ### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Schematic of a Lossy Capacitive Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Terminal 1 of Sensor Electrode] --- B[Parallel RC Block] B --- C[Terminal 2 of Sensor Electrode] subgraph parallelRC[Lossy Capacitive Sensor Equivalent Model] direction TB R1[Leakage Resistance Rx
Models Dielectric Conduction Loss] C1[Sensor Capacitance Cx
Depends on Physical Stimulus] end B -. Contains .-> parallelRC style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style R1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style C1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` ### 4.3 Frequency-Domain Response Behavior of a Lossy Capacitive Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Low Frequency Region
f less than fc] --> nodeB[Sensor Behaves as Resistor
Rx Dominates] nodeB --> nodeC[Loss Tangent is High
Poor Measurement Quality] nodeC --> nodeD[Choose Excitation Frequency
above the Cutoff fc] nodeD --> nodeE[High Frequency Region
f greater than 10 times fc] nodeE --> nodeF[Sensor Behaves as Ideal Capacitor
Capacitive Reactance Dominates] style nodeA fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style nodeE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style nodeF fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this architecture matters for KTU 2024:** Modern interfacing circuits use **dual-phase demodulation** (also called **I/Q detection**) to independently extract the in-phase (loss) and quadrature (capacitive) components. This allows the circuit to compute **both** $C_x$ **and** $R_x$ simultaneously — a major advancement over single-phase designs that only measure capacitance. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** *Define the term "loss tangent" for a lossy capacitive sensor. State its significance in sensor interfacing.* **Model Answer:** The loss tangent ($\tan \delta$) of a lossy capacitive sensor is the ratio of the resistive (loss) current to the capacitive (reactive) current in the equivalent parallel RC model, expressed as $\tan \delta = 1/(\omega R_x C_x)$. It is a **dimensionless** figure of merit that quantifies the deviation of the sensor from ideal capacitive behavior. **Significance in interfacing:** - It determines the **minimum operating frequency** required for accurate capacitance readout. - A high $\tan \delta$ implies large phase errors, demanding **phase-sensitive (lock-in) detection** in the interface circuit. - It directly limits the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of the measurement; lower loss tangent yields a cleaner output. *(3 marks: definition – 1, formula – 1, significance – 1)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** *State any three causes of dielectric loss in capacitive sensors.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Bulk conduction of the dielectric material** — real insulators have non-zero DC conductivity, allowing a small leakage current to flow between the electrodes. 2. **Surface leakage currents** — moisture, ionic contamination, and dust on the sensor's outer surface create a resistive path between the electrode terminals. 3. **Dielectric absorption (interfacial polarization)** — slow polarization mechanisms in heterogeneous dielectrics dissipate energy as heat at the operating frequency. *(3 marks: 1 mark per cause)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the complex impedance of a lossy capacitive sensor modeled as a parallel combination of $R_x$ and $C_x$. Clearly separate the real and imaginary parts. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A capacitive humidity sensor has $C_x = 250$ pF and a leakage resistance $R_x = 50$ M$\Omega$ at an operating frequency of $f = 100$ kHz. Calculate: (i) the loss tangent $\tan \delta$, (ii) the quality factor $Q$, (iii) the equivalent series resistance $R_s$, and (iv) the phase angle of the sensor impedance. **\[7 marks\]** ##### **Model Solution for Part (a):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The full derivation proceeds as: $$ Z(\omega) = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} - j\dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} $$ - The **real part** represents the effective series resistance contributed by the leakage path. - The **imaginary part** is the net capacitive reactance, reduced from its ideal value due to the loading effect of $R_x$. **Boundary conditions to mention:** - As $\omega \to 0$ (DC): $Z \to R_x$ (sensor is a pure resistor) - As $\omega \to \infty$ (very high frequency): $Z \to 0$ (sensor becomes a short circuit through the capacitance) *(7 marks: 2 for admittance formulation, 3 for algebraic manipulation, 2 for boundary/physical interpretation)* ##### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given:** $C_x = 250 \text{ pF} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $R_x = 50 \text{ M}\Omega = 50 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega$, $f = 100$ kHz. **Step 1:** Compute the angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 100 \times 10^3 = 6.2832 \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s}$ **Step 2:** Compute the product $\omega R_x C_x$: $\omega R_x C_x = (6.2832 \times 10^5)(50 \times 10^6)(250 \times 10^{-12}) = 7.854$ **Step 3:** (i) Loss tangent: $\tan \delta = 1/(\omega R_x C_x) = 1/7.854 = \mathbf{0.1273}$ **Step 4:** (ii) Quality factor: $Q = 1/\tan \delta = 1/0.1273 = \mathbf{7.854}$ **Step 5:** (iii) Equivalent series resistance (using $R_s = R_x / [1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2]$): $R_s = (50 \times 10^6) / [1 + (7.854)^2] = 50 \times 10^6 / 62.69 = \mathbf{7.97 \times 10^5}\ \Omega \approx \mathbf{797\ k\Omega}$ **Step 6:** (iv) Phase angle: $\phi = -90^\circ + \delta = -90^\circ + \arctan(0.1273) = -90^\circ + 7.26^\circ = \mathbf{-82.74^\circ}$ *(7 marks: 2 for subpart (i), 1 for subpart (ii), 2 for subpart (iii), 2 for subpart (iv))* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the modern phase-sensitive demodulation technique used to interface lossy capacitive sensors. Highlight how it overcomes the limitations of single-phase readout. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Explain the series-to-parallel and parallel-to-series equivalent circuit transformations for lossy capacitors. A capacitive sensor has a series representation of $C_s = 220$ pF and $R_s = 1.2$ k$\Omega$ at 50 kHz. Find its parallel equivalent $R_p$ and $C_p$. **\[7 marks\]** ##### **Model Solution for Part (a):** The modern **dual-phase (I/Q) demodulation** technique works as follows: 1. A **sinusoidal excitation** at a precise carrier frequency (typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz) is applied to the sensor bridge. 2. The sensor output is amplified by a **low-noise charge amplifier** that converts the displacement current into a voltage proportional to the sensor impedance. 3. The amplified signal is fed into **two synchronous demodulators** — one in-phase (I) and one quadrature (Q) — driven by the same reference oscillator, with the Q channel shifted by $90^\circ$. 4. The I-channel output is proportional to the **real part of the impedance** (i.e., proportional to $1/R_x$, the loss component). 5. The Q-channel output is proportional to the **imaginary part** (i.e., proportional to $\omega C_x$, the desired capacitance). 6. A microcontroller computes $C_x$ and $R_x$ **independently** using: $$ C_x = \dfrac{Q_{channel}}{\omega \cdot V_{ref} \cdot G}, \qquad R_x = \dfrac{V_{ref} \cdot G}{I_{channel}} $$ **Advantages over single-phase readout:** - Simultaneously extracts both $C_x$ and $R_x$, enabling **self-compensation** of leakage-induced drift. - Provides **rejection of $1/f$ noise and DC offsets** by AC coupling. - Allows the circuit to be **calibrated dynamically** for environmental changes (e.g., humidity-induced variation in $R_x$). *(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 2 for working principle, 2 for signal equations, 1 for advantage summary)* ##### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1:** Compute $\omega = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 = 3.1416 \times 10^5$ rad/s. **Step 2:** Compute the loss tangent for the series model: $\tan \delta = \omega R_s C_s = (3.1416 \times 10^5)(1200)(220 \times 10^{-12}) = 0.0829$ **Step 3:** Apply the transformation $C_p = C_s(1 + \tan^2 \delta)$: $C_p = 220 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.0069) = 220 \times 1.0069 = \mathbf{221.52 \text{ pF}}$ **Step 4:** Apply the transformation $R_p = R_s \cdot (1 + \tan^2 \delta) / \tan^2 \delta$: $R_p = 1200 \times (1.0069) / (0.0069) = 1200 \times 145.93 = \mathbf{1.751 \times 10^5}\ \Omega \approx \mathbf{175.1\ k\Omega}$ *(7 marks: 2 for theory, 2 for $\tan \delta$ calculation, 1.5 for $C_p$, 1.5 for $R_p$)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing the **series** and **parallel** formulas for $R$ and $C$. The most frequent error is writing $R_p = R_s \cdot \tan^2 \delta / (1 + \tan^2 \delta)$ instead of the correct inverse relationship. Memorize: **"Series resistance is small; parallel resistance is large."** > - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to **square the loss tangent** in the transformation. Many students write $C_p = C_s (1 + \tan \delta)$ — this is wrong; it must be $\tan^2 \delta$. > - **Common mistake 3:** In Part (a) derivations, leaving the result as a single fraction without **explicitly separating** the real and imaginary parts. KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for clearly labeling $\text{Re}(Z)$ and $\text{Im}(Z)$. > - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to mention **boundary conditions** ($\omega \to 0$ and $\omega \to \infty$). This is a frequently tested "trick" sub-question worth 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):** - A lossy capacitive sensor is modeled as $R_x \parallel C_x$ (parallel) or $R_s + C_s$ (series). - The **loss tangent** is the key figure of merit: $\tan \delta = 1/(\omega R_x C_x)$ for the parallel model. - The **quality factor** $Q = 1/\tan \delta$ must be as high as possible for precision applications. - The sensor **time constant** $\tau_x = R_x C_x$ sets the **lowest usable excitation frequency**. - **Series-to-parallel transformation** requires **squared** loss tangent terms — never forget the square. - The phase angle of a lossy sensor is **not exactly $-90^\circ$**; it is $-90^\circ + \delta$ where $\delta$ is the loss angle. - **Modern interfaces** use **I/Q (dual-phase) demodulation** to extract $C_x$ and $R_x$ simultaneously. - **Charge amplifiers** (capacitive feedback op-amp) are the standard front-end because they present a virtual ground that **minimizes the loading effect** of $R_x$. - **Synchronous (lock-in) detection** rejects $1/f$ noise and DC drift — essential for high-precision readout. - A **higher excitation frequency** reduces the apparent loss tangent — this is why capacitive sensor systems avoid low-frequency operation. - For KTU 2024 derivations, **always quote the boundary conditions** (DC limit and high-frequency limit) to earn full marks. - Remember the **magnitude of the impedance at resonance/cutoff** conditions: $|Z| = R_x / \sqrt{2}$ at $f = f_c = 1/(2\pi R_x C_x)$.

topics.title: Various Advanced Interfacing Schemes for Lossy Capacitive Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Various Advanced Interfacing Schemes for Lossy Capacitive Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Lossy Capacitive Sensor?

A **lossy capacitive sensor** is a capacitive transducer whose equivalent electrical model cannot be represented by a pure capacitance $C_x$ alone. Instead, it must be modeled as a **complex impedance** consisting of a variable sensing capacitance $C_x$ in parallel (or sometimes in series) with a finite parasitic/loss resistance $R_x$. The term "lossy" is borrowed from transmission-line and dielectric theory, where the dielectric material's inability to perfectly store charge introduces a dissipative current component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A lossy capacitive sensor is a two-terminal transducer whose lumped electrical equivalent circuit is represented by a parallel (or series) combination of a capacitance $C_x$ and a resistance $R_x$, where at least one of these elements is modulated by the measurand (physical variable such as pressure, humidity, displacement, chemical concentration, or acceleration).

The **admittance** of such a sensor is given by:

$$
Y_x = G_x + j\omega C_x
$$

where $G_x = 1/R_x$ is the conductance. The corresponding **complex impedance** is:

$$
Z_x = \frac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} - j \frac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}
$$

The presence of $R_x$ means the current through the sensor leads the voltage by an angle less than $90^\circ$, and energy is dissipated as heat in the dielectric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, the term "lossy" specifically refers to the unavoidable leakage path that appears in nearly all real capacitive sensors, including:
> - **Capacitive humidity sensors** (dielectric absorption current)
> - **Capacitive pressure sensors** (conductive diaphragm through gas medium)
> - **Capacitive chemical/biosensors** (ionic conduction through the sensitive layer)
> - **Capacitive accelerometers** (feedthrough and substrate leakage)
> - **Liquid level capacitive sensors** (electrolyte conductivity)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket"

Imagine you are trying to measure how much water is poured into a **leaky bucket**. The actual water level inside represents the **capacitance** (the quantity you want to measure), while the slow leak through the bucket's bottom represents the **loss resistance** $R_x$ (an unavoidable parasitic path).

- A **pure (ideal) capacitive sensor** = a perfectly sealed bucket. You can measure the water level by simply observing how much you poured in, because no water escapes.
- A **lossy capacitive sensor** = a leaky bucket. Even if you know exactly how much water entered, some is leaking out continuously. The measurement becomes ambiguous unless you somehow compensate for (or measure) the leak rate.
- The **interfacing circuit** is the equivalent of a smart measuring cup that *also* measures the leak rate, subtracts it, and gives you the true fill level.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in real engineering?** Many environmental and biomedical sensors are inherently lossy. For example, a capacitive humidity sensor exposed to a humid environment has a hygroscopic polymer dielectric that absorbs water; this absorbed water is slightly conductive, creating a parallel leakage resistance that varies with humidity. Ignoring this loss leads to measurement errors of 5–40% in precision applications.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used when characterizing lossy capacitive sensors:

- **Dielectric constant of vacuum:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- **Quality factor of the sensor:** $Q_x = \omega R_x C_x$ (dimensionless)
- **Loss tangent:** $\tan \delta = 1/Q_x = 1/(\omega R_x C_x)$ (dimensionless)
- **Dissipation factor (DF):** $D_x = G_x/(\omega C_x) = 1/Q_x$ (dimensionless)
- **Measurement excitation frequency:** typically **1 kHz to 10 MHz** depending on application
- **Standard parasitic capacitance range:** **0.1 pF to 10 nF**
- **Standard parallel loss resistance range:** **10 kΩ to 10 GΩ**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A lossy capacitor is *not* a defective capacitor. Loss is an intrinsic, deterministic property of the dielectric material and the sensor geometry. In many sensors (e.g., humidity, chemical), the loss resistance is itself a useful measurement channel carrying complementary information about the measurand.

## 1.4 The Core Challenge of Interfacing Lossy Capacitive Sensors

The fundamental problem addressed by all advanced interfacing schemes can be stated in one sentence:

> **How can we accurately extract the value of $C_x$ (the desired measurand) when the measured quantity (impedance, current, voltage, or phase) is contaminated by the unknown parallel resistance $R_x$?**

Standard low-impedance or high-impedance voltage-divider interfaces fail because they only respond to the *magnitude* of impedance, not its *phase*. The presence of $R_x$ shifts the phase and changes the magnitude in a way that depends on frequency, temperature, and the measurand itself. Advanced interfacing schemes use one or more of the following techniques:

1. **AC excitation at a defined frequency** so that the capacitive and resistive components can be separated in the complex plane.
2. **Phase-sensitive detection (synchronous demodulation)** to isolate the in-phase (resistive) and quadrature (capacitive) components.
3. **Reference-based auto-balancing** so that the loss resistance is automatically nulled out.
4. **Time-domain (charge-based) methods** that integrate the sensor current over a precisely defined interval, naturally rejecting DC leakage paths.
5. **Frequency translation and heterodyning** to shift the measurement to a band where $1/f$ noise is negligible.
6. **Digital feedback (sigma-delta / charge-balancing)** to convert the sensor capacitance directly into a digital code.

These six techniques form the basis of the advanced schemes covered in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Models of a Lossy Capacitive Sensor

A lossy capacitive sensor can be represented in two equivalent forms:

### (a) Parallel RC Model (most common for dielectric-type sensors)

$$
Z_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_x \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_x}}{R_x + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_x}} = \frac{R_x}{1 + j\omega R_x C_x}
$$

### (b) Series RC Model (most common for electrode-type sensors)

$$
Z_{\text{series}} = R_s + \frac{1}{j\omega C_s}
$$

The two models are mathematically equivalent for a single frequency but differ in their physical interpretation:

| Feature | Parallel Model | Series Model |
|---|---|---|
| Represents | Dielectric conduction loss | Electrode/contact resistance |
| Best for | Humidity, chemical sensors | MEMS, displacement sensors |
| $C$ is "real" capacitance | $C_x = C_s(1 + 1/Q^2)$ | $C_s$ |
| $R$ is "real" resistance | $R_x = R_s(1 + Q^2)$ | $R_s$ |
| Dominant at | Low frequency | High frequency |

## 2.2 The Role of the Loss Tangent in Sensor Accuracy

The **loss tangent** $\tan \delta$ is the figure of merit that quantifies how badly the sensor's loss will affect measurement accuracy. For a parallel RC model:

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{G_x}{\omega C_x} = \frac{1}{\omega R_x C_x}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Module 3):**
> A sensor is considered "well-behaved" (easy to interface) when $\tan \delta < 0.01$ at the operating frequency. When $\tan \delta > 0.1$, advanced interfacing schemes (covered in this module) become mandatory. When $\tan \delta > 1$, the sensor behaves more like a resistor than a capacitor, and standard capacitive interfaces will fail outright.

## 2.3 Decomposition of the Sensor Current

When a sinusoidal excitation $v(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, the current through a parallel-RC lossy sensor is:

$$
i(t) = V_0 \left[ \frac{1}{R_x} \sin(\omega t) + \omega C_x \cos(\omega t) \right]
$$

The first term is the **in-phase (resistive) component**, and the second term is the **quadrature (capacitive) component**, leading the voltage by $90^\circ$. The total current leads the voltage by a phase angle $\varphi$ where:

$$
\varphi = 90^\circ - \delta = 90^\circ - \arctan\left(\frac{1}{\omega R_x C_x}\right)
$$

The magnitude of the current is:

$$
I_0 = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{1}{R_x^2} + (\omega C_x)^2}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_x = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + j\omega R_x C_x}$ | Parallel RC impedance | Ω | Equivalent circuit |
| 2 | $Y_x = \dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x$ | Parallel RC admittance | S | Equivalent circuit |
| 3 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_x C_x}$ | Loss tangent | dimensionless | Sensor characterization |
| 4 | $Q_x = \omega R_x C_x$ | Quality factor | dimensionless | Sensor characterization |
| 5 | $C_x^{\text{meas}} = \dfrac{-\text{Im}(Y_x)}{\omega}$ | Capacitance from admittance | F | Phase-sensitive readout |
| 6 | $G_x^{\text{meas}} = \text{Re}(Y_x)$ | Conductance from admittance | S | Phase-sensitive readout |
| 7 | $V_{\text{out}} = -\dfrac{C_x}{C_f} V_{\text{in}}$ | Charge amplifier transfer | V | Charge-mode interface |
| 8 | $C_x = C_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{EXC}} - V_{\text{out}}}$ | Auto-balancing bridge | F | Bridge interface |
| 9 | $\tau_x = R_x C_x$ | Sensor time constant | s | Time-domain analysis |
| 10 | $f_{\text{opt}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_x C_x}$ | Optimal excitation frequency | Hz | Frequency selection |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\Delta C_x}{C_x} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{EXC}}} \cdot \dfrac{C_f + C_p + C_x}{C_f}$ | Sensitivity (charge amp) | dimensionless | Error analysis |
| 12 | $V_{\text{out,DC}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EXC}}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C_x - C_{\text{ref}}}{C_x + C_{\text{ref}}}$ | SC integrator balance | V | Switched-capacitor |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules):** All absolute-value and "such that" symbols in the formulas above use $\vert$ inside LaTeX math mode rather than the markdown pipe `|`. This preserves the markdown table structure.

## 2.5 Six Categories of Advanced Interfacing Schemes

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the advanced interfacing schemes into six families. Each family addresses a specific weakness of simpler interfaces when faced with sensor loss.

### 2.5.1 Category 1 — Charge Amplifier Based Interfaces

A **charge amplifier** converts the sensor's displacement current into an output voltage using a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and an inverting op-amp. The transfer function is ideally:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{C_x}{C_f} V_{\text{in}}
$$

**Key "Why":** The op-amp's virtual ground forces the sensor to discharge into $C_f$ rather than into its own parallel resistance $R_x$. This **bootstraps** the sensor terminals to a low-impedance point, dramatically reducing the error caused by $R_x$ at moderate frequencies.

**Limitation:** At low frequencies, the input bias current of the op-amp and the finite open-loop gain introduce drift; at high frequencies, the finite gain-bandwidth product (GBW) limits accuracy.

### 2.5.2 Category 2 — Switched-Capacitor (SC) Interfaces

A switched-capacitor circuit uses MOSFET switches and a reference capacitor $C_{\text{ref}}$ to periodically transfer charge packets $Q = C_{\text{ref}} \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$ into the integrator. The output of the SC integrator is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{C_x}{C_{\text{int}}} \cdot N \cdot V_{\text{ref}}
$$

where $N$ is the number of clock cycles. This converts the capacitance ratio into a voltage that is naturally ratiometric and insensitive to clock jitter.

**Key "How":** By synchronously demodulating, the SC circuit rejects DC offsets and low-frequency $1/f$ noise. The loss resistance $R_x$ only causes an error during the *switching transients*, which can be made arbitrarily small by using sufficiently fast switches and a sufficiently low source resistance.

### 2.5.3 Category 3 — AC Bridge with Synchronous (Phase-Sensitive) Detection

A classical **Wheatstone-style bridge** is excited by a sinusoidal source $V_{\text{EXC}} \sin(\omega t)$. The bridge output is amplified and fed into two multipliers driven by reference signals $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ (in-phase and quadrature components). After low-pass filtering:

$$
V_I = K \cdot \text{Re}(\Delta Z) = K \cdot \frac{R_{\text{ref}} R_x - R_x R_x}{(\text{denominator})}
$$

$$
V_Q = K \cdot \text{Im}(\Delta Z) = K \cdot \frac{-\omega R_{\text{ref}}^2 C_x \cdot R_x}{(\text{denominator})}
$$

The quadrature output is proportional to $C_x$ and largely independent of $R_x$, while the in-phase output is proportional to $R_x$ and largely independent of $C_x$. This provides **simultaneous dual-parameter readout** of both the desired capacitance and the interfering loss.

**Engineering Utility:** This is the gold-standard technique for laboratory characterization of capacitive sensors. It is used in precision LCR meters (e.g., Keysight E4980A, Wayne Kerr 6500B).

### 2.5.4 Category 4 — Auto-Balancing Bridge (Digital Impedance Meter Method)

The **auto-balancing bridge** uses an inverting amplifier with a reference impedance $Z_{\text{ref}}$ connected between the amplifier output and its summing junction. The op-amp automatically adjusts its output voltage $V_{\text{out}}$ until the summing junction is at virtual ground:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{EXC}} \cdot \frac{Z_{\text{ref}}}{Z_x} = -V_{\text{EXC}} \cdot \frac{Z_{\text{ref}} (1 + j\omega R_x C_x)}{R_x}
$$

The amplitude gives $\vert Z_x \vert$ and the phase gives the loss angle. The output is digitized and processed.

**Real-World Utility:** This is the architecture used in virtually every commercial handheld LCR meter. It provides four-terminal measurement, good noise rejection, and can measure capacitances from femtofarads to farads.

### 2.5.5 Category 5 — Relaxation Oscillator / Frequency-Output Interfaces

A relaxation oscillator converts the sensor capacitance $C_x$ into a frequency $f_0$:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2 R_{\text{ref}} C_x \ln 3} \quad \text{(for a classic 555-based oscillator)}
$$

The loss resistance $R_x$ appears in parallel with $C_x$ and changes the duty cycle rather than the period. By using a *zero-crossing comparator* and AC-coupling the timing node, the influence of $R_x$ can be reduced.

**Key "Why":** Frequency output is inherently noise-immune, can be transmitted digitally over long cables, and is directly compatible with microcontrollers. This is the dominant scheme for low-cost, battery-powered sensor nodes (e.g., IoT humidity sensors).

### 2.5.6 Category 6 — Sigma-Delta (Charge-Balancing) Interfaces

A **sigma-delta modulator** uses a switched-capacitor front-end, an integrator, a comparator, and a 1-bit DAC in a feedback loop. The modulator outputs a dense bitstream whose average value is proportional to $C_x / C_{\text{ref}}$. A digital decimation filter converts this to a high-resolution code (16–24 bits).

**Engineering Utility:** This is the architecture used in modern capacitive touch controllers (e.g., Cypress PSoC, Microchip AT42QT) and in MEMS accelerometer ASICs. It achieves sub-femtofarad resolution at low power.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

| Application | Dominant Interfacing Scheme | Reason for Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Laboratory LCR meters | Auto-balancing bridge + PSD | High precision, dual-parameter |
| MEMS accelerometers | Sigma-delta | Low power, high resolution |
| Industrial humidity sensors | Relaxation oscillator | Low cost, frequency output |
| Capacitive touch screens | Sigma-delta + mutual-cap | High SNR, multi-touch |
| Biomedical impedance spectroscopy | AC bridge + lock-in amplifier | Wide frequency range |
| Chemical / biosensor readout | Charge amplifier + SC | DC leakage rejection |
| Precision pressure transducers | Synchronous detector | Wide dynamic range |
| Harsh-environment level sensors | Relaxation oscillator + optocoupler | Galvanic isolation |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Complex Impedance of a Parallel-RC Lossy Sensor

We derive the impedance of a lossy sensor modeled as a parallel combination of $C_x$ and $R_x$.

**Step 1 — Write the admittance of each branch.**

The admittance of the resistor branch is $Y_R = 1/R_x$, and the admittance of the capacitor branch is $Y_C = j\omega C_x$.

**Step 2 — Apply the parallel admittance rule.**

For two elements in parallel, total admittance is the sum of individual admittances:

$$
Y_x = Y_R + Y_C = \frac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x
$$

**Step 3 — Take the reciprocal to obtain impedance.**

$$
Z_x = \frac{1}{Y_x} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_x} + j\omega C_x}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator.**

The complex conjugate of the denominator is $\left(\dfrac{1}{R_x} - j\omega C_x\right)$. Multiplying:

$$
Z_x = \frac{\dfrac{1}{R_x} - j\omega C_x}{\left(\dfrac{1}{R_x}\right)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Separate the real and imaginary parts.**

$$
Z_x = \frac{1/R_x}{(1/R_x)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2} \; - j \cdot \frac{\omega C_x}{(1/R_x)^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator of both terms by $R_x^2$ to simplify.**

$$
Z_x = \frac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2} \; - j \cdot \frac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}
$$

**Result:** The real part of the impedance is $R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{R_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}$, and the imaginary part is $X_{\text{eff}} = -\dfrac{\omega R_x^2 C_x}{1 + (\omega R_x C_x)^2}$.

**Interpretation:** Both the effective resistance and effective reactance are *frequency-dependent* when the sensor is lossy. This is the mathematical origin of all the design challenges addressed by advanced interfaces.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Phase Angle and Loss Tangent Relationship

**Step 1 — Express the phase of the impedance.**

The phase angle $\varphi$ of $Z_x$ is the arctangent of the ratio of imaginary to real parts (with sign convention for capacitive sensors):

$$
\varphi = \arctan\left( \frac{\text{Im}(Z_x)}{\text{Re}(Z_x)} \right) = \arctan\left( \frac{-\omega R_x^2 C_x}{R_x} \right) = \arctan(-\omega R_x C_x)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the loss angle $\delta$ as the deviation from $-90^\circ$.**

For a pure capacitor, $\varphi = -90^\circ$. For a lossy capacitor:

$$
\delta = -90^\circ - \varphi = -90^\circ - \arctan(-\omega R_x C_x) = \arctan\left( \frac{1}{\omega R_x C_x} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to the loss tangent.**

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{1}{\omega R_x C_x}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** When $\omega R_x C_x \gg 1$ (high frequency, high $Q$), $\tan \delta \to 0$ and the sensor behaves nearly ideally. When $\omega R_x C_x \ll 1$ (low frequency, low $Q$), $\tan \delta \to \infty$ and the sensor is dominated by its loss resistance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge Amplifier Output Voltage for a Lossy Sensor

We derive the actual output voltage when a parallel RC sensor is connected to a real (finite-gain) charge amplifier.

**Step 1 — Sensor model under AC excitation.**

Let $V_{\text{in}}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The current flowing into the summing junction is:

$$
i_x(t) = \frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{R_x} + V_0 \omega C_x \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — This current flows into the feedback impedance $Z_f = R_f \parallel (1/j\omega C_f)$.**

The op-amp keeps its inverting input at virtual ground, so all of $i_x$ must flow through $Z_f$. The output voltage is therefore:

$$
V_{\text{out}}(t) = -i_x(t) \cdot Z_f
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Z_f$ and separate in-phase and quadrature components.**

$$
V_{\text{out}}(t) = -\left[ \frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{R_x} + V_0 \omega C_x \cos(\omega t) \right] \cdot \frac{R_f}{1 + j\omega R_f C_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the magnitude of the capacitive (quadrature) term, which is the desired signal.**

In the limit $\omega R_f C_f \gg 1$ (the standard operating regime for a charge amplifier):

$$
\frac{R_f}{1 + j\omega R_f C_f} \approx \frac{1}{j\omega C_f} = -\frac{j}{\omega C_f}
$$

The quadrature term becomes:

$$
V_{\text{out,quad}}(t) \approx -V_0 \omega C_x \cos(\omega t) \cdot \left(-\frac{j}{\omega C_f}\right) = j V_0 \frac{C_x}{C_f} \cos(\omega t)
$$

Taking the real magnitude:

$$
|V_{\text{out,quad}}| = V_0 \frac{C_x}{C_f}
$$

**Result:** The desired (capacitive) signal is recovered as a clean sinusoid of amplitude $V_0 (C_x / C_f)$, completely independent of $R_x$.

**Step 5 — The unwanted (resistive) term becomes:**

$$
V_{\text{out,in-phase}}(t) = -\frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{R_x} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_f} = j \frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{\omega R_x C_f}
$$

This is $90^\circ$ phase-shifted from the desired signal and is proportional to $1/R_x$. It can be removed by a synchronous detector or a phase-locked loop, leaving only the desired capacitance information.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Switched-Capacitor Charge Transfer

We derive the output voltage of a basic SC integrator connected to a lossy sensor.

**Step 1 — Phase 1 of the clock: Sensor is connected to the integrator input.**

During phase $\phi_1$, the sensor is connected to the integrator summing node. The charge on the sensor capacitance is $Q_1 = C_x V_{\text{EXC}}$.

**Step 2 — Phase 2 of the clock: Sensor is disconnected; charge is transferred to feedback capacitor.**

During phase $\phi_2$, the sensor is disconnected. The integrator op-amp forces the charge $Q_1$ to transfer to the feedback capacitor $C_f$, changing its voltage by:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{Q_1}{C_f} = \frac{C_x V_{\text{EXC}}}{C_f}
$$

**Step 3 — After $N$ clock cycles, total output voltage is:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = N \cdot \frac{C_x V_{\text{EXC}}}{C_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of loss resistance $R_x$.**

During phase $\phi_1$, the loss resistance $R_x$ provides an additional DC path that bleeds charge from $C_x$ with time constant $\tau = R_x C_x$. If the clock period $T_{\text{clk}}$ is much shorter than $\tau$ (i.e., $T_{\text{clk}} \ll R_x C_x$), the charge loss is negligible:

$$
\frac{\Delta Q_{\text{loss}}}{Q_1} = 1 - e^{-T_{\text{clk}}/(R_x C_x)} \approx \frac{T_{\text{clk}}}{R_x C_x}
$$

**Result:** The relative error due to $R_x$ is approximately $T_{\text{clk}} / (R_x C_x)$. To keep this below 0.1%, the clock frequency must satisfy $f_{\text{clk}} > 1000 / (R_x C_x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical design rule (KTU Module 3):** Always choose the SC clock frequency at least **100× greater** than the sensor's loss-corner frequency $1/(2\pi R_x C_x)$. This is the design rule that makes SC interfaces immune to lossy sensor behavior.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Synchronous Detector Output for a Lossy Sensor

**Step 1 — Sensor output is $v_s(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \varphi)$, where $\varphi$ is shifted from $90^\circ$ by the loss.**

Write $v_s(t) = A \cos(\varphi)\sin(\omega t) + A\sin(\varphi)\cos(\omega t)$. The first term is the in-phase (resistive) component, and the second is the quadrature (capacitive) component.

**Step 2 — Multiply by in-phase reference $\sin(\omega t)$ and integrate over one period.**

$$
V_I = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_s(t) \sin(\omega t)\, dt = \frac{A \cos(\varphi)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by quadrature reference $\cos(\omega t)$ and integrate.**

$$
V_Q = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_s(t) \cos(\omega t)\, dt = \frac{A \sin(\varphi)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Recover $C_x$ and $R_x$ independently.**

Since $A = I_0$, $\cos(\varphi) = G_x / \sqrt{G_x^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}$, and $\sin(\varphi) = \omega C_x / \sqrt{G_x^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}$:

$$
V_I = \frac{V_0}{2} \cdot \frac{G_x}{\sqrt{G_x^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}}
$$

$$
V_Q = \frac{V_0}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega C_x}{\sqrt{G_x^2 + (\omega C_x)^2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio to eliminate the unknown gain factor $V_0/2$.**

$$
\frac{V_Q}{V_I} = \frac{\omega C_x}{G_x} = \omega R_x C_x = Q_x
$$

**Result:** The ratio $V_Q / V_I$ gives the *quality factor* of the sensor directly, providing a clean readout of $C_x$ that is ratiometric and insensitive to the absolute amplitude of the excitation.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Relaxation Oscillator Frequency with Lossy Sensor

A classic relaxation oscillator uses a Schmitt trigger with a reference resistor $R_{\text{ref}}$ charging the sensor capacitance $C_x$.

**Step 1 — Charging equation.**

The voltage across $C_x$ charges through $R_{\text{ref}}$ according to:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{\text{CC}} \left( 1 - e^{-t/(R_{\text{eq}} C_x)} \right)
$$

where $R_{\text{eq}} = R_{\text{ref}} \parallel R_x$ (the parallel combination of the reference and the loss resistance).

**Step 2 — Threshold crossing times.**

The Schmitt trigger switches at fractions $\beta V_{\text{CC}}$ and $(1-\beta) V_{\text{CC}}$ of the supply. The period is:

$$
T = R_{\text{eq}} C_x \ln\left( \frac{1 - \beta_{\text{low}}}{\beta_{\text{high}}} \right)
$$

For a standard 1/3 – 2/3 hysteresis:

$$
T = R_{\text{eq}} C_x \ln 3
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency of oscillation:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}} C_x \ln 3}
$$

**Step 4 — Error introduced by $R_x$:**

$$
\frac{\Delta f_0}{f_0} = -\frac{\Delta R_{\text{eq}}}{R_{\text{eq}}} = -\frac{R_{\text{ref}}}{R_{\text{ref}} + R_x}
$$

**Result:** The error is minimized when $R_x \gg R_{\text{ref}}$. In practice, designers choose $R_{\text{ref}}$ at least $100\times$ smaller than the smallest expected $R_x$ to limit the error to $<1\%$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scheme | Frequency Range | Resolution | Power | Cost | Robustness to Loss |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Charge amplifier | 100 Hz – 1 MHz | 0.1 fF | Medium | Low | Moderate |
| Switched capacitor | DC – 1 MHz | 1 fF | Medium | Medium | High (with $f_{\text{clk}}$ rule) |
| AC bridge + PSD | 1 mHz – 10 MHz | 0.01 fF | High | High | Very High |
| Auto-balancing bridge | 20 Hz – 10 MHz | 0.001 fF | High | High | Very High |
| Relaxation oscillator | DC – 1 kHz | 10 fF | Low | Very Low | Low to Moderate |
| Sigma-delta | DC – 100 kHz | 16 aF (asymptotic) | Low | Medium | High |
