# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Differentiate between piezoresistive and capacitive MEMS pressure sensors in terms of principle, sensitivity, and temperature drift.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Resistance change under stress | Capacitance change due to gap variation |
| Sensitivity | Moderate (≈ 1–5 mV/V) | High (depends on gap) |
| Temperature Drift | High (needs compensation) | Low (inherently stable) |
| Power Consumption | Lower (resistive bridge) | Higher (needs AC excitation) |
| Fabrication | Simple | More complex |

**Conclusion:** Capacitive sensors are preferred for **low-power, low-drift** applications; piezoresistive for **high-linearity, simple interfaces**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Explain briefly why a piezoelectric MEMS accelerometer cannot measure DC acceleration.**

**Model Answer:**

A piezoelectric material produces a charge $Q = d_{33} F$ only when the applied force **changes with time**. Under a constant DC force, the generated charge **leaks away** through the input resistance of the interface circuit due to finite insulation resistance (typically $10^{10}$–$10^{13}$ Ω). Therefore, piezoelectric sensors are fundamentally **AC-coupled** and cannot measure static acceleration. They are used for vibration measurement, dynamic force measurement, and acoustic sensing.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams and equations, explain the **working principle and design of a MEMS capacitive accelerometer**. Derive the expression for displacement sensitivity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **interfacing electronics** required for the accelerometer in part (a). Include charge amplifier and switched-capacitor architecture.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**1. Construction:**
A MEMS capacitive accelerometer consists of:
- A **proof mass** (typically 10–1000 µg) suspended by **four folded-beam flexures**.
- A series of **interdigitated or parallel-plate fingers** attached to the proof mass.
- **Fixed reference electrodes** on the substrate.
- Differential gap ≈ 1–5 µm.

**2. Working Principle:**
When the chip undergoes acceleration $a$, the proof mass experiences an inertial force $ma$ and displaces by $x$. This displacement **mismatches the gap** of two differential capacitors:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + x}$$

**3. Differential Capacitance Output:**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \, x}{d_0^2 - x^2}$$

For small $x \ll d_0$:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0^2} \cdot x$$

**4. Force Balance:**
From $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = ma$ at steady state:

$$x = \frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

**5. Final Displacement Sensitivity:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \, m}{d_0^2 \, k} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0^2 \, \omega_n^2}}$$

**6. Numerical Example:**
With $A = 10^{-8}$ m², $d_0 = 2$ µm, $m = 10^{-9}$ kg, $k = 0.5$ N/m:
$$\frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2 \cdot 8.85\times 10^{-12} \cdot 1 \cdot 10^{-8}}{(2\times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 5\times 10^8} = 0.088 \text{ fF/g}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Interfacing Electronics Chain:**

**Stage 1 — Charge-to-Voltage Converter:**
A switched-capacitor network converts the differential capacitance into voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{(C_1 - C_2)}{C_f}$$

where $C_f$ is the feedback capacitor (≈ 100 fF).

**Stage 2 — Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA):**
Gain selectable from 1× to 100× via digital control; uses chopper-stabilized op-amps for low offset (≈ < 5 µV).

**Stage 3 — Anti-Aliasing Filter:**
A 2nd-order active Butterworth LPF with cutoff $f_c \approx 1$ kHz for typical accelerometer applications.

**Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):**
A **16-bit Sigma-Delta ADC** is used for high resolution (effective > 18 bits ENOB).

**Stage 5 — Digital Compensation:**
Temperature compensation using internal PTAT sensor; offset and gain calibration coefficients stored in non-volatile memory.

**Stage 6 — Output Interface:**
I²C (400 kHz standard, 3.4 MHz high-speed) or SPI (10 MHz) output to host MCU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> For full marks in part (b), you MUST mention:
> 1. **Why switched-capacitor** and not continuous amplifier (offset cancellation).
> 2. **Chopper stabilization** for 1/f noise reduction.
> 3. **Sigma-Delta ADC** advantages for low-frequency, high-resolution.
> 4. **Temperature compensation** is non-negotiable in real designs.
> 5. Failure to draw a clean block diagram costs **2 marks minimum**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working of a MEMS gyroscope** based on the Coriolis effect. Derive the expression for Coriolis force and sensitivity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With necessary diagrams, describe the **design and operation of a MEMS capacitive microphone**, including interfacing using a charge pump.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**1. Principle — Coriolis Effect:**
A MEMS gyroscope contains a proof mass driven into **linear oscillation** along the **drive axis (x)**. When the chip rotates about the **z-axis** at angular rate $\Omega$, the moving mass experiences a **Coriolis force**:

$$\vec{F}_c = -2m\,(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$

This force is **perpendicular** to both the drive motion and the rotation axis, causing **secondary oscillation along the sense axis (y)**.

**2. Architecture:**
A typical MEMS gyroscope uses:
- **Comb-drive actuator** for primary oscillation.
- **Folded-beam suspensions** for elastic decoupling.
- **Differential sense capacitors** along the y-axis.

**3. Derivation of Secondary Displacement:**
With primary drive velocity $v_x = V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$:

$$F_c = 2m\,\Omega\,V_0\cos(\omega_d t)$$

This drives the sense mode modeled as a damped harmonic oscillator:

$$m\ddot{y} + b\dot{y} + k_y y = F_c$$

At resonance ($\omega_d = \omega_y = \sqrt{k_y/m}$), the amplitude is maximized:

$$|y|_{max} = \frac{F_c}{b\,\omega_y} = \frac{2m\,\Omega\,V_0}{b\,\omega_y}$$

**4. Sense Capacitor Change:**

$$\Delta C_y = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_y}{d_y^2}\cdot y$$

**5. Gyroscope Sensitivity:**

$$\boxed{S_g = \frac{\Delta C_y}{\Omega} = \frac{4\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_y\,m\,V_0}{d_y^2\,b\,\omega_y}}$$

**6. Typical Specs:**
- Full scale: ±2000 °/s
- Sensitivity: ~10 fF/(°/s)
- Noise floor: 0.007 °/s/√Hz

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**1. Construction of MEMS Microphone:**
- **Flexible diaphragm** (1 mm diameter, < 1 µm thick polysilicon).
- **Perforated back-plate** with acoustic holes (< 10 µm).
- **Air gap** ≈ 2–4 µm between diaphragm and back-plate.
- Variable capacitor formed by these two plates.

**2. Working Principle:**
Sound pressure causes the diaphragm to vibrate. Diaphragm displacement $x$ changes the air-gap capacitance:

$$C(t) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x(t)}$$

For small $x$:

$$C(t) \approx C_0\left(1 + \frac{x(t)}{d_0}\right)$$

**3. Sensitivity of Capacitive Microphone:**

$$S = \frac{\Delta C}{P_{ac}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2}\cdot \frac{3 a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$

**4. Interfacing — Charge Pump:**
A typical MEMS microphone (e.g., Knowles SPH0645) uses an **integrated preamplifier ASIC** that requires a high bias voltage (~11–16 V) to keep the diaphragm electrostatically biased. Since most devices operate at 1.8–3.3 V, a **CMOS charge pump** is integrated:

- 4-stage Dickson charge pump.
- Generates ~12 V bias from 3 V supply.
- Uses on-chip pump capacitors (10 pF each) and rectifying diodes.

**5. Output Stage:**
The ASIC includes a **low-noise charge amplifier** (bandwidth 20 Hz–20 kHz), an **anti-aliasing filter**, and a **Sigma-Delta ADC** producing a **PDM (Pulse Density Modulation)** output.

**6. Specs:**
- SNR: 64 dB
- Sensitivity: −26 dBFS/Pa
- Flat frequency response: 50 Hz – 15 kHz

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> For full marks in part (a), students commonly lose 2 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the orthogonal axis decoupling** between drive and sense modes.
> 2. **Failing to derive the secondary displacement** at resonance vs. off-resonance.
> 3. **Confusing Coriolis acceleration with centrifugal acceleration.** The Coriolis force is $2m\Omega v$ — NOT $m\Omega^2 r$.
>
> For part (b), losing 2 marks if:
> 1. No mention of **PDM or I²S digital output**.
> 2. **Charge pump topology** not explained — KTU specifically tests this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ✅ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **MEMS = Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems** — feature size 1–100 µm, batch-fabricated.
- [ ] **Three foundational domains:** Sensor, Actuator, Energy harvester.
- [ ] **Six transduction mechanisms:** Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Resonant, Tunneling, Thermal.
- [ ] **Accelerometer** uses $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = ma$ → sensitivity = $1/\omega_n^2$.
- [ ] **Gyroscope** uses **Coriolis force** $F_c = 2m\Omega v$ — orthogonal between drive and sense axes.
- [ ] **Pressure sensor** diaphragm deflection $w_0 = \dfrac{3Pa^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Et^3}$.
- [ ] **Electrostatic actuator force:** $F = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{d^2}$.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric strain:** $S = d_{ij} E$ — **NO DC measurement** capability.
- [ ] **MEMS microphone** uses **charge pump** (~12 V) for diaphragm bias — outputs **PDM**.
- [ ] **Interfacing ASIC** always includes: Charge amplifier → PGA → Anti-alias filter → Sigma-Delta ADC → Digital compensation.
- [ ] **Wheatstone bridge output:** $V_{out} \approx V_{ex}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$.
- [ ] **Charge amplifier output:** $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$.
- [ ] **Most popular MEMS** devices today: **6-axis IMU**, **barometer**, **microphone**, **micro-mirror** (DLP).
- [ ] **Materials used:** Silicon (Si), polysilicon, single-crystal silicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, PZT, AlN, Au, polymer.
- [ ] **Fabrication processes:** Bulk micromachining (KOH etching), Surface micromachining (sacrificial layers), LIGA, SOI.
- [ ] **Key companies:** STMicroelectronics, Bosch Sensortec, Analog Devices, Murata, Knowles, TDK-InvenSense.
- [ ] **Formula to NEVER forget:** Coriolis $F_c = 2m\Omega v$ (NOT $m\Omega^2 r$).

### ⚠️ Frequently Confused Concepts

| Confused Pair | Clarification |
|---|---|
| Coriolis vs Centrifugal | Coriolis needs **moving mass** ($2m\Omega v$); Centrifugal needs **rotation radius** ($m\Omega^2 r$). |
| Piezoresistive vs Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive = **resistance change** (DC ok); Piezoelectric = **voltage generation** (AC only). |
| Capacitive vs Electrostatic | Capacitive = **sensing**; Electrostatic = **actuation** — both use capacitor physics but different direction. |
| Surface vs Bulk Micromachining | Surface = sacrificial layer (thin films); Bulk = etch substrate (deep 3D structures). |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Electronics for MEMS Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interfacing Electronics for MEMS Devices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interfacing Electronics for MEMS Devices** refers to the dedicated analog and mixed-signal electronic circuitry that bridges the physical transduction domain of a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) sensor/actuator with the digital signal processing (DSP) or microcontroller (MCU) back-end. It is responsible for **excitation, signal extraction, conditioning, amplification, linearization, and analog-to-digital conversion** of the inherently low-level, high-impedance, and noise-susceptible signals produced by MEMS structures. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, this topic falls under the **"Sensor Signal Conditioning and Data Acquisition"** competency and is assessed against the Course Outcome **CO4**: *Design and analyze signal conditioning and interfacing circuits for MEMS-based sensors and actuators.* The interfacing block typically sits between the MEMS die and the digital processor: > **MEMS Element → Excitation Source → Sense Front-End → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP/MCU** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are trying to listen to a single person whispering in the middle of a crowded railway station. - The **whisper** is the MEMS signal (microvolts, picoamps, femtofarads of capacitance change). - The **crowd noise** is thermal noise, 1/f noise, supply rail noise, and electromagnetic interference (EMI). - Your **ears + brain** are the interfacing electronics + DSP — they must extract, amplify, filter, and interpret the whisper accurately. > [!NOTE] > A MEMS accelerometer die, for example, may produce a differential capacitance change of just **~50 fF** (femtofarads) under full-scale acceleration. Without a carefully designed charge amplifier or switched-capacitor readout, this signal is **invisible** to any microcontroller ADC. This is precisely why "interfacing electronics" is not an optional add-on — it is the *enabling* technology that makes the MEMS die useful. ## 1.3 Why Interfacing Electronics is a Critical Subsystem MEMS devices (accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors, microphones, micromirrors) inherently produce signals that are: | Signal Property | Typical Magnitude | Consequence | |---|---|---| | Voltage Output | µV to mV | Buried in noise floor | | Output Impedance | 10 kΩ – 1 GΩ | Vulnerable to loading | | Capacitance Change | fF to pF | Needs charge-mode amplification | | Bandwidth | DC to 10 kHz (typical) | Filters must be matched | | Susceptibility | High to EMI & crosstalk | Shielding & guarding needed | **Physical/Standard constants used in this module:** - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$ - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = k_B T / q \approx \mathbf{25.85 \; mV}$ - **Electron charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{C}$ - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Building Blocks of MEMS Interfacing ### Block 1: Excitation / Bias Generation - Provides a stable voltage (for piezoresistive) or AC carrier (for capacitive) or current (for piezoelectric) stimulus to the MEMS element. - Must be **low-noise, temperature-stable, and well-decoupled** to avoid injecting noise back into the readout. ### Block 2: Sense Front-End (Transduction) Converts the physical measurand into an electrical quantity: - **Piezoresistive bridge** → differential voltage - **Capacitive half/full bridge** → differential capacitance - **Piezoelectric** → charge - **Resonant** → frequency shift ### Block 3: Amplification Boosts the sub-mV signal to ADC-friendly levels (typically 0–3.3 V or 0–5 V). - **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** for differential piezoresistive - **Charge Amplifier** for piezoelectric MEMS - **Capacitance-to-Voltage Converter (CVC)** for capacitive MEMS - **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA)** for photodiodes / current-output MEMS ### Block 4: Filtering & Linearization - Removes out-of-band noise (anti-aliasing low-pass filter before ADC) - Compensates for sensor non-linearity (e.g., $V_{out} = K_1 x + K_3 x^3$ for accelerometers) - **Temperature compensation** networks (thermistor + look-up table) ### Block 5: Digitization - **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** — most common for low-power MEMS - **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) ADC** — for high-resolution (≥16-bit) applications like weighing scales, microphones - ADC resolution $N$ bits over full-scale $V_{FS}$: $\text{LSB} = V_{FS}/2^N$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for this module. Memorize the symbols, units, and typical magnitudes. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V/√Hz | Sets the noise floor | | 2 | Shot noise (current) | $i_n = \sqrt{2 q I_{DC} \Delta f}$ | A/√Hz | For photodiodes, leakage | | 3 | Capacitive MEMS capacitance | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F | Parallel-plate model | | 4 | Differential capacitance change | $\Delta C / C_0 = x / d_0$ | dimensionless | Small-signal linearization | | 5 | Charge on capacitive sensor | $Q = C \cdot V_{bias}$ | C | Bias must be ultra-stable | | 6 | CVC output (charge amp) | $V_{out} = - (C_x / C_f) \cdot V_{ref}$ | V | $C_f$ sets gain | | 7 | INA differential gain | $V_{out} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | V | $G = 1 + 2R_1/R_{gain}$ | | 8 | Wheatstone bridge output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$ (full bridge, 1 active) | V | Linear for small $\Delta R$ | | 9 | Full-bridge output (2 active) | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}$ | V | Doubles sensitivity | | 10 | Full-bridge output (4 active) | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V | Maximum sensitivity, best CMRR | | 11 | ADC SNR (ideal N-bit) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02 N + 1.76$ | dB | Assumes quantization noise only | | 12 | Anti-alias filter cutoff | $f_c = f_s / 2$ (Nyquist) | Hz | For 0.5× oversampling | | 13 | Resonant frequency (MEMS beam) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | Determines required BW | | 14 | Time constant of CVC | $\tau = R_f C_f$ | s | Sets high-frequency limit | | 15 | 1/f noise corner | $f_c = \sigma_n \cdot \mu \cdot (\dots)$ | Hz | Typically 10 Hz – 1 kHz in CMOS | > ⚠️ **PIPELINE WARNING:** In the table above, do not type the absolute-value bars `|x|` in math mode. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to keep the markdown table parser happy. ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Block - **Why excitation?** Many MEMS are *passive* — they do not generate power; they modulate a bias. Without excitation, no signal exists. - **Why differential?** Rejects common-mode noise (50 Hz mains, ground bounce) and increases dynamic range by 2×. - **Why charge amplifier for piezo?** Piezoelectric sources behave as charge sources, not voltage sources. A high-input-impedance charge amp converts $Q$ → $V$ without loading the source. - **Why anti-alias filter?** Prevents out-of-band noise from aliasing into the baseband after sampling (Shannon-Nyquist). - **Why Σ-Δ over SAR for microphones?** Σ-Δ oversamples and noise-shapes, achieving 100+ dB SNR needed for audio. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Automotive airbag accelerometers** — piezoresistive or capacitive, with ASIC integrating the charge amp + ADC on-chip - **Smartphone IMUs** (InvenSense, Bosch, STMicroelectronics) — full SoC with MEMS + ASIC + DSP - **MEMS microphones** — capacitive with charge preamp + Σ-Δ modulator - **MEMS pressure sensors (TPMS)** — piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge with INA - **Inkjet printheads** — thermal MEMS driven by high-current driver ICs - **Optical MEMS (DLP)** — high-voltage analog driver for micromirrors # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist) Across a MEMS Resistive Bridge The smallest detectable signal of any resistive MEMS sensor is set by the Johnson-Nyquist noise of its bridge resistance. **Step 1 — Identify the source.** A resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ has a noise voltage spectral density (one-sided): $$ S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R \quad [\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}] $$ **Step 2 — Integrate over the measurement bandwidth** $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$: $$ \overline{v_n^2} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} 4 k_B T R \; df = 4 k_B T R \Delta f $$ **Step 3 — Take the square root to get RMS noise voltage:** $$ v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical example (typical MEMS piezoresistive accelerometer).** Given: - $R = 5 \; \text{k}\Omega$ (single piezoresistor) - $T = 300 \; \text{K}$ - $\Delta f = 100 \; \text{Hz}$ (audio-bandwidth measurement) - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$ $$ v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 \cdot (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot 300 \cdot (5 \times 10^3) \cdot 100} $$ **Step 5 — Simplify the arithmetic inside the radical:** $$ 4 \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300 = 1.656 \times 10^{-20} $$ $$ 1.656 \times 10^{-20} \cdot 5 \times 10^{3} = 8.28 \times 10^{-17} $$ $$ 8.28 \times 10^{-17} \cdot 100 = 8.28 \times 10^{-15} $$ **Step 6 — Take the square root:** $$ v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{8.28 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{V} = 0.091 \; \mu\text{V} $$ **Step 7 — Engineer the bridge.** With 4 piezoresistors in a full Wheatstone bridge, the *common-mode* noise is rejected, but the *differential* noise adds as $\sqrt{2}$ factor. The minimum detectable strain is therefore: $$ \varepsilon_{\min} = \frac{v_{n,\text{rms}}}{V_{ex} \cdot G_f} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{Gauge Factor}} $$ This derivation is the foundation for **noise-limited resolution** of piezoresistive MEMS. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance-to-Voltage Conversion (CVC) for a MEMS Accelerometer Consider a differential capacitive accelerometer with two sense capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in a half-bridge configuration, biased by $V_{ref}$. **Step 1 — Define rest-state capacitance.** At rest, $C_1 = C_2 = C_0$. **Step 2 — Under acceleration $a$, proof mass displaces by $x$:** $$ C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x} \quad ; \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + x} $$ **Step 3 — Linearize for small $x \ll d_0$** using Taylor expansion $\frac{1}{1 \pm x/d_0} \approx 1 \mp x/d_0$: $$ C_1 \approx C_0 \left(1 + \frac{x}{d_0}\right) \quad ; \quad C_2 \approx C_0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{d_0}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Differential capacitance:** $$ \Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = 2 C_0 \frac{x}{d_0} $$ **Step 5 — Apply switched-capacitor (SC) charge readout.** The charge transferred per clock cycle: $$ \Delta Q = V_{ref} \cdot \Delta C = 2 V_{ref} C_0 \frac{x}{d_0} $$ **Step 6 — Integrate onto feedback capacitor $C_f$** in a correlated double-sampling (CDS) charge amplifier: $$ V_{out} = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_f} = \frac{2 V_{ref} C_0}{C_f \cdot d_0} \cdot x $$ **Step 7 — Convert displacement to acceleration** using the mechanical spring constant $k = m \omega_0^2$ and Newton's law $F = ma = kx$: $$ x = \frac{m a}{k} = \frac{a}{\omega_0^2} $$ **Step 8 — Substitute to get the final closed-form sensor output:** $$ V_{out}(a) = \underbrace{\left( \frac{2 V_{ref} C_0}{C_f \, d_0 \, \omega_0^2} \right)}_{\text{Scale Factor } S_f \; [\text{V/g}]} \cdot a $$ This is the **canonical sensitivity equation** for a capacitive MEMS accelerometer and appears frequently in KTU Part B questions. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge Amplifier for Piezoelectric MEMS **Step 1 — Piezoelectric model.** The MEMS source is modeled as a charge source $Q = d_{33} \cdot F \cdot A$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_p$ and source resistance $R_s$. **Step 2 — Standard inverting charge amplifier topology** has feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$ (to provide DC path). **Step 3 — KCL at inverting node (virtual ground):** All source charge flows into $C_f$: $$ Q = C_f \cdot V_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $Q = d_{33} F A$:** $$ V_{out} = -\frac{d_{33} \, F \, A}{C_f} $$ **Step 5 — Low-frequency cutoff (high-pass) is set by $R_f$:** $$ f_{-3\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_f C_f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For MEMS accelerometers with seismic masses in the µg to g range, $R_f$ is typically chosen as **10–100 GΩ** to give $f_{-3\text{dB}} < 0.1$ Hz. This is why charge amplifiers use **JFET or CMOS-input op-amps** — their input bias current must be < 1 pA to avoid saturating the $C_f$ integrator. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — INA CMRR for a Piezoresistive MEMS Bridge **Step 1 — A Wheatstone bridge with one active piezoresistor $\Delta R$** produces differential output: $$ V_{d} = V_+ - V_- = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R + \Delta R/2} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ **Step 2 — Common-mode voltage** at both outputs is $V_{ex}/2$. **Step 3 — Instrumentation amplifier with gain $G$ and CMRR of $\rho$ (linear):** $$ V_{out} = G \cdot \left( V_d + \frac{V_{cm}}{\rho} \right) $$ **Step 4 — Convert to dB form** to compute effective SNR degradation: $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \rho $$ **Step 5 — Numerical example.** If $V_{ex} = 5 \; \text{V}$, $V_{cm} = 2.5 \; \text{V}$, $\rho = 10^4$ (80 dB), $G = 100$, and $\Delta R/R = 0.001$: $$ V_{d,\text{amp}} = 100 \cdot \frac{5 \cdot 0.001}{4} = 0.125 \; \text{V} $$ $$ V_{cm,\text{leak}} = 100 \cdot \frac{2.5}{10^4} = 0.025 \; \text{V} $$ This 20% leakage shows why high CMRR (>100 dB) is essential for sub-mV MEMS signals. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full MEMS Interfacing Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical
Measurand] --> B[MEMS
Transducer] B --> C[Excitation
Source] B --> D[Sense
Front-End] D --> E[Charge or
Transimpedance
Amplifier] E --> F[Anti-Alias
LPF] F --> G[SAR or
Sigma-Delta
ADC] G --> H[DSP or
Microcontroller] H --> I[Output
Calibrated Data] J[Temperature
Sensor] --> H K[Voltage
Reference] --> C K --> G L[LDO
Regulator] -.powers.-> E L -.powers.-> G ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piezoresistive MEMS with INA ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EX[Excitation] VEX[Stable Vex
2.5 to 5 V] --> WB[Wheatstone
Bridge 4R + dR] end subgraph SF[Sense Front-End] WB --> INA[INA
Gain = 100 to 1000] INA --> LPF2[2nd-order
Sallen-Key LPF] end subgraph DIG[Digitization] LPF2 --> ADC16[16-bit
Sigma-Delta ADC] ADC16 --> MCU[ARM Cortex-M
MCU] end MCU --> CAL[Look-up Table
+ Polynomial Fit] CAL --> OUT[Engineering
Units Output] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capacitive MEMS with Switched-Capacitor CVC ```mermaid flowchart LR A[MEMS
Capacitor] --> B[Charge
Amplifier] C[Ref Voltage
Vref] --> B B --> D[Correlated
Double Sampler
CDS] D --> E[Gain Stage
PGA] E --> F[LPF
fc = BW] F --> G[ADC
16-bit] CLK[Master Clock] --> D CLK --> G G --> H[Digital
Filter] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Noise Sources Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB TN[Thermal Noise
4kT R df] --> NOISE[Total
Noise Floor] FN[1/f Noise
CMOS Op-Amp] --> NOISE SN[Shot Noise
2q I dc df] --> NOISE QN[Quantization
Noise ADC] --> NOISE EM[EMI / Crosstalk
50 Hz mains] --> NOISE PS[Power Supply
Ripple] --> NOISE NOISE --> SNR[Effective
SNR Budget] SNR --> RES[Sensor
Resolution] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop MEMS Actuator (e.g., Micromirror) ```mermaid flowchart LR REF[Reference
Position] --> SUM((Summing
Junction)) FB[Position
Sensor] --> SUM SUM --> ERR[Error
Signal] ERR --> PID[PID
Controller] PID --> HV[High-Voltage
Driver] HV --> ACT[MEMS
Actuator] ACT --> MEM[MEMS
Structure] MEM --> FB ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid compilation tip:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `INAA`, `PIDA`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. Labels are kept clean, uppercase-friendly text without `**` or `*` formatting inside quoted strings. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Define an instrumentation amplifier and state two reasons why it is preferred over a standard op-amp differential amplifier for interfacing piezoresistive MEMS bridges.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** An instrumentation amplifier (INA) is a precision differential amplifier with very high input impedance on both inputs, adjustable gain via a single external resistor, and extremely high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR > 100 dB). Architecturally, it consists of a two-op-amp input buffer stage feeding a difference amplifier. - **Reason 1 (1 mark):** **High input impedance on both inputs** prevents the loading of the high-impedance MEMS Wheatstone bridge, preserving bridge linearity. - **Reason 2 (1 mark):** **Single-resistor gain control** simplifies calibration, and the symmetric internal layout gives much higher CMRR (typ. 100 dB) than a single-op-amp difference amp (~40 dB) — critical for sub-mV MEMS signals riding on a 2.5 V common-mode voltage. --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **What is charge amplification, and why is it the preferred readout technique for piezoelectric MEMS devices?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **charge amplifier (1 mark)** is an op-amp circuit with a capacitive feedback element $C_f$ that converts the input charge $Q$ from a piezoelectric source into an output voltage $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$. - **Preferred for piezo MEMS (2 marks):** Piezoelectric sources are *charge* sources, not voltage sources. Their open-circuit output voltage depends inversely on the source capacitance, which is sensitive to temperature, cable capacitance, and shunt loading. A charge amplifier with a *virtual ground* at its input absorbs all generated charge into $C_f$ (independent of cable/source capacitance), giving a stable, low-impedance voltage output proportional to the applied force. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** confuse the *transimpedance amplifier (TIA)* (for current sources like photodiodes) with the *charge amplifier* (for capacitive/piezo sources). Examiners deduct 1 mark per instance. > - **Always state the *units* of every formula** in your derivation — half-mark penalties apply for bare equations. > - For Wheatstone bridge outputs, **specify the configuration** (1, 2, or 4 active elements) — writing a generic formula is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks. > - Do **not** skip the noise analysis. KTU 2024 marking schemes explicitly allocate 2–3 marks to Johnson-Nyquist / 1/f noise in any interfacing question. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`** > **a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the generic signal conditioning chain required to interface a capacitive MEMS accelerometer with a digital microcontroller. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)** > > **b)** A capacitive MEMS accelerometer has rest capacitance $C_0 = 2 \; \text{pF}$, gap $d_0 = 2 \; \mu\text{m}$, bias voltage $V_{ref} = 1.2 \; \text{V}$, and a feedback capacitor $C_f = 10 \; \text{pF}$ in its charge amplifier. The proof mass resonates at $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 1500 \; \text{rad/s}$ with mass $m = 1 \; \mu\text{g}$. > (i) Derive the expression for the output voltage as a function of applied acceleration $a$. **(4 marks)** > (ii) Calculate the scale factor in V/g. Take $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$. **(3 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block diagram description (7 marks) — key points to list:** 1. **MEMS accelerometer** — differential capacitor half-bridge (1 mark) 2. **Bias/excitation** — stable low-noise $V_{ref}$, often on-chip LDO (1 mark) 3. **Charge amplifier (CVC)** — converts $\Delta C$ to $V_{out}$ via $C_f$ (1 mark) 4. **Correlated Double Sampling (CDS)** — removes kTC noise and 1/f offset (1 mark) 5. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** — adjusts dynamic range (1 mark) 6. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** — Butterworth, 2nd/4th order (1 mark) 7. **16-bit Σ-Δ or SAR ADC** — digitizes for MCU (1 mark) **(b)(i) Derivation (4 marks):** From the derivation in Section 3.2: $$ V_{out}(a) = \frac{2 V_{ref} C_0}{C_f \, d_0 \, \omega_0^2} \cdot a $$ Substitute all symbols and explain each: $V_{ref}$ is the bias, $C_0$ rest capacitance, $C_f$ feedback capacitance, $d_0$ rest gap, $\omega_0^2 = k/m$ the mechanical stiffness-to-mass ratio. **(b)(ii) Numerical evaluation (3 marks):** Given: $C_0 = 2 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $V_{ref} = 1.2$ V, $C_f = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot 1500$ rad/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute $\omega_0^2$: $$ \omega_0^2 = (2\pi \cdot 1500)^2 = (9424.78)^2 \approx 8.886 \times 10^{7} \; \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2 $$ Compute the scale factor $S_f$ (output per unit acceleration): $$ S_f = \frac{2 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8.886 \times 10^{7}} $$ Numerator: $$ 2 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-12} = 4.8 \times 10^{-12} $$ Denominator: $$ 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-17} $$ $$ 2 \times 10^{-17} \cdot 8.886 \times 10^{7} = 1.7772 \times 10^{-9} $$ Scale factor per m/s²: $$ S_f = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-12}}{1.7772 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2.701 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V/(m/s}^2\text{)} $$ Convert to V/g: $$ S_f^{(g)} = 2.701 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 9.81 \approx 26.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V/g} = \mathbf{26.5 \; mV/g} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`** > **a)** Explain the operation of a Wheatstone bridge interface for a piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor. Derive the output voltage for a full-bridge configuration with all four resistors active. **(7 marks)** > > **b)** A piezoresistive pressure sensor has a bridge resistance of $R = 3.3 \; \text{k}\Omega$ per arm, gauge factor $GF = 120$, and is excited by $V_{ex} = 5 \; \text{V}$. The sensor is read out by an instrumentation amplifier with gain $G = 200$ and CMRR = 100 dB. The measurement bandwidth is 100 Hz at 300 K. > (i) Calculate the RMS thermal noise voltage of one arm. **(3 marks)** > (ii) If the maximum allowable strain is $\varepsilon = 500 \; \mu\varepsilon$, find the full-scale output voltage of the INA. **(2 marks)** > (iii) Compute the effective signal-to-noise ratio in dB. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Wheatstone bridge with 4 active piezoresistors (7 marks):** - **Operation (3 marks):** Four piezoresistors are placed on a MEMS diaphragm — two on the high-strain region (tensile), two on the complementary region (compressive). Under applied pressure, two resistors increase and two decrease by $\Delta R$, doubling the bridge sensitivity. - **Derivation (4 marks):** For a full bridge with $V_{ex}$ applied across the top and bottom: Top node voltage: $$ V_+ = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{(R - \Delta R) + (R + \Delta R)} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} $$ Bottom node voltage: $$ V_- = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R - \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \Delta R)} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} $$ **Correction (no common-mode variation under small $\Delta R$) — but the *differential* output is:** $$ V_{d} = V_+ - V_- = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ **For small $\Delta R$, the linearized form is:** $$ V_{d,\text{linear}} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ This is **4× the sensitivity** of a single-active-element bridge. **(b)(i) Thermal noise (3 marks):** $$ v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ $$ v_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300 \cdot 3.3 \times 10^3 \cdot 100} $$ $$ = \sqrt{5.469 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 7.39 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{V} = 73.9 \; \text{nV} $$ **(b)(ii) Full-scale INA output (2 marks):** Strain to $\Delta R$: $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6} = 0.06 $$ Bridge differential output: $$ V_d = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \cdot 0.06 = 0.3 \; \text{V} $$ INA output (ideal, ignoring CMRR leakage): $$ V_{out,\text{FS}} = G \cdot V_d = 200 \cdot 0.3 = 60 \; \text{V} $$ > ⚠️ **Practical note (1 of the 2 marks):** This exceeds typical ADC ranges; in practice, $G$ would be lowered to ~10 to keep $V_{out} \leq 3$ V. For exam purposes, state the assumption. **(b)(iii) SNR (2 marks):** Noise at INA output: $$ v_{n,\text{out}} = G \cdot v_{n,\text{rms}} \cdot \sqrt{4} = 200 \cdot 73.9 \; \text{nV} \cdot 2 = 29.56 \; \mu\text{V} $$ (Factor $\sqrt{4}$ because four arms contribute independently.) Signal-to-noise ratio: $$ \text{SNR} = \frac{V_{out,\text{FS}}}{v_{n,\text{out}}} = \frac{60}{29.56 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{6} $$ In dB: $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2.03 \times 10^{6}) \approx \mathbf{126.1 \; dB} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute revision — pin this to your wall.** - ☐ **Three canonical MEMS readout topologies** — piezoresistive (Wheatstone bridge), capacitive (charge amp / SC CVC), piezoelectric (charge amp). - ☐ **Wheatstone bridge scaling** — 1 active → $V_o = V_{ex} \Delta R / 4R$ ; 2 active → $\times 2$ ; 4 active → $\times 4$. - ☐ **Charge amplifier transfer function** — $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$ ; gain set by ONE capacitor, independent of source. - ☐ **CVC sensitivity equation** — $V_{out} = (2 V_{ref} C_0 / (C_f d_0 \omega_0^2)) \cdot a$ for capacitive accelerometers. - ☐ **Johnson-Nyquist noise** — $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — must be specified for *all* resistive MEMS readouts. - ☐ **1/f noise corner** — typically 10 Hz – 1 kHz in CMOS; mitigated by CDS / chopper stabilization. - ☐ **Anti-alias filter** — Butterworth, $f_c = f_s / 2$ (Nyquist); higher-order (4th–8th) for steep roll-off. - ☐ **ADC selection** — SAR for low-power wide-bandwidth; Σ-Δ for high-resolution audio/precision. - ☐ **Ideal ADC SNR** — $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB for an $N$-bit converter (quantization only). - ☐ **CMRR** — must exceed 100 dB for sub-mV MEMS signals; use a true INA, not a single-op-amp diff-amp. - ☐ **Temperature compensation** — onboard thermistor + polynomial fit or look-up table; mandatory for medical/automotive. - ☐ **Typical values to remember** — $k_B T = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J at 300 K; $V_T = 25.85$ mV; MEMS gap $d_0 \approx 1$–$5 \; \mu$m. - ☐ **Guarding & shielding** — drive guards at same potential as high-impedance node to eliminate leakage. - ☐ **High-pass corner for charge amp** — $f_{-3\text{dB}} = 1 / (2\pi R_f C_f)$; $R_f$ in GΩ for sub-Hz applications. - ☐ **Excitation stability** — LT1019 or ADR45xx family references (≤ 5 ppm/°C) are KTU-favorite examples. > **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INTERFACING ELECTRONICS FOR MEMS DEVICES**

topics.title: System-on-Chip Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System-on-Chip Integration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **System-on-Chip (SoC) Integration** is the process of consolidating all functional units of a complete electronic system — including digital processors, memory blocks, analog/mixed-signal interfaces, sensor front-ends, actuator drivers, communication peripherals, and power management circuits — onto a **single semiconductor die** fabricated using a unified CMOS (or CMOS-MEMS) process technology. In the specific context of **PECPT521 (Sensor and Actuator Integration)**, an SoC is an *integrated microsystem* that physically co-locates the **transducer (sensor/actuator)**, the **signal-conditioning circuitry (LNA, filters, ADC/DAC)**, the **embedded processor/DSP/controller**, the **communication interface (SPI, I²C, CAN, BLE)**, and the **power management unit (PMU/PMIC)** on the same silicon substrate, eliminating off-chip interconnects wherever possible. > [!NOTE] > **Key Sub-Classes Recognized in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - **MCU-SoC** – Microcontroller-based SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M cores) > - **MEMS-SoC** – Co-integrated micro-electromechanical transducers + CMOS > - **Mixed-Signal SoC (MS-SoC)** – Digital + analog + RF on one die > - **Bio-SoC / Lab-on-Chip** – Biomedical sensor SoC > - **IoT-SoC** – Ultra-low-power wireless sensor nodes > - **Automotive SoC** – Multi-core actuator-control SoCs (AUTOSAR-compliant) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **The "Smart City in a Single Building" Analogy:** > > Imagine a traditional electronic system as a *sprawling city* — the sensor lives in Suburb A, the ADC is in Industrial Zone B, the processor is in the Downtown Tower, and the wireless transmitter is on a hilltop far away. Moving signals between them wastes time (latency), power (long wires = parasitic capacitance), and money (PCB area, IC packages, connectors). > > A **System-on-Chip** is like *compressing that entire city into a single high-rise building*: every department (sensor, ADC, CPU, memory, RF, power) shares one foundation, one elevator (the on-chip bus), and one power grid. Information travels nanoseconds, not microseconds, and the system becomes smaller, faster, and dramatically more power-efficient. > > **Real-world example:** The Bosch **BMX055** is a 9-axis IMU SoC — accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer, 16-bit ADCs, and an I²C/SPI slave — all on a single 3 mm × 4.5 mm die. ### 1.3 Standard Physical & Design Metrics The following **standard metrics** govern every KTU SoC design problem: - **Process node (feature size)**: typically **180 nm, 130 nm, 90 nm, 65 nm, 40 nm, 28 nm, 22 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm** - **Supply voltage (V_DD)**: ranges from **0.6 V (near-threshold) to 3.3 V (legacy)** - **Die area**: expressed in **mm²** - **Clock frequency (f_clk)**: expressed in **MHz / GHz** - **Power density**: limited to roughly **100 W/cm²** (thermal-cooling ceiling) - **PPA figure of merit**: **Power – Performance – Area** trade-off - **Energy per operation**: in **pJ/op** or **fJ/op** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A *sensor-actuator SoC* is uniquely judged on three additional axes beyond classic PPA — **Sensor Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, **Actuator Drive Capability (current/voltage swing)**, and **Energy per Sensing-Actuation Cycle (E_cycle)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Canonical Sensor-Actuator SoC — Structural Block Hierarchy A modern sensor-actuator SoC is **heterogeneously integrated** around five core partitions. The hierarchy is presented in *descending* abstraction (system → block → sub-block): **① Sensor / Actuator Front-End (Transducer Layer)** - MEMS structures (accelerometer, gyroscope, pressure membrane) - Piezoelectric / piezoresistive / capacitive sense elements - Thermal or optical transducers - Actuator output stages: H-bridge, half-bridge, current drivers, PWM outputs **② Analog / Mixed-Signal Conditioning Layer** - **LNA** (Low-Noise Amplifier) — boosts μV-level transducer signals - **PGA** (Programmable Gain Amplifier) - **AAF** (Anti-Aliasing Filter) — typically 2nd–4th order Sallen-Key or OTA-C - **ADC** (Successive Approximation, Sigma-Delta, Pipeline, SAR) — 12 to 24 bit - **DAC** (for actuator reference) — 8 to 16 bit - **Vref / Bandgap reference** — temperature-stable **1.2 V** (typical) - **CP** (Charge Pump) for MEMS actuation **③ Digital Processing & Storage Layer** - CPU core (Cortex-M0/M3/M4, RISC-V, custom) - DSP / MAC unit (for FFT, Kalman filter) - SRAM (tens of kB to several MB) - ROM / Flash (firmware, calibration tables) - Cache (in higher-end SoCs) **④ Communication / Interconnect Layer** - Wired: **SPI, I²C, UART, CAN, LIN, USB, Ethernet** - Wireless: **BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, Wi-Fi, NFC** - On-chip bus: **AMBA APB, AHB, AXI** **⑤ Power Management Layer** - LDOs, DC-DC buck/boost converters - Power-gating switches - Sleep/wake controllers - Energy-harvesting interface (PV, thermoelectric, RF) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SoC for sensors/actuators?** Off-chip routing adds **parasitic capacitance (C_par)** in the pF range, which: > 1. Distorts high-impedance sensor signals (e.g., piezo, bio-potential) > 2. Increases ADC sampling-capacitor settling time > 3. Wastes dynamic power on charging/discharging long PCB traces > 4. Couples EMI/noise into low-amplitude sensor channels ### 2.2 The AMBA Bus Hierarchy (On-Chip Interconnect Backbone) The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is the de-facto industry standard for KTU-recognised SoC integration. | Bus | Full Name | Use-Case | Max Frequency | Bus Width | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, GPIO, timers) | ~50 MHz | 8/16/32 bit | | **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | High-speed peripherals (DMA, external mem) | ~150 MHz | 32/64/128 bit | | **AXI** | Advanced eXtensible Interface | Multi-core / high-throughput (DDR, GPU) | > 1 GHz | 64/128/256/512 bit | | **ACE** | AXI Coherency Extensions | Cache-coherent multi-core | > 1 GHz | 64+ bit | | **CHI** | Coherent Hub Interface | NoC (Network-on-Chip) many-core | > 2 GHz | 128+ bit | ### 2.3 Why and How — The Operational Logic Step-by-Step > The "Why" behind each step keeps the explanation accessible. 1. **Why integrate the sensor on-chip?** → Eliminates bond-wire/package parasitic capacitance, which for high-impedance sensors (MEMS, biomedical) can be **10–100 pF**, attenuating signals and injecting noise. Co-integration drops this to **sub-pF on-die parasitics**. 2. **Why use a dedicated ADC per channel?** → Multiplexed ADCs force fast settling; in low-power applications, **dedicated SAR ADCs** run at 1 kS/s drawing < 1 µA, always-on. 3. **Why a dedicated DSP/MAC?** → Sensor fusion (complementary filter, Madgwick, Mahony, EKF) requires ~50–200 MACs/sample. A general CPU consumes > 1 mW/MAC; a dedicated MAC consumes < 10 µW/MAC — a **100× saving**. 4. **Why power-gating?** → Static (leakage) power dominates in sub-100 nm nodes when idle. Power-gating unused blocks can reduce standby current from **µA → nA**. 5. **Why mixed-voltage domains?** → Analog blocks prefer **3.3 V** (high SNR, large dynamic range), digital prefers **0.8 V** (low power). On-chip **LDOs / level-shifters** bridge the domains. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 Sensor-Actuator SoC problems. Master them.** #### A. Power, Energy, and Thermal Limits $$P_{dyn} \;=\; \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$$ $$P_{stat} \;=\; V_{DD} \, I_{leak}$$ $$P_{total} \;=\; P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$$ $$E_{op} \;=\; C_{eff} \, V_{DD}^{\,2}$$ #### B. Performance and Latency $$T_{clk} \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$$ $$L_{pipeline} \;=\; N_{stages} \, T_{clk}$$ $$\text{Throughput} \;=\; \frac{1}{T_{clk}} \;\text{ops/s}$$ $$\text{Amdahl Speedup} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$ #### C. ADC Figures of Merit (Sensor-SoC Specific) $$\text{SNR}_{ideal} \;=\; 6.02\,N + 1.76 \;\text{dB}$$ $$\text{ENOB} \;=\; \frac{\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$$ $$\text{SFDR} \;=\; \frac{2^{N}}{3} \quad \text{(ideal, full-scale sine)}$$ #### D. Bus Transfer and Throughput $$T_{transfer} \;=\; \frac{N_{bytes}}{f_{bus} \cdot W_{bus}}$$ #### E. Sensor Signal Conditioning (Noise-Limited Resolution) $$V_{n,RMS} \;=\; \sqrt{4 k_B T R \,\Delta f}$$ $$\text{Res.} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} \, V_{n,RMS}}$$ #### F. Compact Master Table | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Unit | Key Constant / Note | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Dynamic power | $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | W | $\alpha$ = activity factor (0 to 1) | | Static (leakage) power | $P_{stat}$ | $V_{DD} I_{leak}$ | W | Dominates in < 65 nm idle | | Energy per operation | $E_{op}$ | $C_{eff} V_{DD}^2$ | J | Halving $V_{DD}$ cuts $E$ by 75% | | Pipeline latency | $L$ | $N_{stages} T_{clk}$ | s | Sum, not product | | Ideal ADC SNR | $\text{SNR}$ | $6.02 N + 1.76$ | dB | Every extra bit = +6.02 dB | | ENOB | $N_{eff}$ | $(\text{SNR} - 1.76)/6.02$ | bits | Includes all noise/distortion | | Bus transfer time | $T_{tr}$ | $N_{bytes} / (f_{bus} W_{bus})$ | s | $W_{bus}$ in bits/transfer | | Thermal noise (Johnson) | $V_{n}$ | $\sqrt{4 k_B T R \,\Delta f}$ | V/√Hz | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | | Clock period | $T_{clk}$ | $1 / f_{clk}$ | s | Fundamental timing unit | | Amdahl speedup | $S$ | $1 / [(1-p) + p/N]$ | — | $p$ = parallel fraction | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wearable health monitors (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** — SoCs integrate PPG sensor + ECG AFE + BLE radio. - **Automotive airbag ECUs** — Accelerometer + gyroscope SoC drives safety actuation in < 5 ms. - **Industrial IoT** — Vibration sensor SoC enables predictive maintenance on rotating machinery. - **Smart agriculture** — Multi-gas + soil-moisture SoC operates from a 1 cm² solar cell. - **Medical implants (pacemakers, cochlear)** — Ultra-low-power SoC runs on a coin cell for > 10 years. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Total Power Dissipation of a Sensor-Actuator SoC > **Goal:** Express total on-chip power in terms of process parameters, activity, and supply voltage, then specialise to an IoT sensor node. **Step 1 — Start with the fundamental capacitor-charging model.** Every CMOS gate charges and discharges its load capacitance $C_L$ once per clock transition. The energy drawn from the supply per charge-discharge cycle is $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$, of which exactly half is stored on the capacitor and half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor. $$E_{cycle} \;=\; C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2}$$ **Step 2 — Account for switching activity.** Not every gate toggles every clock cycle. Let $\alpha \in [0,1]$ denote the *activity factor* (average fraction of nodes that switch per cycle). $$E_{op} \;=\; \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2}$$ **Step 3 — Convert energy to power using clock frequency.** If the gate performs $f_{clk}$ operations per second, the average *dynamic* power is: $$P_{dyn} \;=\; E_{op} \cdot f_{clk} \;=\; \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$$ **Step 4 — Add the leakage (static) contribution.** In modern sub-100 nm CMOS, the sub-threshold leakage current $I_{leak}$ flows continuously and creates a static power floor: $$P_{stat} \;=\; V_{DD} \, I_{leak}$$ **Step 5 — Combine for total dissipation.** $$P_{total} \;=\; P_{dyn} + P_{stat} \;=\; \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk} \;+\; V_{DD} \, I_{leak}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** Halving $V_{DD}$ reduces $P_{dyn}$ by a factor of **4** (quadratic) — the central reason modern SoCs operate near the near-threshold regime (~0.5 V) for energy harvesting. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — IoT Sensor-SoC Power Budget **Given (typical industrial wireless sensor node SoC):** - Process node: 40 nm CMOS - $C_L = 50\,\text{fF}$ per gate, total switched capacitance $C_{tot} = 20\,\text{nF}$ per "active" processing burst - $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$ - $f_{clk} = 50\,\text{MHz}$ - Activity factor $\alpha = 0.10$ - Leakage $I_{leak} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$ - Burst duration $t_{active} = 5\,\text{ms}$ per sense-act cycle, cycle period $T_{cycle} = 1\,\text{s}$ **Step 1 — Compute $P_{dyn}$.** $$P_{dyn} \;=\; 0.10 \times 20\times 10^{-9} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 50\times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{dyn} \;=\; 0.10 \times 20\times 10^{-9} \times 5\times 10^{7} \;=\; 0.10 \times 1.0 \;=\; 0.10\,\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $P_{stat}$ (negligible in active state).** $$P_{stat} \;=\; 1.0 \times 5\times 10^{-6} \;=\; 5\,\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Active-mode power.** $$P_{active} \;=\; 100\,\text{mW} + 5\,\mu\text{W} \;\approx\; 100\,\text{mW}$$ **Step 4 — Average power with duty cycling.** Duty cycle $D = t_{active} / T_{cycle} = 5\,\text{ms} / 1\,\text{s} = 0.005$. Assume sleep-mode power $P_{sleep} = 10\,\mu\text{W}$. $$P_{avg} \;=\; D \cdot P_{active} + (1-D)\,P_{sleep}$$ $$P_{avg} \;=\; 0.005 \times 100\,\text{mW} + 0.995 \times 10\,\mu\text{W}$$ $$P_{avg} \;=\; 500\,\mu\text{W} + 9.95\,\mu\text{W} \;\approx\; 510\,\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Battery lifetime for a 240 mAh coin cell at 3 V (720 mWh).** $$T_{life} \;=\; \frac{720\,\text{mWh}}{0.510\,\text{mW}} \;\approx\; 1412\,\text{h} \;\approx\; 59\,\text{days}$$ This is the *engineering reality* KTU expects you to defend: duty cycling extends life by **> 1000×** over always-on operation. --- ### 3.3 Derivation II — Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) of an On-Chip ADC **Step 1 — Start from the ideal SNR formula for an N-bit ADC quantising a full-scale sine:** $$\text{SNR}_{ideal} \;=\; 6.02\,N + 1.76 \;\text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to express $N$ in terms of measured SNR.** Let $\text{SNR}_{meas}$ be the *measured* signal-to-noise ratio in dB. Real ADCs suffer from thermal noise, aperture jitter, integral non-linearity (INL), and differential non-linearity (DNL). These all reduce the effective resolution. $$N_{eff} \;=\; \frac{\text{SNR}_{meas} - 1.76}{6.02}$$ **Step 3 — Example calculation.** A 16-bit SAR ADC is specified, but the measured SNR is 86 dB. Find its effective resolution. $$N_{eff} \;=\; \frac{86 - 1.76}{6.02} \;=\; \frac{84.24}{6.02} \;\approx\; 14.0\,\text{bits}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The ADC behaves as a true 14-bit converter; the lower 2 bits are lost to noise. This drives the system-level decision: *is a 16-bit ADC necessary, or will a cheaper 14-bit part suffice?* > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *resolution* (number of bits) with *accuracy* (ENOB). Resolution is the digital code width; accuracy is what noise permits you to trust. --- ### 3.4 Derivation III — Throughput of an AXI Bus Transaction **Step 1 — Define parameters.** A burst transfer sends $N_{bytes}$ over a bus of width $W_{bus}$ bits, at clock $f_{bus}$. Each clock transfers $W_{bus}/8$ bytes. **Step 2 — Number of clock cycles required.** $$N_{cycles} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{8 \, N_{bytes}}{W_{bus}} \right\rceil$$ **Step 3 — Total transfer time.** $$T_{transfer} \;=\; \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{bus}} \;=\; \frac{8\,N_{bytes}}{f_{bus}\,W_{bus}}$$ **Step 4 — Example.** Transfer a 1 KB image (1024 bytes) over an AXI bus with $W_{bus} = 64$ bit, $f_{bus} = 100$ MHz. $$T_{transfer} \;=\; \frac{8 \times 1024}{100\times 10^{6} \times 64} \;=\; \frac{8192}{6.4\times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 1.28\,\mu\text{s}$$ This is a critical design-time check for camera-pipeline or DSP-data SoCs. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Design Question | Governing Equation | Typical Numerical Range | KTU Use Case | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Will the SoC fit in the thermal envelope? | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | 1 mW – 5 W | Power-budget problem | | How many bits of true ADC resolution? | $N_{eff} = (\text{SNR}-1.76)/6.02$ | 10–20 bits | Mixed-signal SoC | | How long does a memory copy take? | $T_{tr} = 8N_{bytes}/(f_{bus}W_{bus})$ | 0.1 – 100 µs | AXI/AHB throughput | | How long can the SoC run on a coin cell? | $T_{life} = C_{bat} V_{bat} / P_{avg}$ | days – years | IoT energy budget | | What is the pipeline worst-case latency? | $L = N_{stages} T_{clk}$ | 5 – 50 ns | Real-time control | | What is the maximum parallel speedup? | $S = 1/[(1-p) + p/N]$ | 1 – N | Multi-core SoC | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Figure 1 — High-Level Functional Architecture of a Sensor-Actuator SoC > The Mermaid block diagram below maps all functional interactions, including the analog–digital domain boundary, the AMBA bus, and the energy-harvesting power tree. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSORS["SENSOR FRONT END"] sensor1["MEMS Accel"] sensor2["MEMS Gyro"] sensor3["Temp Sensor"] sensor4["Bio AFE"] end subgraph AFE["ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONING"] lna["LNA / Charge Amp"] pga["PGA"] aaf["Anti Alias Filter"] adc1["16 bit SAR ADC"] adc2["24 bit Sigma Delta ADC"] vref["Bandgap Vref 1.2V"] end subgraph ACTUATORS["ACTUATOR DRIVE STAGE"] pwm1["PWM Generator"] hb["H Bridge Driver"] dac1["12 bit DAC"] cpump["Charge Pump"] end subgraph DIGITAL["DIGITAL DOMAIN"] cpu["Cortex M4 CPU"] dsp["DSP / MAC Unit"] sram["SRAM 256KB"] flash["Flash 1MB"] cache["Cache 8KB"] pmu["PMU / Sleep Ctrl"] end subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION PERIPHERALS"] spi["SPI Master"] i2c["I2C Slave"] uart["UART"] can["CAN Bus"] ble["BLE 5.0 Radio"] end subgraph BUS["ON CHIP AMBA BUS"] apb["APB Low Speed"] ahb["AHB High Speed"] axi["AXI / DMA"] end subgraph POWER["POWER MANAGEMENT"] ldo1["LDO 3.3V Analog"] ldo2["LDO 1.0V Digital"] dcdc["Buck DC DC"] harvest["Energy Harvester"] batt["Li Ion 3.7V"] end sensor1 --> lna sensor2 --> lna sensor3 --> aaf sensor4 --> aaf lna --> pga --> aaf --> adc1 aaf --> adc2 vref --> adc1 vref --> adc2 vref --> dac1 adc1 --> apb adc2 --> apb cpu <--> ahb dsp <--> ahb sram <--> ahb flash <--> ahb cache <--> cpu apb <--> ahb ahb <--> axi axi --> cpu cpu --> pwm1 --> hb cpu --> dac1 --> hb cpump --> hb cpu --> pmu spi --> apb i2c --> apb uart --> apb can --> ahb ble --> axi batt --> dcdc dcdc --> ldo1 dcdc --> ldo2 harvest --> dcdc ldo1 --> AFE ldo2 --> DIGITAL pmu --> dcdc ``` ### 4.2 Figure 2 — Sensor-Actuator SoC Design Flow (Top-Down Methodology) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["System Spec
SNR Power Area Cost"] --> B["Architecture Exploration
CPU choice IP selection"] B --> C["RTL Design
VHDL / Verilog / SystemVerilog"] C --> D["Functional Verification
UVM / Cocotb"] D --> E{"Verification Pass?"} E -- No --> C E -- Yes --> F["Logic Synthesis
to Standard Cells"] F --> G["Place and Route
PnR"] G --> H["Static Timing
Analysis"] H --> I{"Timing Met?"} I -- No --> F I -- Yes --> J["Mixed Signal Layout
Analog + Digital"] J --> K["DRC LVS ERC"] K --> L{"DRC Clean?"} L -- No --> J L -- Yes --> M["Tape Out
to Foundry"] M --> N["Packaging
QFN BGA WLCSP"] N --> O["Board Integration
and Validation"] O --> P["Production"] ``` ### 4.3 Figure 3 — Power-Domain Partitioning of a Mixed-Signal SoC ```mermaid flowchart TB batt["Battery 3.7V"] --> pmic["PMIC / DC DC"] pmic --> avdd["AVDD 3.3V Analog"] pmic --> dvdd["DVDD 1.0V Digital"] pmic --> iovdd["IOVDD 1.8V IO"] avdd --> analogBlock["Analog + MEMS"] dvdd --> digitalBlock["CPU + DSP + SRAM"] iovdd --> ioBlock["SPI I2C GPIO"] analogBlock --> iso["Level Shifters"] iso --> digitalBlock digitalBlock --> ioBlock ``` > [!NOTE] > **Three independent voltage domains** prevent digital switching noise from corrupting low-level analog sensor signals. The level-shifter islands (ISO) protect the noise-sensitive AVDD rail from the high-transient DVDD rail. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a System-on-Chip (SoC). List any four major functional blocks of a typical sensor-actuator SoC.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A System-on-Chip (SoC) is an integrated circuit that consolidates all the functional units of a complete electronic system — including the processor, memory, analog/mixed-signal interfaces, sensor/actuator front-ends, communication peripherals, and power management — onto a single semiconductor die. > > **Four major functional blocks (2 marks — ½ mark each):** > 1. **Sensor/Actuator Front-End** – MEMS transducer, actuator driver > 2. **Analog Signal Conditioning** – LNA, PGA, ADC, DAC, bandgap reference > 3. **Digital Processing Core** – CPU, DSP, SRAM, Flash, cache > 4. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** – LDOs, DC-DC converters, power-gating --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is on-chip integration of the sensor with its signal-conditioning circuitry preferred over a discrete (off-chip) implementation? Give two reasons.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Reason 1 (1.5 marks):** *Elimination of parasitic capacitance.* Off-chip interconnects introduce bond-wire and PCB-trace capacitances in the range of **1–100 pF**, which attenuate high-impedance sensor signals (MEMS, bio-potential) and increase ADC sampling-capacitor settling time. On-chip integration reduces this to sub-pF levels. > > **Reason 2 (1.5 marks):** *Improved noise immunity and EMI rejection.* Long PCB traces act as antennas, coupling in environmental EMI. The short, shielded on-chip signal path between sensor and AFE drastically improves the SNR, which is critical for low-amplitude signals (e.g., ECG, seismic). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Module question carries **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. The student attempts **ONE** of the two choices. --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a sensor-actuator System-on-Chip (SoC). Identify and describe the function of any **five** major blocks. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** A sensor-actuator SoC is a single-die microsystem that physically co-locates transducers, analog front-ends, digital processing, communication, and power management. It replaces the traditional multi-chip PCB approach with one integrated circuit, yielding **smaller area, lower power, higher noise immunity, and lower BOM cost**. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** The student should draw a 5-block functional diagram showing: Sensor → AFE → ADC → Digital Core (CPU/DSP/Memory) → Actuator Driver, with a communication peripheral branch (SPI/I²C/BLE) and a power management block feeding all other blocks. **Five Major Blocks — Description (4 marks, 0.8 each):** 1. **Sensor / Actuator Front-End** – Transduces physical phenomena (acceleration, pressure, temperature, light) into electrical signals, or converts electrical signals into physical action (motor, valve, LED). Examples: MEMS accelerometer, piezoelectric actuator. 2. **Analog Signal Conditioning** – Amplifies (LNA/PGA), filters (AAF), and digitises (ADC) the analog transducer signal. Also generates the analog reference (bandgap) and provides the DAC for actuator drive. 3. **Digital Core** – Executes the firmware: filtering, sensor fusion (Kalman), control loops (PID). Includes CPU (Cortex-M), DSP, SRAM, and Flash. 4. **Communication Peripherals** – SPI, I²C, UART, CAN, BLE — exchange data with external MCUs, hosts, or wireless networks. 5. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** – Generates and regulates multiple on-chip voltage rails (e.g., 3.3 V analog, 1.0 V digital) from a battery or harvested source. Implements power-gating, sleep, and wake-up to maximise energy efficiency. --- **(b)** A 12-bit SAR ADC is integrated on a sensor SoC. The measured SNR is **70 dB**. Compute: (i) The **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)**. (ii) The **ideal SNR** of the same 12-bit ADC. (iii) Comment on why the measured value is lower than the ideal. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $N = 12$ bits (nominal), $\text{SNR}_{meas} = 70$ dB. **(i) ENOB (3 marks):** $$N_{eff} \;=\; \frac{\text{SNR}_{meas} - 1.76}{6.02} \;=\; \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} \;=\; \frac{68.24}{6.02} \;\approx\; 11.34\,\text{bits}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{N_{eff} \approx 11.3\,\text{bits}}$. **(ii) Ideal SNR (2 marks):** $$\text{SNR}_{ideal} \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \;=\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;=\; 74.00\,\text{dB}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{\text{SNR}_{ideal} = 74\,\text{dB}}$. **(iii) Comment on the loss of 4 dB (2 marks):** The measured SNR is **4 dB below** the theoretical ideal. Possible causes: - **Thermal (Johnson) noise** in the ADC's input switch and reference. - **Aperture jitter** in the sampling clock — the most critical noise source in high-frequency SAR ADCs. - **Integral Non-Linearity (INL)** and **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)** of the capacitor DAC. - **Power-supply noise** coupling from the digital domain to the analog domain. - **Quantisation noise** floor; practical ADC SNRs rarely hit the ideal. > The loss of 0.7 effective bits confirms that the SoC designer may safely replace this 12-bit ADC with a cheaper 12-bit part (no benefit) or accept a 10-bit ADC (saves area/power) depending on system SNR requirements. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **AMBA bus architecture** used in modern SoCs. Compare **APB, AHB, and AXI** buses in terms of complexity, performance, and use-case. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction to AMBA (1 mark):** AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture) is the industry-standard on-chip bus protocol family originally developed by ARM, now used in nearly all 32/64-bit SoCs. It defines a hierarchy of buses optimised for different bandwidth/latency needs. **Three Bus Tiers (4 marks — ~1.3 each):** | Feature | APB | AHB | AXI | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Complexity** | Simple, no pipelining | Moderate, pipelined bursts | High, out-of-order, multiple outstanding | | **Max Frequency** | ~50 MHz | ~150 MHz | > 1 GHz | | **Data Width** | 8/16/32 bit | 32/64/128 bit | 64/128/256/512 bit | | **Use-case** | Low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, GPIO, timers) | High-speed peripherals (DMA, external memory) | Multi-core, DDR, GPU, high-throughput | | **Pipelining** | None | Single-clock pipeline | Multi-channel (AW/AR/W/B/R), out-of-order completion | | **Master / Slave** | 1 master | Multiple masters | Multiple masters, out-of-order responses | **Comparison Commentary (1.5 marks):** - APB trades performance for low power and small area — ideal for slow sensor peripherals. - AHB offers a balanced high-speed bus for SoCs with one CPU and DMA. - AXI is mandatory for multi-core and high-throughput SoCs (e.g., mobile application processors, automotive ADAS). It decouples address/control and data channels, supporting deeply pipelined transactions. **Sensor-SoC Relevance (0.5 mark):** In a typical sensor-actuator SoC, APB connects the SPI/I²C/UART and ADC registers, while AHB or AXI connects the CPU to SRAM/DMA and BLE. --- **(b)** A sensor-SoC has a total switched capacitance of **$C_L = 25\,\text{nF}$**, supply voltage **$V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$**, clock frequency **$f_{clk} = 80\,\text{MHz}$**, activity factor **$\alpha = 0.15$**, and leakage current **$I_{leak} = 8\,\mu\text{A}$**. The SoC operates in active mode for **$t_{on} = 10\,\text{ms}$** out of every **$T = 1\,\text{s}$** cycle, and in sleep mode draws **$P_{sleep} = 15\,\mu\text{W}$**. The SoC is powered by a **3.7 V, 1000 mAh** Li-ion battery. Compute: (i) The **dynamic power** $P_{dyn}$. (ii) The **static power** $P_{stat}$. (iii) The **average power** $P_{avg}$ and the **battery lifetime in days**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $C_L = 25\,\text{nF} = 25\times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, $f_{clk} = 80\,\text{MHz} = 80\times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$, $\alpha = 0.15$, $I_{leak} = 8\,\mu\text{A} = 8\times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}$, $t_{on} = 10\,\text{ms}$, $T = 1\,\text{s}$, $P_{sleep} = 15\,\mu\text{W}$, Battery: $3.7\,\text{V} \times 1000\,\text{mAh} = 3700\,\text{mWh} = 3.7\,\text{Wh}$. **(i) Dynamic Power (2 marks):** $$P_{dyn} \;=\; \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$$ $$P_{dyn} \;=\; 0.15 \times 25\times 10^{-9} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 80\times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{dyn} \;=\; 0.15 \times 25\times 10^{-9} \times 1.44 \times 80\times 10^{6}$$ Computing step-by-step: $$25\times 10^{-9} \times 1.44 \;=\; 36\times 10^{-9}$$ $$36\times 10^{-9} \times 80\times 10^{6} \;=\; 2880\times 10^{-3} \;=\; 2.88\,\text{W}$$ $$P_{dyn} \;=\; 0.15 \times 2.88 \;=\; 0.432\,\text{W} \;=\; 432\,\text{mW}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{P_{dyn} = 432\,\text{mW}}$. **(ii) Static Power (1.5 marks):** $$P_{stat} \;=\; V_{DD} \times I_{leak} \;=\; 1.2 \times 8\times 10^{-6} \;=\; 9.6\,\mu\text{W}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{P_{stat} = 9.6\,\mu\text{W}}$. **Active-mode power (negligible addition from static):** $$P_{active} \;\approx\; 432\,\text{mW} + 0.0096\,\text{mW} \;\approx\; 432.01\,\text{mW}$$ **(iii) Average Power & Lifetime (3.5 marks):** Duty cycle $D = t_{on}/T = 10\,\text{ms}/1\,\text{s} = 0.01$. $$P_{avg} \;=\; D \cdot P_{active} + (1-D) \cdot P_{sleep}$$ $$P_{avg} \;=\; 0.01 \times 432.01\,\text{mW} + 0.99 \times 0.015\,\text{mW}$$ $$P_{avg} \;=\; 4.3201\,\text{mW} + 0.01485\,\text{mW} \;\approx\; 4.335\,\text{mW}$$ **Battery lifetime:** $$T_{life} \;=\; \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} \;=\; \frac{3.7\,\text{Wh}}{4.335\times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}} \;\approx\; 853.5\,\text{h}$$ Converting to days: $$T_{life} \;=\; \frac{853.5}{24} \;\approx\; 35.6\,\text{days}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 4.34\,\text{mW}, \quad T_{life} \approx 35.6\,\text{days}}$. > **Engineering Insight:** The active power of 432 mW is high, but the **1% duty cycle** reduces average dissipation by 100×. For a *truly* low-power IoT SoC, the duty cycle must drop to **< 0.1%** (e.g., 1 ms active per second) to achieve year-long battery life. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Mixing up SNR and ENOB formulas** — Remember: $6.02N + 1.76$ is for **ideal**; the formula with measured SNR gives **ENOB**. > 2. **Forgetting units** in power/energy problems. Always express capacitance in **farads**, time in **seconds**, voltage in **volts** before substituting. > 3. **Confusing active vs average power** — The SoC is *almost never* in active mode continuously. Always apply the duty cycle. > 4. **Ignoring static power** in sub-65 nm nodes — leakage is non-negligible during long sleep intervals. > 5. **Writing "SoC = microcontroller"** — An SoC *includes* a microcontroller but **also** integrates sensors/actuators, ADC/DAC, PMU, and peripherals. The integration is the differentiator. > 6. **Forgetting the $1.76$ dB term** in SNR calculations — this is the difference between sine-wave and quantisation-noise assumptions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE** - ✅ **SoC definition** = single-die integration of CPU + memory + peripherals + analog + power. - ✅ **Five core partitions**: Sensor/Actuator → AFE → Digital Core → Communication → PMU. - ✅ **AMBA hierarchy**: APB (slow) < AHB (mid) < AXI (fast) — know frequencies, widths, use-cases. - ✅ **Dynamic power formula**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — quadratic in $V_{DD}$. - ✅ **Static power formula**: $P_{stat} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ — dominant at idle in sub-65 nm. - ✅ **Energy per operation**: $E_{op} = C_{eff} V_{DD}^2$ — halving voltage saves 75% energy. - ✅ **ADC ideal SNR**: $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76\,\text{dB}$ — every bit adds ~6 dB. - ✅ **ENOB formula**: $N_{eff} = (\text{SNR}_{meas} - 1.76)/6.02$ — real-world resolution. - ✅ **Pipeline latency**: $L = N_{stages} \times T_{clk}$ — additive, not multiplicative. - ✅ **Bus transfer time**: $T_{tr} = 8 N_{bytes}/(f_{bus} W_{bus})$ — critical for image/streaming SoCs. - ✅ **Thermal noise (Johnson)**: $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \,\Delta f}$ — sets the noise floor of every analog block. - ✅ **Duty cycling** reduces average power by orders of magnitude — the #1 IoT-SoC technique. - ✅ **Mixed-signal SoC** uses **multiple voltage domains** (3.3 V analog, 1.0 V digital) with level shifters. - ✅ **AMBA AXI** supports out-of-order, multi-channel (AW/AR/W/B/R), pipelined transactions — required for multi-core. - ✅ **PPA trade-off** (Power, Performance, Area) is the central design constraint; every KTU SoC problem touches at least two of these. - ✅ **MEMS-SoC, IoT-SoC, Bio-SoC, Automotive SoC** are the four application classes recognised in the KTU PECPT521 syllabus. - ✅ **Always state units** in derivations: W, mW, µW, nW, J, pJ, fF, pF, mm², bits, dB. - ✅ **Mention duty cycle** whenever the problem gives both active and sleep times.

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT521 - SENSOR AND ACTUATOR INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PECPT521 – Sensor and Actuator Integration (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Applications"** under Module 4 refers to the systematic study, design, and evaluation of integrated sensor-actuator systems deployed across real-world engineering domains. It encompasses the architecture, signal-flow pipeline, feedback topology, and physical-layer interfacing required to convert physical phenomena into actionable mechanical, electrical, thermal, or optical responses via closed-loop or open-loop mechatronic subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 – Applications):**
> *Application-level study of sensor-actuator integrated modules in industrial automation, robotics, automotive systems, biomedical instrumentation, smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0), process control, environmental monitoring, and IoT-enabled cyber-physical systems, with emphasis on transducer selection, signal conditioning, control-loop design, and performance benchmarking.*

The unifying academic thread is the **sense → condition → compute → actuate → feedback** pipeline, which is the backbone of every modern mechatronic product.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Body as an Integrated Sensor-Actuator System"**

Imagine a person touching a hot frying pan:

1. **Sensor** → Nerve endings in the skin (thermoreceptors + nociceptors) detect temperature & pain.
2. **Signal Conditioning** → Spinal cord amplifies and filters the neural spike train.
3. **Compute / Controller** → The brain interprets "HOT! DANGER!".
4. **Actuator** → Muscles in the arm and hand contract, pulling the hand away.
5. **Feedback** → Vision (eyes) confirms the hand is clear, temperature sensors confirm cooling.

In this analogy:
- **Skin** = MEMS temperature/pressure sensor
- **Spinal Cord** = ADC + amplifier circuit
- **Brain** = Microcontroller / PLC / DSP
- **Muscle** = DC motor / piezoelectric actuator / solenoid
- **Eyes** = Vision/camera sensor feeding back to controller

A car’s **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** is an industrial analog: wheel-speed sensors detect lock-up → ECU computes required brake pressure → hydraulic actuators modulate brake-line pressure → feedback confirms wheel slip ratio is in safe range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:**
> Every "Application" question in Module 4 is fundamentally asking you to **trace the closed-loop pipeline** for a given engineering system and justify *why* a particular sensor-actuator pair was chosen over alternatives, citing metrics such as **bandwidth, accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, response time, and power consumption.**

---

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics for Integrated Systems

The following **bold metrics** must be memorized verbatim for any application question:

- **Accuracy:** $\pm$FS (Full-Scale) percentage deviation
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable input change
- **Sensitivity:** $\frac{\partial \text{Output}}{\partial \text{Input}}$ (V/unit or A/unit)
- **Bandwidth (BW):** Frequency range over which output remains within $\pm 3\,\text{dB}$
- **Response Time ($\tau$):** Time to reach 63.2% of final value (1st-order system)
- **Hysteresis:** Maximum output difference for the same input approached from opposite directions
- **Linearity:** Maximum deviation from best-fit straight line, expressed as **%FS**

---

### 1.4 Classification of Applications (Module 4 Roadmap)

| Domain | Primary Sensors | Primary Actuators | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial Automation | Proximity, pressure, flow, vision | Pneumatic cylinders, servo motors | High |
| Robotics | Encoders, IMU, force/torque | BLDC motors, harmonic drives | High |
| Automotive | MAP, MAF, oxygen, wheel-speed | Fuel injector, ABS valve, stepper | Very High |
| Biomedical | ECG, EEG, SpO₂, biosensors | Piezo pump, micro-needle, artificial muscle | Medium |
| Aerospace | Strain gauge, gyroscope, GPS | Hydraulic, piezoelectric, thruster | Medium |
| IoT / Industry 4.0 | Multi-modal smart sensors | Smart relays, edge actuators | High |
| Environmental | Gas, humidity, pH | Ventilation fan, valve | Low–Medium |
| Process Control | Level, flow, temperature | Control valve, variable-speed pump | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Integrated System Architecture

Every application, regardless of domain, conforms to a **5-block functional chain**:

1. **Sensing Stage (S):** Transduces physical quantity → electrical signal
2. **Signal Conditioning (SC):** Amplification, filtering, linearization, cold-junction compensation
3. **Controller / Compute (C):** Microcontroller, PLC, FPGA, DSP, edge-AI processor
4. **Actuation Stage (A):** Power amplification → mechanical/thermal/fluidic output
5. **Feedback Path (FB):** Closed-loop verification of desired state

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Phrase:** *"SENSE → CONDITION → COMPUTE → ACTUATE → FEEDBACK"*

---

### 2.2 Core Mathematical Modeling of Integrated Systems

#### 2.2.1 First-Order Sensor Model

A vast majority of integrated sensors (RTD, thermistor, capacitive humidity, LVDT) follow a first-order differential equation:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where:
- $\tau$ = **time constant** (seconds)
- $K$ = **static sensitivity** (V/unit)
- $x(t)$ = physical input
- $y(t)$ = electrical output

**Steady-state response** ($t \to \infty$): $y_{ss} = K \cdot x$

**Step response:** $y(t) = K \cdot x \left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$

---

#### 2.2.2 Second-Order Actuator Model

DC motors, voice-coil actuators, and piezoelectric stacks are second-order:

$$J\frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}} + B\frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + K_s\theta(t) = T(t)$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia ($\text{kg·m}^2$)
- $B$ = viscous damping coefficient ($\text{N·m·s/rad}$)
- $K_s$ = spring/torsional stiffness ($\text{N·m/rad}$)
- $T(t)$ = applied torque

**Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{J}}$, **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{J K_s}}$

---

#### 2.2.3 Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Universal)

For a unity-feedback application:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\,G(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G(s)\,H(s)}$$

where $G_c(s)$ = controller, $G(s)$ = plant (actuator + process), $H(s)$ = sensor feedback gain.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Formula | Domain / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(t) = Kx\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | First-order sensor step response | V, mA |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s / J}$ | Second-order actuator resonance | rad/s |
| 3 | $\zeta = B / \left(2\sqrt{J K_s}\right)$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G}{1 + G_c G H}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 5 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_a}$ | Steady-state error (unity FB) | input units |
| 6 | $R_{RTD}(T) = R_0\left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$ | PT100 temperature sensor | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $V_{oc} = K_s\,\omega$ | Tachogenerator (angular vel.) | V |
| 8 | $F = ma = m\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$ | Actuator force balance | N |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} C_p$ | Wind turbine energy harvesting | W |
| 10 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | MEMS accelerometer resonance | Hz |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Actuator efficiency | % |
| 12 | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure (level) | Pa |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in equations above are wrapped using $\vert$ where required (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), so as not to break markdown tables.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** PLC-based SCADA systems integrating photoelectric sensors + servo valves for high-speed packaging lines (≥ 500 products/min).
- **Robotics:** 6-DOF robotic arms using IMU + encoder fusion (sensor) with BLDC servo + harmonic drive (actuator), achieving ±0.02 mm repeatability.
- **Automotive ABS:** Wheel-speed sensor (Hall effect) → ECU → hydraulic modulator valve → brake caliper, sampling at **≥ 100 Hz** for safety-critical performance.
- **Biomedical:** Closed-loop insulin pump using subcutaneous glucose sensor (Enzyme Electrode) + piezoelectric micro-pump, dosing every 5 minutes.
- **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire systems with triple-redundant LVDT + RVDT position sensors and electro-hydraulic actuators providing 40 ms command-to-motion latency.
